item.title: White House official: Trump has said a US-Cuba deal could be done easily
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - Donald Trump has said the U.S. is talking to Cuba, its leaders should ÔÇőagree to a deal and that could easily be done, ÔÇîa White House official said on Friday, reiterating the U.S. president's previous remarks after Havana confirmed that negotiations were under way.
Cuban President Miguel Diaz-Canel ÔÇősaid earlier that his government had opened talks with ÔÇőWashington, as an oil blockade imposed by Trump pushes Ôüáthe Communist-run nation deeper into economic crisis.
"As the President stated, ÔÇőwe are talking to Cuba, whose leaders should make a deal, ÔÇőwhich he believes 'would be very easily made'," the White House official said, on condition of anonymity.
Diaz-Canel said in a video aired on state television ÔÇőthat negotiations "have been aimed at finding solutions through dialogue to ÔÇőthe bilateral differences we have between the two nations."
Since the U.S. captured ÔÇîVenezuelan ÔüáPresident Nicolas Maduro in January, removing Cuba's most important foreign benefactor, Trump has cut off Venezuelan oil shipments to Cuba and threatened to slap tariffs on any country that sells oil to ÔÇőCuba.
On Monday, ÔÇőTrump said Cuba Ôüámay be subject to a "friendly takeover," then added, "it may not be a friendly takeover."
The Caribbean ÔÇőnation's citizens, exhausted by years of economic crisis ÔÇőand Ôüáshortages, now live the majority of their days without electricity, strictly rationed fuel and shortages of medicine.
"Cuba is a failing nation whose Ôüárulers have ÔÇőhad a major setback with the ÔÇőloss of support from Venezuela and with Mexico ceasing to send them oil," the ÔÇőWhite House official said.

item.title: Israel targets Iranian checkpoints using tip-offs from informants, source says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 13 (Reuters) - Israel has launched a new phase of its assault on Iran, targeting checkpoints manned by ÔÇőthe Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) based on tip-offs from ÔÇîinformants on the ground, a source briefed on Israel's military strategy told Reuters.
The targeting of checkpoints secured by IRGC personnel suggests an intensification ÔÇőof Israel's efforts to weaken Iran's elite forces as ÔÇőit carries out joint aerial bombardment with the ÔüáUnited States.
Israel says its war objectives include destroying Iran's military ÔÇőand nuclear capabilities but also "creating the conditions" for Iranians to ÔÇőoverthrow their government, though Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has said this is an uncertain prospect.
There has been no sign of organised dissent while the ÔÇőcountry is under attack, and no sign of Iran's ÔÇőrulers relinquishing power.
On Thursday, Israel's military said it had struck checkpoints in ÔÇîTehran Ôüáoperated by the Basij, a part-time paramilitary force under IRGC control that is often used to quell protests inside Iran.
The source, who spoke on condition of anonymity because they were not ÔÇőauthorised to ÔÇőspeak publicly, said Ôüáinformants on the ground in Tehran had passed intelligence to Israel on the locations of ÔÇőthree checkpoints that were struck in the past ÔÇőthree Ôüádays. The source was not able to confirm whether these included the Basij positions mentioned by the military.
The use of informants Ôüáto ÔÇőidentify checkpoint locations was first reported ÔÇőby the Wall Street Journal.
Israel's military did not immediately respond to a request ÔÇőfor comment.

item.title: Deputy head of Russian space centre detained on suspicion of bribery, TASS reports
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 13 (Reuters) - The deputy head of Russia's Star City, a settlement near ÔÇőMoscow that hosts the country's main ÔÇîspace training facility, has been detained along with six associates on suspicion of ÔÇőbribery, state news agency TASS reported ÔÇőon Friday.
According to RIA news agency, Ôüáin January the mayor of ÔÇőStar City Yevgeny Barishevsky was detained on ÔÇősuspicion of accepting a bribe of 365,000 roubles ($4,557) and put under house arrest.
Investigators said on ÔÇőFriday that the inquiry had ÔÇőestablished that between 2023 and 2025 the mayor ÔÇîand Ôüáhis deputy received several bribes from businessmen, both directly and through intermediaries, in exchange for favourable outcomes in the ÔÇőawarding of ÔÇőmunicipal Ôüácontracts.
The total amount involved exceeded 9.9 million roubles ($123,602).
Star City lies ÔÇőabout an hour's drive northeast ÔÇőof Ôüáthe Kremlin. For decades, the town did not appear on maps, with Ôüáits ÔÇőlocation revealed only after ÔÇőthe collapse of the Soviet Union.
($1 = 80.0955 roubles)

item.title: Drone strike in Sudan kills 11, injures over 20, MSF says
item.description: GENEVA, March 13 (Reuters) - French medical charity M├ędecins Sans Fronti├Ęres said on Friday it had treated ÔÇőover 20 people for injuries after a ÔÇîdrone strike set fuel reserves alight in western Sudan, killing 11 people.

item.title: Russia names great-granddaughter of Soviet leader Khrushchev as 'foreign agent'
item.description:
LONDON, March 13 (Reuters) - U.S. academic Nina Khrushcheva, the great-granddaughter of a ÔÇőformer Soviet leader, was designated by Russia on Friday as a "foreign agent" - a term ÔÇîwith connotations of spying that Moscow applies to people it views as engaged in anti-Russian activity.
Khrushcheva, 62, is a professor at The New School university in New York and has continued to make research trips to Russia since ÔÇőits full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
Her ancestor Nikita Khrushchev led the Soviet Union from ÔÇő1953 to 1964, when he was ousted by fellow members of the ruling ÔüáPolitburo.
Contacted by Reuters, Khrushcheva said she was not surprised at being added to Russia's "foreign agent" list, ÔÇőwhich as of Friday contains 1,164 names including politicians, journalists, artists, NGOs and media organisations.
"It would have ÔÇőbeen sloppy on their part not to do this sooner or later," she said, adding that it was too early to say what the practical impact would be.
"There is certainly historical irony but not anything shocking. When ÔÇőStalin is up, Khrushchev is down," she said.
She was alluding to a resurgence in Russia of ÔÇőthe reputation of Soviet dictator Josef Stalin, whose reign of terror was denounced by Khrushchev in a famous ÔÇî1956 address Ôüáto a Communist Party congress.
Russia marked the 70th anniversary of the speech last month, prompting renewed debate about the legacies of both men.
Khrushchev was the Soviet leader who transferred Crimea to Ukraine from Russia in 1954, an act reversed in 2014 when Russian forces invaded the peninsula and President Vladimir ÔÇőPutin declared its annexation .
Khrushchev is also ÔÇőremembered for facing Ôüáoff against U.S. President John F. Kennedy in the 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis, when the world teetered on the brink of nuclear war.
Russian news agency ÔÇőTASS cited the Justice Ministry as saying Nina Khrushcheva had disseminated false ÔÇőinformation about Russian Ôüápolicies and opposed what Moscow calls its "special military operation" in Ukraine.
People listed as foreign agents are subjected to onerous bureaucratic requirements and restrictions on their income in Russia. They are obliged to place the foreign Ôüáagent label ÔÇőon social media posts or anything else they publish.
Some ÔÇőKremlin critics have worn the label as a badge of honour, while others say it is a burden that hampers them ÔÇőin their work because it causes other Russians to shun them.

item.title: Iran coach Jafari says television comments created difficult atmosphere for players during Asian Cup
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Comments from an Iranian state television presenter about the country's women's team during their Asian Cup campaign affected the players psychologically and drove some to ÔÇőseek asylum in Australia, coach Marziyeh Jafari said on Friday.
The tournament, ÔÇîhosted by Australia, started just as the U.S. and Israel launched air strikes on Iran, killing the Islamic Republic's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei.
After the team opted to stand in silence during Iran's ÔÇőanthem before their first match against South Korea, they were labelled as "wartime traitors" ÔÇőby Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting presenter Mohammad Reza Shahbazi.
In a Ôüástatement posted by Iran's football federation (FFIRI) on its Telegram page on Friday, which ÔÇőhas since been deleted, Jafari said: "Our girls were affected in the first match ÔÇőby the heavy atmosphere that had been created...
"But the greater mistake was made by those who, at home, failed to understand that atmosphere and sounded the call to arms against the daughters ÔÇőof this land," she added, referring to the presenter's comments.
"What I have asked ÔÇőof the federation is to pursue the matter, because it affected our players psychologically, and ÔÇîwe suffered Ôüáthe consequences. I am certain that if that atmosphere had not been created, not a single one of our players would have stayed in Australia."
Australia this week granted humanitarian visas to five Iranian players who sought asylum during the tournament.
Two more ÔÇősquad members were granted ÔÇőasylum on Wednesday, Ôüáand one decided to return to Iran .
"Australian police called the players in several stages and sat down with them one-on-one to ÔÇőpersuade them to stay, influenced by the political atmosphere that ÔÇőhad arisen," ÔüáJafari said.
"Fortunately, the majority of the team members responded negatively. Even Mohaddeseh Zolfi, who had responded positively, soon changed her mind and, God willing, will come to Iran Ôüáwith the ÔÇőteam.
"The rumours about Golnoosh Khosravi and Afsaneh Chatrenoor ÔÇőare also not true at all, and they are now with us in Malaysia and we will ÔÇőbe leaving for Iran soon."

item.title: Pentagon elevates investigation into Iran school strike
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - The U.S. military said on Friday it has elevated the investigation into a devastating February 28 strike on an Iranian girls' school after media reports revealed ÔÇőthe probe shows U.S. forces were likely responsible.
Iran has said the strike on the Shajareh Tayyebeh School killed 168 children. If U.S. fault is confirmed, ÔÇîit would rank among the worst incidents of civilian deaths in decades of U.S. military strikes in the Middle East.
Reuters first reported the investigation's initial findings on March 5.
Citing the seriousness of the matter, U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth declined to comment on the preliminary finding of the investigation, saying: "We're not going to let reporting lead us or force our hand into indicating what happened."
He said a higher-level investigation would be led by ÔÇőan unnamed U.S. general officer from outside of U.S. Central Command, which is overseeing operations against Iran. Such a move is typically taken by the U.S. military to ÔÇőensure greater independence for investigators.
"The command investigation will take as long as necessary to address all the matters surrounding this incident," Hegseth told Ôüáa press conference at the Pentagon.
Iran's new supreme leader blamed the country's enemies for the school strike in his first message to the country on Thursday, without explicitly saying the ÔÇőU.S. was responsible.
Three U.S. officials told Reuters the command investigation Hegseth referred to is an administrative probe known as a 15-6, which can become the ÔÇőbasis for disciplinary action if warranted. It usually includes sworn statements or interviews with those involved. The officials spoke on condition of anonymity to describe internal military processes.
The head of U.S. Central Command, Admiral Brad Cooper, ordered the 15-6 probe last week after the initial review was completed. The outside officer was appointed on Thursday, one official said.
The development underscores the serious concern about how to respond to possible U.S. responsibility ÔÇőfor the tragedy and the need to comprehensively examine the incident.
"It signals a recognition that something went wrong and to try to understand what happened and why," said Annie Shiel, ÔÇőU.S. advocacy director with the Center for Civilians in Conflict.
It remains unclear how U.S. forces could have struck the school. Preliminary findings suggest U.S. forces may have relied upon outdated targeting data ÔÇîthat apparently did Ôüánot distinguish between the school and the adjoining Iranian military base in Minab, a town in southern Iran.
Video and other evidence suggest the school was hit by a Tomahawk cruise missile , a powerful, precision-guided munition that few nations other than the U.S. possess.
The investigation comes despite public comments by President Donald Trump initially doubting the possibility of U.S. involvement that stoked concern among some defense officials about whether he would be willing to accept blame.
The president initially said, without offering evidence, that Iran may have attacked the school and suggested Tehran may have Tomahawk missiles, ÔÇőwhich military experts call extremely unlikely.
Still, Trump ÔÇőon Monday said publicly he would "certainly" accept Ôüáthe results of the U.S. investigation: "Whatever the report shows, I'm willing to live with that report."
A second U.S. official pointed to those remarks as evidence that Trump was moving toward accepting the preliminary investigation results. Further, the official said public discussion of the investigation had made it ÔÇőpolitically difficult for Trump to reject the findings once they are finalized.
The official noted that a 15-6 investigation reflected the seriousness of ÔÇőthe Trump administration's approach Ôüáto the investigation because it would comprehensively examine what went wrong and how to correct those issues.
A Reuters visual investigation published on Thursday showed the Iranian school had a years-long, public online presence, including dozens of photos of the children and their activities.
The Shajareh Tayyebeh School was one of 59 schools within the Persian Gulf Martyrs' Cultural Educational Institute, a network affiliated with the ÔüáIslamic Revolutionary ÔÇőGuard Corps, the military force that reports to Iran's supreme leader, according to archived copies of the networkÔÇÖs ÔÇőwebsite.
The schoolÔÇÖs website includes photos of students gathered in the yard, which matched verified videos outside the building after the strike.
Satellite imagery from mid-2015 shows the building was walled off from the Iranian military base and appears to have ÔÇőoperated as a school since at least 2018, when the painted murals are first visible on its outer walls.

item.title: Israeli strike kills three in Gaza as regional offensives escalate
item.description:
CAIRO/JERUSALEM, March 13 (Reuters) - An ÔÇőIsraeli airstrike in Gaza killed three Palestinians, including two 17-year-olds, Palestinian medics ÔÇîsaid on Friday, with violence continuing in the West Bank and Gaza even as Israel expands its offensive across the region.
Israel has used lethal force in Iran , Lebanon , Gaza and the occupied ÔÇőWest Bank over the past 24 hours.
Israeli forces killed two in ÔÇőthe West Bank city of Nablus on Thursday, according to Palestinian officials, Ôüáwhile the death toll in Lebanon reached 773, its Health Ministry said on ÔÇőFriday.
After nearly two weeks of war, 2,000 people have been killed, most of ÔÇőthem in Iran, with several million people displaced from their homes.
The Israeli military told Reuters that they were not aware of the strike in Gaza.
The military said separately in a statement ÔÇőon Thursday that they had killed two individuals after they attempted to carry ÔÇőout a car-ramming attack on soldiers. It did not immediately respond to a request for ÔÇîevidence Ôüáof the attempted ramming.
While Israeli attacks on Gaza declined at the beginning of the war with Iran, they have since begun to rise. Israel has killed 23 in Gaza since the U.S. and Israel jointly struck Iran on February 28.
While ÔÇőa ceasefire went into ÔÇőeffect in Gaza Ôüáin October, there have been regular outbreaks of violence since then.
Reuters has reported that U.S. President Donald Trump's peace plan ÔÇőfor Gaza has been on hold since the start of the ÔÇőIran war.
Violence Ôüáhas also persisted in the West Bank. Israeli settlers and military forces have killed at least eight Palestinians across the West Bank since the U.S.-Israeli attacks on ÔüáIran began.
Reuters ÔÇőhas reported that there has been a spike ÔÇőin settler violence since then, as Israel has put much of the West Bank under lockdown.

item.title: Mother and three sons in custody over US embassy bomb in Norway
item.description:
OSLO, March 13 (Reuters) - Three brothers and their mother have been ÔÇőremanded in police custody for ÔÇîup to four weeks on suspicion of taking part in ÔÇőthe bombing of the ÔÇőU.S. embassy in Oslo last Ôüáweek, a Norwegian court ÔÇősaid in a ruling on ÔÇőFriday.
The U.S. embassy was hit by an explosion on Sunday and police later ÔÇősaid they had apprehended the ÔÇősuspects, accusing them of a "terror bombing" ÔÇîintended Ôüáto kill or cause significant damage.
The powerful early-morning blast from an improvised explosive device (IED) ÔÇődamaged the ÔÇőentrance Ôüáto the embassy's consular section but caused ÔÇőno injuries, Norwegian authorities ÔÇőhave Ôüásaid.
One of the men has admitted to placing a Ôüádevice, ÔÇőwhile the three ÔÇőother suspects have denied involvement, their lawyers ÔÇőhave said.

item.title: Exclusive: Iran lets two gas tankers sail to India through Hormuz, sources say
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 13 (Reuters) - Iran has allowed two Indian-flagged liquefied petroleum gas carriers to sail through the Strait of Hormuz, four sources with direct knowledge of the matter said, a ÔÇőrare exception to the Iranian blockade that has disrupted global energy supplies.
The Indian-flagged LPG tanker Shivalik ÔÇîcrossed the Strait under escort from the Indian Navy, said two of the sources, and the second vessel, Nanda Devi, was expected to clear in the next few hours.
Since the United States and Israel launched a bombing campaign on Iran, Tehran has largely halted ÔÇőtraffic through the strait, which runs past its coast and normally supplies around 20% of global oil ÔÇőand seaborne liquefied natural gas.
Iran has said it will not permit any supplies for Ôüáthe United States or its allies to leave the strait, but India has sought exemptions. Prime Minister Narendra Modi said ÔÇőon Thursday he had spoken to Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian and discussed the transit of goods and energy from ÔÇőthe Gulf.
India has also given safe harbour to 183 Iranian sailors from a vessel that docked after the war broke out. The vessel was one of three that had participated in exercises in India; one of the others was sunk by a U.S. torpedo off Sri ÔÇőLanka.
India is reeling under its worst gas crisis in decades with the government cutting supplies for industries to shield ÔÇőhouseholds from any shortage of cooking gas.
Shivalik and Nanda Devi are owned by state-run Shipping Corp of India. (SCI.NS) , opens new tab
Shivalik, which had arrived in ÔÇîQatari Ôüáwaters on February 25, last reported its position on March 12 as within the exclusive economic zone waters up to 24 miles off the United Arab Emirates, according to tracking data on MarineTraffic.
Nanda Devi was last seen on Friday in Iranian waters close to the entrance of the Strait of Hormuz, according to MarineTraffic data. It had called ÔÇőat QatarÔÇÖs Ras Laffan anchorage ÔÇőon February 27 before Ôüásailing to UAE waters, the data showed.
Separately, a crude tanker is expected to arrive in India on Saturday carrying Saudi Arabian oil after sailing through the Strait around March ÔÇő1, according to two of the sources and data from Lloyd's List Intelligence.
The Liberia ÔÇőflagged Smyrni crude Ôüáoil suezmax tanker, which can carry a maximum capacity of 1 million barrels, is expected to arrive at an Indian port for state refiner Hindustan Petroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab .
India's foreign ministry, navy, Shipping Corp and HPCL did not respond to Reuters emails Ôüásent out ÔÇőafter business hours.
India consumed 33.15 million metric tons of cooking gas ÔÇőlast year, with imports accounting for about 60% of demand. About 90% of those imports came from the Middle East.
On Thursday India said 24 Indian-flagged ÔÇővessels were stuck in the Gulf area past the narrow strait.

item.title: US proposes easing limits on cancer-causing gas used to clean medical devices
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - The Trump administration is proposing to lift some limits on the use of ethylene oxide, a carcinogenic gas used for sterilization, saying ÔÇőprevious constraints imposed under former President Joe Biden may hamper medical device makers' ÔÇîability to clean their facilities.
In a statement on Friday, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency said its proposed rule would follow current law but make it easier for companies to ÔÇőfollow commercial sterilization regulations while also saving costs and protecting the ÔÇőnation's supply chain for devices such as heart stents and wound Ôüádressings.
The medical community, industry officials and the Food and Drug Administration all ÔÇőexpressed concerns about the more stringent rule issued in 2024 under the Democratic ÔÇőBiden administration, which included a second required risk review, as well as standards that required new monitoring systems, vents and enclosures.
The new proposal would allow medical device companies a choice ÔÇőbetween installing new monitoring systems or making other adjustments to new aeration ÔÇőroom vents in areas where ethylene oxide is greater than 10 tons per year.
"These changes ÔÇîbetter Ôüáreflect the complexity of facilities and give them the flexibility to work to use safe and effective equipment to sterilize medical tools and devices without compromising clean air for all Americans," the EPA said in a statement.
They would ÔÇőalso save an estimated $43 ÔÇőmillion annually, Ôüáit added.
Ethylene oxide, or EtO, is a colorless gas used to sterilize equipment, but its toxic emissions pollute the air. ÔÇőLong-term exposure has been linked to cancer, especially for ÔÇőpeople living Ôüáclose to manufacturing plants that make or use it.
The gas is used to sterilize about half of all U.S. medical devices made each year, according to the ÔüáEPA.
The ÔÇőproposed rule will face a public hearing in ÔÇőabout 15 days and is open to public comment for 45 days before the administration makes ÔÇőa final decision.

item.title: Britain's energy price-cap forecast to rise about 11%, Cornwall Insight says
item.description:
LONDON, March 13 (Reuters) - Britain's domestic energy price cap is forecast to rise by about 11% in July, analysts ÔÇőat Cornwall Insight said on Friday, increasing ÔÇîtheir forecast due to the ongoing impact of the Iran war on wholesale energy prices.
British finance minister Rachel Reeves said ÔÇőon Wednesday she would consider action to shield households ÔÇőfrom surging energy costs due to the Ôüáconflict in the Middle East, but that it was ÔÇőpremature at this stage.
Wholesale gas prices have risen more ÔÇőthan 60% over the past two weeks. Those prices are the largest single factor contributing to British energy regulator Ofgem's ÔÇődomestic price cap, which is set quarterly using ÔÇőa formula that also reflects suppliers' network costs and environmental and ÔÇîsocial Ôüálevies.
Disruptions to shipping in the Middle East and a halt to liquefied natural gas exports from Qatar, one of the world's largest gas exporters, has led ÔÇőto a surge ÔÇőin international Ôüágas prices which is showing no signs of abating.
Cornwall Insight forecasts Ofgem's price ÔÇőcap will rise in July to 1,827 ÔÇőpounds ($2,422) Ôüáa year for typical use, from 1,641 pounds in April.
Last week they had forecast a rise of 10% Ôüáin ÔÇőthe cap.
Ofgem is expected to ÔÇőpublish the next price cap level by May 27.
($1 = 0.7545 pounds)

item.title: Brazil's Petrobras expects little impact on consumers from diesel price hike
item.description:
SAO PAULO/RIO DE JANEIRO, March 13 (Reuters) - Brazilian state-run ÔÇîoil company Petrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab will hike the price of diesel sold to distributors by 0.38 reais ($0.0725) per liter, but expects next to no impact on consumers, CEO Magda Chambriard said on Friday.
The Petrobras hike starting on Saturday should lead to an added cost of ÔÇőup to 0.06 reais per liter for consumers, Chambriard told journalists, calling it "residual" as federal tax cuts ÔÇőease the impact.
"Petrobras is following its commercial strategy, avoiding passing on the volatility of Ôüáinternational prices, and the government is doing its part by eliminating federal taxes," said Chambriard.
The move comes after ÔÇőthe recent spike in oil prices due to the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran , which widened the gap between Petrobras' local diesel ÔÇőprices and international benchmarks to a record level in recent days.
Diesel prices have become a major source of concern for Brazil's President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, who is running for reelection this year.
On Thursday, Lula's government scrapped taxes on diesel in a bid ÔÇőto soften the impact of higher global prices on local consumers, while imposing a 12% levy on oil ÔÇőexports to compensate for the lost revenue.
The Petrobras CEO said the export tax will be more than offset by higher oil ÔÇîprices. Exports Ôüáhave become a major driver of the company's revenue growth, as it sold record volumes of oil abroad in the last quarter.
"I was exporting for $60," said Chambriard. "Now it's at $100. Can I complain about a temporary 12% tax?"
The CEO added that the higher oil prices are "good" for dividends to shareholders and said that shareholders - both the government ÔÇőand private ones - are happy ÔÇőwith the firm.
While most of the diesel Brazil consumes is produced locally by Petrobras, about 25% of the fuel is imported, leaving the country vulnerable to price hikes ÔÇőabroad.
The imbalance between local and international prices was making distributors reluctant to sell at ÔÇőPetrobras' prices, Ôüáas they were worried they would have to resupply at higher cost.
To increase diesel production, Petrobras is operating its refineries at around 97% of capacity and postponed maintenance stoppages at two of them, Chambriard said.
Last year, the company was operating Ôüárefineries ÔÇőat around 91% of capacity, and had previously announced it would raise ÔÇőit to 95% over the first quarter.
"We are making an effort to deliver more product to the Brazilian market," she said.
($1 = 5.2441 reais)

item.title: Storms cut power to 396,000 customers in US Midwest, Mid-Atlantic; Ohio hardest hit
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 13 (Reuters) - Over 396,000 homes ÔÇőand businesses were without ÔÇîpower on Friday as severe storms battered the U.S. ÔÇőMidwest and Mid-Atlantic ÔÇőregion, according to data from ÔüáPowerOutage.us.
The hardest-hit state ÔÇőwas Ohio, with around ÔÇő123,000 outages, representing about 2.3% of the state's roughly 5.4 ÔÇőmillion customers, according to ÔÇőthe data.
The hardest-hit utility in ÔÇîOhio Ôüáwas a unit of American Electric Power (AEP.O) , opens new tab , with around 40,000 homes and ÔÇőbusinesses in ÔÇőthe Ôüástate without service. AEP provides service ÔÇőto around 1.5 ÔÇőmillion Ôüáelectric customers in Ohio, the data showed.
Here are Ôüáthe ÔÇőmajor outages by ÔÇőstate:

item.title: US offers reward for information on Iran's supreme leader, senior officials
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - The United States is offering ÔÇőa reward of up to $10 million for information about senior Iranian military and intelligence ÔÇîofficials, including its new Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei.
The reward targets 10 officials associated with Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), according to the State Department website. The military force, created after IranÔÇÖs 1979 Islamic Revolution, is ÔÇőloyal to the supreme leader and tasked with protecting the ShiÔÇÖite clerical establishment.
Mojtaba Khamenei ÔÇőrecently succeeded his father, Ali Khamenei, as Iran's supreme leader after the elder Khamenei Ôüáwas killed along with several other top Iranian officials in joint U.S. and Israeli strikes ÔÇőthat began on February 28. The younger Khamenei, believed to have been injured in the strikes , hasn't ÔÇőbeen seen publicly since, although he released his first statement on Thursday.
In addition to the supreme leader, the U.S. is seeking information about Iran's security chief Ali Larijani, Intelligence Minister Esmail Khatib, Interior Minister Eskandar Momeni and ÔÇőtwo officials in Khamenei's office.
Larijani appeared Friday in videos verified by Reuters alongside President Masoud Pezeshkian ÔÇőand Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi attending a rally in Tehran, despite an assertion by U.S. Defense Secretary Pete ÔÇîHegseth Ôüáthat Iran's leadership was "cowering" underground.
The reward website also lists four other officials, including the IRGC commander and secretary of the defense council, but doesn't include their names or photos.
"These individuals command and direct various elements of the IRGC, which plans, organizes, and executes terrorism around the world," the ÔÇőState Department said.
The Revolutionary Guards ÔÇőcould not be Ôüáimmediately reached for comment on Friday ÔÇö the weekly day of rest in Iran. Iran's mission to the United Nations in New York didn't immediately ÔÇőrespond to a request for comment.
The U.S. has designated the IRGC as ÔÇőa foreign Ôüáterrorist organization, accusing it of being responsible for attacks that have killed U.S. citizens. Washington has also accused Iran of orchestrating assassination plots against President Donald Trump and other U.S. officials in retaliation for Ôüáthe killing ÔÇőof Iranian military commander Qassem Soleimani in 2020.
Iran denies being ÔÇőa sponsor of terrorism. Iranian officials routinely dismiss U.S. terrorism allegations as baseless political attacks, arguing Washington raises such claims ÔÇőto justify pressure campaigns or sanctions.

item.title: Zelenskiy tells Ukrainian media US sought postponement of latest talks on war settlement
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy was quoted as saying ÔÇőon Friday the U.S. ÔÇîhad sought a postponement of the latest round of ÔÇőthree-sided talks on ÔÇőa settlement to Ukraine's four-year-old Ôüáconflict with Russia.
Zelenskiy, ÔÇőwhose comments were quoted by ÔÇővarious Ukrainian media outlets at the end of a visit ÔÇőto France, said ÔÇőthe U.S. side said its negotiators ÔÇîwere Ôüánot permitted to leave the United States in view of circumstances in ÔÇőthe ÔÇőMiddle ÔüáEast.
He said Russia had not wanted to ÔÇőhold the talks ÔÇőin Ôüáthe United States and had proposed alternative sites Ôüáin ÔÇőSwitzerland or Turkey.

item.title: US expands Venezuela sanctions waivers amid rising prices
item.description:
LOS ANGELES, March 13 (Reuters) - The United States on Friday ÔÇőupdated waivers to Venezuela sanctions ÔÇîaimed at easing the way for investment and activities related to the ÔÇőcountry's oil and the export ÔÇőof fertilizer, as prices rise Ôüáamid U.S. strikes on Iran.
The ÔÇőU.S. Treasury Department issued three updated ÔÇőgeneral licenses on Friday as part of the move. In a post on ÔÇőX, the department said it "updated ÔÇőseveral Venezuela-related licenses to further support the revitalization ÔÇîof Ôüáthe countryÔÇÖs energy sector and help ensure a well-supplied global commodity market."
"These authorizations expand permitted investment ÔÇőand activities ÔÇőin ÔüáVenezuelaÔÇÖs energy industry and allow for the export ÔÇőof fertilizer directly to the ÔÇőU.S. Ôüáto support our great American farmers," the Treasury Department said in Ôüáa ÔÇőpost on X.

item.title: Earthquake of magnitude 6.5 strikes near coast of central Chile, GFZ says
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - A magnitude ÔÇő6.5 ÔÇîearthquake struck near ÔÇőthe ÔÇőcoast of Ôüácentral ÔÇőChile ÔÇőon Friday, the German ÔÇőResearch ÔÇőCentre for ÔÇîGeosciences (GFZ) Ôüásaid.
The quake was at ÔÇőa ÔÇődepth Ôüáof ÔÇő10 ÔÇőkm (6.21 Ôüámiles), GFZ Ôüásaid.

item.title: US-owned tanker attacked near Iraq was hit by unmanned boats, early findings show
item.description:
LOS ANGELES, March 13 (Reuters) - Two explosive-laden, unmanned ÔÇîboats rammed the Safesea Vishnu tanker in an Iraqi seaport on Wednesday, sparking a fiery blast that engulfed the vessel's port side in flames and left crew only seconds to react, according to an early assessment conducted by the vessel's ÔÇőU.S. owner and operator.
"After speaking to the surviving crew members, the attack appears to have ÔÇőbeen deliberate and calculated," New Jersey-based Safesea Group said in a statement.
At least Ôüá16 tankers and other vessels have come under attack in the Gulf during the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran. ÔÇőHundreds more have dropped anchor due to Tehran's threat to attack vessels in or near the Strait ÔÇőof Hormuz, which is used to transport roughly one-fifth of the world's oil.
The Marshall Islands-flagged Safesea Vishnu was anchored at the Iraqi port of Khor Al Zubair and engaged in ship-to-ship loading of 53,000 metric tons of naphtha at ÔÇőthe time of the attack.
The vessel's 28 crew members, without time to deploy lifeboats, jumped into ÔÇőthe water to escape the burning vessel. One died; the remaining 27 crew are safe and receiving assistance from ÔÇîthe Embassy Ôüáof India in Iraq, Safesea said. The tanker is reported to be listing in the water, and a salvage team has been sent to stabilize the ship and make sure the surrounding marine environment is safe.
The attack must serve as a wake-up call for governments, maritime authorities, and the international community, ÔÇőSafesea said.
"Commercial shipping lanes ÔÇőcannot become battle zones," Ôüáit said.
The Malta-flagged Zefyros was the other ship involved in the transfer. A projectile struck that vessel during the Wednesday night assault, its Greece-based manager ÔÇősaid on Thursday. All 23 crew on the Zefyros were safely evacuated.
Some 20,000 ÔÇőseafarers aboard Ôüávessels operating in the region face "a dangerous and highly uncertain security situation," according to the World Shipping Council.
U.S. President Donald Trump has said the United States is prepared to escort tankers through the Strait of Hormuz when Ôüánecessary, ÔÇőbut so far the U.S. Navy has refused near-daily requests ÔÇőfrom the shipping industry for military escorts since the start of the war on Iran, saying the risk of attacks is too ÔÇőhigh for now, according to sources familiar with the matter.

item.title: UN official calls for humanitarian cargo to be allowed through Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - A top United Nations humanitarian official on Friday called for humanitarian aid to be ÔÇőallowed to pass safely through the Strait of ÔÇîHormuz amid the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
The comments made by Tom Fletcher, the U.N. under-secretary-general for humanitarian affairs and emergency relief coordinator, comes as ÔÇőIran threatens to close the strait , which is considered a key ÔÇőtrade route .
"When routes close and costs surge, the help Ôüáwe can deliver shrinks ÔÇô and the people who need ÔÇőit most are the ones who lose it first," Fletcher ÔÇősaid in a statement.
"So my message to the parties to the conflict and all those with influence over them is simple: humanitarian cargo ÔÇőmust be allowed to pass safely through the Strait of ÔÇőHormuz."
The war started two weeks ago when the U.S. and Israel carried out ÔÇîstrikes Ôüáon Iran, killing top officials, including its supreme leader. Hundreds others have also died since the Feb. 28 start of the war, which has spread across the Gulf region.
Iran retaliated ÔÇőto the initial ÔÇőU.S.-Israeli attack Ôüáby launching its own strikes and deploying about a dozen mines in the strait.
The ongoing conflict ÔÇőhas already caused a near halt in traffic ÔÇőthrough Ôüáthe Strait of Hormuz, Fletcher said. This, he added, will make it harder and more expensive to deliver critical supplies, including Ôüáfood ÔÇőand medicine.
"I am speaking directly with ÔÇőkey parties, pressing for humanitarian supplies to be allowed to keep moving unobstructed ÔÇőthrough the Strait," he said.

item.title: Refiner MOL complains to EU over Croatian pipeline fees
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 13 (Reuters) - Hungarian refiner MOL (MOLB.BU) , opens new tab said on Friday it and subsidiary Slovnaft had ÔÇőfiled a complaint with the European Commission ÔÇîover Croatian pipeline operator JANAF's pricing policy.
It follows a complaint MOL filed last week with the EU's competition ÔÇőwatchdog over JANAF's refusal to deliver ÔÇőRussian crude to MOL and its Slovak Ôüásubsidiary.
An outage since January on the Druzhba pipeline delivering ÔÇőRussian oil via Ukraine has left landlocked Hungary ÔÇőand Slovakia reliant on oil supply via JANAF.
"JANAF has consistently applied abusive pricing practices," MOL said in its ÔÇőlatest complaint, adding its orders by volume ÔÇőhad increased by one and a half times while ÔÇîJANAF's Ôüáfees had nearly doubled.
"The fee increases imposed by JANAF are excessive compared to its costs and cannot be objectively justified."
Janaf did not immediately ÔÇőto a ÔÇőrequest for Ôüácomment.
Hungary and Slovakia hold exemptions to EU restrictions on Russian oil imports.
MOL ÔÇőis allowed to source Russian seaborne ÔÇőcrude Ôüáif the Druzhba pipeline is inoperable, the company has said.
Croatia has expressed its willingness to help Ôüásupply ÔÇőcrude, but has baulked at ÔÇősending Russian crude via the JANAF's pipeline.

item.title: Iran war puts many in US on high alert, but synagogue attack shows limits
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - U.S. law enforcement officials are on heightened alert ÔÇőas the Iran war enters its third week, but the limits of their vigilance were on display as seeming lone-wolf attacks unfolded more than 500 miles apart in Virginia and Michigan on ÔÇîThursday alone.
In Michigan, where a Lebanon-born man rammed an explosives-laden truck into the Temple Israel synagogue, it appeared to be one of the first known instances of a specific bombing in the Iran war leading to retaliatory violence on American soil since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran on February 28.
Although authorities have not announced a motive, the man, identified by U.S. officials as Ayman Ghazali, carried out his attack a week after Israel bombed his family's town in Lebanon on March 5, killing two of his brothers ÔÇőand a niece and nephew, according to news reports.
Around the same time as the synagogue attack, a man who was previously convicted of supporting a designated terrorist group fatally shot one person and injured ÔÇőtwo others, both U.S. Army personnel, at Old Dominion University in Norfolk, Virginia, which has close ties to the U.S. military. The attacker was killed.
These kinds of lone-wolf outbursts of retaliatory violence are the hardest to spot in advance and intercept, counter-terrorism experts say.
"Someone who is self-radicalized, a lone wolf, is the hardest to track," said Kenneth Gray, a former ÔÇőFBI counter-terrorism agent who now teaches at the University of New Haven. "Normally there would be opportunities to track weapon transfers, foreign training, or transfers of funds. We apparently have none of that."
The latest incidents took place with the ÔÇőU.S. engaged in what is already a deeply unpopular war among American voters, which was launched after President Donald Trump made drastic cuts to the Department of Homeland Security's intelligence unit.
Except for a security guard knocked down when the truck crashed through the entrance, everyone at the synagogue, including the children at its preschool, escaped unharmed, although dozens of law enforcement officers were treated for smoke inhalation. Ghazali, who became a U.S. citizen in 2016 and lived in nearby Dearborn Heights, was killed by synagogue security staff.
Shawn Brokos, ÔÇőwho was an FBI official in Pittsburgh when 11 people were killed in the Tree of Life synagogue shooting in 2018, credited Temple Israel for a "textbook reaction" that protected those inside.
The attack also showed the need for ÔÇővigilance, she said, to be alert for someone who might be embracing "a grievance narrative."
"They're upset about current events," said Brokos, now the director of community security for the Jewish Federation of Greater Pittsburgh. "They're starting to access weapons. They're starting to make ÔÇîplans. These Ôüáare all the things that are helpful for us to know so we can do these checks and share it with law enforcement."
Dearborn Heights Mayor Mo Baydoun said Ghazali's attack must be unequivocally condemned, and also noted Ghazali had been affected by the Iran war, in which more than 2,000 people have been killed , mostly in Iran and Lebanon.
"The tensions we see across the world too often find their way into our own neighborhoods, reminding us how deeply connected our shared safety is," Baydoun said in a statement.
Since the war began, security scares have affected airports in Kansas City and the Washington, D.C. suburbs; two men were charged with terrorism-related ÔÇőcrimes after throwing home-made bombs at an anti-Muslim rally ÔÇőin New York City; and a man who expressed Ôüápro-Iran views online killed four people after opening fire at a bar in Austin, Texas.
Trump has sought to defend his war to some lawmakers in Congress and, according to polls , many skeptical voters, including some of his "America First" base.
The Department of Homeland Security communicates significant threats to the American public through alerts under the National Terrorism Advisory ÔÇőSystem. DHS issued such an alert in June 2025, saying brief hostilities between the U.S. and Israel and Iran had caused a "heightened threat environment" in the U.S.
That ÔÇőalert, which cited the risk Ôüáof cyber attacks or violence from extremists already in the U.S., expired in September 2025.
There are no current NTAS advisories. And, last week, the White House halted a bulletin from DHS, the FBI and the National Counterterrorism Center warning state and local law enforcement agencies of a heightened threat to the U.S. because of the Iran war.
Since Trump's return to the White House last year with vows to slash the federal workforce, the DHS Intelligence and Analysis unit went from about Ôüá1,000 employees to ÔÇősome 500-600, three former agency officials told Reuters. DHS did not respond to a request for comment.
Travis Nelson, director of Maryland's homeland ÔÇősecurity office, said DHS has been focused on Trump's mass deportation efforts in its public communications rather than the possible threat posed by the Iran conflict.
"It's all about immigration enforcement," Nelson said. "We haven't seen anything from Homeland Security about the potential risk to the homeland as a ÔÇőresult of what's going on in the Middle East."

item.title: Five US Air Force refueling planes hit in Iranian strike on Saudi Arabia, WSJ reports
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Five U.S. Air Force refueling planes ÔÇőwere struck and ÔÇîdamaged on the ground at Prince Sultan ÔÇőair base in ÔÇőSaudi Arabia, the Wall ÔüáStreet Journal reported ÔÇőon Friday, citing two ÔÇőU.S. officials.
The planes, which were hit during an ÔÇőIranian missile strike ÔÇőon the Saudi base in ÔÇîrecent Ôüádays, were damaged but not fully destroyed and are ÔÇőbeing repaired, ÔÇőthe ÔüáJournal said, adding that no ÔÇőone was killed ÔÇőin Ôüáthe strikes.
Reuters could not immediately verify Ôüáthe ÔÇőreport.

item.title: US attacks Iran's Kharg Island, Trump says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump ÔÇőon Friday Said ÔÇîon Friday that the United States carried ÔÇőout strikes against "every military ÔÇőtarget" on Iran's Kharg Island Ôüáexport hub.
"For ÔÇőreasons of decency, I ÔÇőhave chosen NOT to wipe out the Oil ÔÇőInfrastructure on ÔÇőthe Island," Trump wrote on ÔÇîTruth ÔüáSocial.
"However, should Iran, or anyone else, do anything to ÔÇőinterfere ÔÇőwith Ôüáthe Free and Safe Passage ÔÇőof Ships through ÔÇőthe ÔüáStrait of Hormuz, I will immediately Ôüáreconsider ÔÇőthis decision."

item.title: Ecuador declares curfew in coastal provinces for security operations
item.description:
QUITO, March 13 (Reuters) - Ecuador's President Daniel Noboa on Friday decreed a nighttime ÔÇőcurfew in four provinces of the Andean ÔÇîcountry, starting March 15, intended to facilitate military operations against criminal organizations with support from the ÔÇőUnited States.
The measure covers the coastal ÔÇőprovinces of Guayas, El Oro, Santo ÔüáDomingo, and Los Rios - the country's main ÔÇődrug trafficking routes - and will run from ÔÇő11 p.m. to 5:00 a.m. local time.
Joint military operations between Ecuador and the U.S. started in ÔÇőearly March. A recent mission near the ÔÇőColombian border resulted in the destruction of a drug ÔÇîtrafficking Ôüácamp, according to the Ecuadorean Ministry of Defense.
The site was operated by a group known as the Border Commandos, comprised ÔÇőof FARC ÔÇődissidents, and Ôüáhad the capacity to house 50 people, the ministry added.
Noboa ÔÇőhas made military repression against organized ÔÇőcrime Ôüáa cornerstone of his administration. As part of his hardline stance he has criticized the Ôüágovernment ÔÇőof neighboring Colombia for ÔÇőfailing to combat drug trafficking, and implemented tariffs on Colombian imports.

item.title: China urges Afghanistan, Pakistan to resolve tensions via talks, not force
item.description:
SHANGHAI, March 14 (Reuters) - Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi told his Afghan counterpart Amir ÔÇőKhan Muttaqi in a phone call ÔÇîthat disputes between Afghanistan and Pakistan should be resolved through dialogue and consultation, not ÔÇőforce, the foreign ministry said in ÔÇőa statement published on Friday.
Wang urged Ôüáboth sides to remain calm and ÔÇőexercise restraint, hold face-to-face talks as soon ÔÇőas possible, seek an immediate ceasefire and address differences through dialogue, according to the statement.
The ÔÇőfurther use of force would only ÔÇőcomplicate the situation and intensify tensions, Wang said.
The ÔÇîministry's Ôüástatement came after Pakistan on Friday bombed the fuel depot of private airline Kam Air near Afghanistan's Kandahar airport, stepping ÔÇőup the ÔÇőneighbours' worst Ôüáconflict in years, despite China's efforts to mediate.
Wang and ÔÇőMuttaqi also exchanged views on the ÔÇősituation Ôüáin Iran , the statement said.
Wang told Muttaqi, according to the statement, that Beijing is willing Ôüáto ÔÇőwork with the international ÔÇőcommunity, including Afghanistan, to bring peace to Iran.

item.title: Palestinian actor says he can't attend Oscars because of US travel ban
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - Palestinian actor Motaz Malhees said on Friday that a travel ban imposed by the Trump ÔÇőadministration is preventing him from attending this weekend's ÔÇîAcademy Awards, whose nominees include a movie in which he has a starring role.
"The Voice of Hind Rajab," a film about a ÔÇőfive-year-old Palestinian girl killed by Israeli forces in Gaza ÔÇőin 2024, has been Oscar-nominated for best international Ôüáfeature film.
Malhees, who plays the role of a call ÔÇőcenter operator attempting to help her, said he cannot ÔÇőattend the Academy Awards show, also known as the Oscars, because he has been barred from entering the United States.
"I am not allowed ÔÇőto enter the United States because of my Palestinian ÔÇőcitizenship," Malhees wrote on Instagram, adding that "it hurts" that he would not attend ÔÇîthe ÔüáOscars.
The U.S. State Department did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
In a December , opens new tab proclamation restricting entry of foreign nationals, U.S. President Donald Trump said he had "determined ÔÇőto fully restrict ÔÇőand limit Ôüáthe entry of individuals using travel documents issued or endorsed by the Palestinian Authority."
The ÔÇőproclamation restricting entry of people from some ÔÇőcountries cited Ôüásecurity reasons.
The movie was spurred by an event in which five of Rajab's family members and two ambulance workers Ôüáwho ÔÇőwent to save her were also ÔÇőkilled by Israeli fire. Israel says the incident is under review.

item.title: Eight convicted of terrorism-related charges for attack on Texas ICE facility
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - A jury convicted nine ÔÇőpeople on Friday for their involvement in a shooting at a U.S. ÔÇîImmigration and Customs Enforcement detention center in Texas last year, the Justice Department said.
Eight of the nine defendants were found guilty of providing material support to terrorists, rioting, conspiring to use and ÔÇőcarry explosives, and using explosives during a riot, local media reported.
The conviction followed ÔÇőa 12-day trial in Texas.
The defendants were charged for their roles Ôüáin a non-fatal shooting of a police officer at the immigration facility on ÔÇőJuly 4 in Alvarado, Texas.
The defendants, who authorities said were dressed in black "military-style" clothing with head and ÔÇőface coverings when they carried out the attack on the facility, have been described the Trump administration as members of the antifa movement , which was designated a domestic terrorist organization , opens new tab by President Donald Trump ÔÇőlast year.
The case was the first time federal prosecutors filed terrorism charges targeting antifa, a largely ÔÇőunstructured, far-left movement whose followers broadly aim to confront those they view as authoritarian or racist.
Following ÔÇîthe Ôüáconviction, U.S. Attorney General Pam Bondi said this will not be the last case of its kind under the Trump administration.
ICE Director Todd M. Lyons welcomed the conviction, saying the summer attack by the defendants was violent in nature and "an abhorrent way ÔÇőfor antifa terrorists to 'protest.'"
The ÔÇődefendants are Cameron ÔüáArnold, Zachary Evetts, Benjamin Song, Savanna Batten, Bradford Morris, Maricela Rueda, Elizabeth Soto, Ines Soto and Daniel Rolando Sanchez-Estrada.
Lawyers for eight ÔÇőof the nine defendants did not immediately respond to a ÔÇőrequest for Ôüácomment
"We are thankful that the jury could see through the GovernmentÔÇÖs fear mongering antifa 'ambush' narrative," said Cody Cofer, a lawyer for Arnold, the lead defendant in the case.
"We are Ôüáafraid ÔÇőthat our clientÔÇÖs guilty verdicts for Riot and ÔÇőrelated charges resulted from a compromise during deliberations or failure to consider our clientÔÇÖs individual conduct."
Cofer expressed an ÔÇőintent to appeal the case.

item.title: US says oil from strategic reserve to start reaching market next week
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - The Trump administration said on ÔÇőFriday it expects early deliveries of ÔÇîoil from the U.S. strategic petroleum reserve to reach the market by the end ÔÇőof next week as part of ÔÇőan effort to combat the energy price Ôüáspike since the start of the Iran ÔÇőwar.
The oil will be released in ÔÇőthe form of loans that companies will return with extra barrels as a premium, the U.S. ÔÇőEnergy Department said in a statement. ÔÇőThis system was meant to stabilize markets "at no cost ÔÇîto ÔüáAmerican taxpayers," it said.
The Energy Department said it had requested companies to bid for 86 million barrels of crude ÔÇőoil, which ÔÇőwill be Ôüáthe first from the release of 172 million barrels that Washington ÔÇőannounced this week.
The U.S. effort ÔÇőis Ôüápart of a broader release of 400 million barrels of oil agreed to by the Ôüá32-nation ÔÇőInternational Energy Agency on ÔÇőWednesday.

item.title: Barclays raises 2026 Brent forecast to $85 a barrel on Strait of Hormuz disruption
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Barclays on Friday raised its 2026 Brent crude forecast to $85 per barrel, ÔÇőciting ongoing supply disruptions linked to the ÔÇîIran war that have sharply reduced oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz.
The bank said ÔÇőoil flows through the Strait of ÔÇőHormuz route have fallen to a trickle Ôüáand production shut-ins in Gulf countries ÔÇőhave climbed to more than 10 million barrels ÔÇőper day.
The revised forecast assumes the situation in the Strait of Hormuz normalizes within two to ÔÇőthree weeks, Barclays said in a research ÔÇőnote.
"If however, the market internalizes that it might take ÔÇîfour Ôüáto six weeks for that, we could see 2026 Brent reprice to $100/b," the bank added.
Oil prices have risen despite efforts ÔÇőby the International Energy ÔÇőAgency Ôüáto release strategic reserves, as uncertainty around the duration of the ÔÇőconflict continues to support markets.
Brent futures ÔÇőon ÔüáFriday settled at $103.14 a barrel, up $2.68, or 2.67%.
Barclays said the path of least resistance Ôüáfor ÔÇőoil prices remains higher ÔÇőuntil there is a inflection point in the conflict.

item.title: Cyclone Vaianu lashes New Zealand's North Island, hundreds evacuated
item.description:
April 12 (Reuters) - Cyclone Vaianu made landfall in New Zealand's ÔÇőNorth Island on Sunday, triggering floods, power outages and forcing hundreds to evacuate.
The cyclone crossed the coast ÔÇînear the Maketu Peninsula, packing destructive winds exceeding 130 kph (80 mph), heavy rain and large swells, national weather provider MetService said, describing Vaianu as a "life-threatening" system.
Authorities have placed several regions under emergency declarations and issued "red" level wind warnings, which are reserved for only the most extreme weather events.
The ÔÇőcyclone was tracking towards the fringes of the North Island, sparing Auckland, the country's most populous city, from the ÔÇőworst conditions, said Emergency Management Minister Mark Mitchell. But stronger winds and swells were still expected Ôüáafter its afternoon landfall.
"ItÔÇÖs moved more to the fringes and more to the east, which means that we haven't quite ÔÇőseen the intensity that we had prepared for or that we thought we were going to get hit with. So that ÔÇőis good news," Mitchell said.
"In terms of what we're going to expect over the next 12 hours is we are going to see a bit more of intensification, as the cyclone starts to come down and get closer."
The combination of the afternoon's high tide and large swells from ÔÇőthe storm could trigger coastal inundation, Mitchell warned.
"The concerning time is really from 2 p.m. this afternoon (0800 GMT) onwards when ÔÇőwe've got high tides combined with those big swells," he said.
The cyclone has forced hundreds of residents to evacuate and knocked out electricity to ÔÇî5,000 Ôüáhomes, with power restored to roughly 2,000, he said.
Authorities in the coastal Whakatane District reported a significant amount of damage as Vaianu passed through, with mandatory evacuations carried out at 270 properties.
New Zealand Defence Force members and heavy equipment have also been deployed to assist with evacuations.
MetService said it had recorded 130 kph wind gusts in some areas, 24-hour rainfall totals of more ÔÇőthan 100 mm (4 inches) in the ÔÇőcity of Whangarei and Ôüáwave heights exceeding six metres (20 feet).
Air New Zealand (AIR.NZ) , opens new tab said in a statement that it had cancelled more than 90 turboprop flights, primarily out of regional North Island airports.
"Domestic jet and international ÔÇőservices are operating as scheduled, although there are some delays due to the weather conditions," the ÔÇőairline said.
Fire and ÔüáEmergency New Zealand said it responded to more than 100 calls for assistance relating to wind damage and surface flooding.
Conditions will ease as Vaianu works its way down the North Island before exiting on Sunday evening at Hawke's Bay, MetService said.
"Things do improve Ôüáfrom tonight ÔÇőand tomorrow, but at the moment this is still a life-threatening weather system," ÔÇősaid Heather Keats, MetService head of weather news.
Vaianu has conjured up memories of 2023's Cyclone Gabrielle, which killed 11 and displaced thousands in one of New Zealand's ÔÇőbiggest natural disasters this century.

item.title: Vineyard Wind sues GE Vernova to block it from abandoning work on wind farm
item.description:
BOSTON, April 10 (Reuters) - Vineyard Wind has filed a lawsuit seeking to block a wind turbine-supplying unit of GE Vernova (GEV.N) , opens new tab from terminating agreements and abandoning work on a $4.5 ÔÇőbillion offshore wind farm in Massachusetts on the eve of completion.
Vineyard Wind, a ÔÇîjoint venture between Spain's Iberdrola (IBE.MC) , opens new tab and Denmark's Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners, filed a lawsuit , opens new tab this week in a state court in Boston accusing the GE Vernova subsidiary of wrongly trying to terminate their contract at the ÔÇőwind project's "most vulnerable stage."
It is one of five major East Coast offshore wind ÔÇőprojects that President Donald Trump's administration halted construction near the end of Ôüá2025, citing national security.
Federal judges following lawsuits allowed construction to resume on all five.
After resuming ÔÇőconstruction, Vineyard Wind's 806-megawatt project off the coast of Martha's Vineyard in February became the ÔÇőfirst large-scale offshore wind farm in the United States to begin initial operations.
But days later, GE Renewables US LLC, which designed, manufactured and installed Vineyard Wind's wind turbine generators, sent it a notice threatening to ÔÇőend their turbine supply agreement, the lawsuit said.
Vineyard Wind, in a complaint dated Wednesday, ÔÇősaid GE did so citing a failure to pay amounts it was contractually due under their $1.3 billion ÔÇîcontract.
Vineyard Wind Ôüásaid it had exercised its right to withhold about $308 million as a result of GE's "admittedly poor performance," after one of its offshore blades in 2024 collapsed and fell into the waters off Nantucket, causing delays.
Vineyard Wind called GE's termination of the agreements improper and has asked ÔÇőa judge to ÔÇőblock GE from ceasing Ôüáwork on the wind project, which "threatens to deprive hundreds of thousands of Massachusetts ratepayers of clean energy."
A judge has scheduled an April ÔÇő16 hearing to consider whether to issue a preliminary injunction.
GE Vernova ÔÇőin a Ôüástatement said it had elected to terminate its agreements due to Vineyard Wind withholding the money. The company said it did so after completing the installation of all 62 wind turbines Ôüáat the ÔÇőwind farm.
"The company remains committed to the safety of ÔÇőthe wind farm and stands by our performance and our contractual obligations," GE Vernova said. "We will vigorously defend our ÔÇőposition through the appropriate legal process."

item.title: Iberdrola puts $1 billion solar stake sale on hold, sources say
item.description:
MADRID, April 10 (Reuters) - Iberdrola (IBE.MC) , opens new tab has put on hold for now the attempted sale of a minority stake in ÔÇőa roughly 1-gigawatt Spanish solar energy portfolio, according ÔÇîto two people familiar with the matter.
The Spanish company decided to explore the sale last year, seeking a valuation of around $1 billion for ÔÇőthe whole portfolio known as Project Julieta, one of ÔÇőthe sources said.
Yet the size of the valuation Ôüácomplicated talks, one of the people said, while the other ÔÇőpointed to waning interest from one unnamed bidder seen until ÔÇőrecently as the frontrunner.
Iberdrola said it doesn't comment on what it called market speculation.
Spain's solar power capacity has increased rapidly in recent years, ÔÇőwhile electricity demand hasn't kept pace, hitting the prices ÔÇőthat producers get for their output and the value of their generating ÔÇîassets.
For ÔüáIberdrola, asset rotation and partnerships - which include the disposal of non-core or mature assets and the sale of minority stakes - have been key elements of its strategy over recent ÔÇőyears.
The company uses ÔÇőproceeds from Ôüásuch deals to help fund new investments in core businesses, increasingly power grids. It set ÔÇőa target to raise 13.2 billion euros ÔÇőthrough 2028, 58% Ôüáof which would come from partnerships and the rest from asset rotation. Three quarters of this has already been achieved.
It Ôüáhas ÔÇőpartnered with the likes of Japan's Kansai ÔÇőElectric Power , Norway's sovereign wealth fund and Abu Dhabi's state-owned renewable energy company Masdar .

item.title: Dutch government to appeal ruling in Bonaire climate case
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, April 10 (Reuters) - The Dutch state is appealing a court ruling that it ÔÇőmust set binding emissions targets ÔÇîto reach net zero by 2050 to protect the Caribbean island of ÔÇőBonaire, a spokesperson for Climate ÔÇőMinister Stientje van Veldhoven said Ôüáon Friday.
Bonaire, a special municipality ÔÇőof the Netherlands located in the ÔÇősouthern Caribbean and until 1954 a Dutch colony, is well-known as an eco-tourism ÔÇődestination.
In its ruling, the court ÔÇőhad given the Netherlands 18 months to set ÔÇîup Ôüáa legally binding plan to reduce greenhouse gas emissions to net zero by 2050 as agreed ÔÇőin international ÔÇőtreaties.
The Ôüácase had been brought by Greenpeace on behalf ÔÇőof Bonaire residents and was ÔÇőamong Ôüáthe first to test national climate obligations set out in a Ôüálandmark ÔÇő2024 European climate ruling ÔÇőand last year's influential World Court advisory opinion .

item.title: VietnamÔÇÖs Vingroup signs pact with India's Maharashtra to explore $6.5 bln investments
item.description:
April 10 (Reuters) - Vietnam's largest conglomerate Vingroup (VIC.HM) , opens new tab on Friday signed a memorandum of understanding with India's Maharashtra's ÔÇőstate government to explore $6.5 billion in investments aimed at building a ÔÇîmulti-sector ecosystem in the financial capital.
The move will deepen Vingroup's footprint in India, where its electric vehicle unit VinFast operates a manufacturing facility in the ÔÇőstate of Tamil Nadu, and where it said it ÔÇőplans to build a $3 billion ecosystem in Telangana state.
As Ôüápart of the agreement, Vingroup will explore investments across ÔÇősectors including urban development, electric mobility, renewable energy and ÔÇőpublic infrastructure in Maharashtra.
In urban development, the company is considering integrated townships spanning roughly 1,000 hectares near Mumbai at an estimated investment ÔÇőof about $5 billion, while in electric mobility it ÔÇőplans to deploy a fleet of 60,000 electric taxis at a $1.5 billion investment.
Vingroup did not ÔÇîprovide Ôüádetails on the investment disbursement timeline, but said that the plan is expected to create tens of thousands of jobs over the next three to five ÔÇőyears.
Maharashtra is ÔÇőIndia's largest Ôüástate economy, accounting for about 14% of the country's GDP. Major domestic EV players such ÔÇőas Mahindra and Mahindra (MAHM.NS) , opens new tab and Tata Motors (TAMO.NS) , opens new tab ÔÇőhave Ôüáa strong manufacturing presence in the state.
Vingroup's operations span industrials, real estate and green energy. The company has been expanding Ôüáinto ÔÇőoverseas markets, with India identified as ÔÇőa key strategic priority.

item.title: Trump says US will blockade Strait of Hormuz after Iran peace talks fail
item.description:
MIAMI/ISLAMABAD, April 12 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump said on Sunday the U.S. Navy would start ÔÇőblockading the Strait of Hormuz, raising the stakes after marathon talks with Iran failed to reach a deal to end the war , jeopardising a fragile two-week ÔÇîceasefire.
Trump also said in a post on Truth Social that the U.S. would take action against every vessel in international waters that had paid a toll to Iran, and begin destroying mines that he said the Iranians had dropped in the strait, a choke point for about 20% of global energy supplies that Iran has blocked.
"No one who pays an illegal toll will have safe passage on the high seas," Trump wrote, adding: "Any Iranian ÔÇőwho fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be BLOWN TO HELL!"
Iran's Revolutionary Guards responded with a statement warning that military vessels approaching the strait will ÔÇőbe considered a ceasefire breach and dealt with harshly and decisively, underlining the risk of a dangerous escalation.
Iran's Parliamentary Speaker Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf, Ôüáwho led his country's delegation to the talks along with Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi, said Trump's new threats would have no effect on Iran.
"If you fight, we will fight, and if ÔÇőyou come forward with logic, we will deal with logic," he said, in comments carried by state media.
Six weeks of fighting has killed thousands , roiled the global economy and sent oil prices soaring as ÔÇőIran prevented traffic through the strait.
In an interview with Fox News after his post about the strait, Trump said that he believed Iran would continue to negotiate and called the weekend discussions "very friendly".
"I do believe they're going to come to the table on this, because nobody can be so stupid as to say, ÔÇśWe want nuclear weapons,ÔÇÖ and they have no cards," Trump told Fox News from his golf course near Miami, ÔÇőFlorida.
Trump also said that NATO allies, whom he has criticised for failing to back the war he launched along with Israel on February 28, wanted to help with the operation in ÔÇőthe strait.
There was no immediate comment from Washington's allies.
The weekend talks in Islamabad, which followed the announcement of a ceasefire last Tuesday, were the first direct U.S.-Iranian meeting in more than a decade and the ÔÇîhighest-level discussions Ôüásince the 1979 Islamic Revolution.
"The bad news is that we have not reached an agreement, and I think that's bad news for Iran much more than it's bad news for the United States of America," said Vice President JD Vance, who headed the U.S. delegation.
A U.S. official said Iran had rejected Washington's call for an end to all uranium enrichment, the dismantling of all major enrichment facilities and the transfer of highly enriched uranium. The two sides also failed to reach agreement on the U.S. demand that Iran cease funding for Hamas, Hezbollah and the Houthis as ÔÇőwell as fully open the strait, the official ÔÇőadded.
Qalibaf blamed the U.S. for not winning Tehran's Ôüátrust, despite his team offering "forward-looking initiatives". Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian, who discussed the talks in a call with Russian President Vladimir Putin, said Tehran wanted "a balanced and fair agreement".
"If the United States returns to the framework of international law, reaching an agreement is not far off," he ÔÇőtold Putin, Iranian state media reported.
Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency said "excessive" U.S. demands had hindered reaching a deal. Other Iranian ÔÇőmedia said there was Ôüáagreement on a number of issues, but the strait and Iran's nuclear programme were the main sticking points.
Despite the stalemate, three supertankers fully laden with oil passed through the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday, shipping data showed, in what appeared to be the first vessels to exit the Gulf since the ceasefire deal.
Israel has continued bombing Tehran-backed Hezbollah militants in Lebanon, insisting - along with Washington - that that conflict Ôüáwas not part ÔÇőof the Iran-U.S. ceasefire. Iran says the fighting in Lebanon must stop.
The Israeli military said it struck Hezbollah rocket ÔÇőlaunchers overnight into Sunday and black smoke could be seen rising in the southern suburbs of the Lebanese capital Beirut.
And in Israeli villages near the border, air raid sirens sounded, warning of incoming rocket fire from Lebanon.

item.title: Gaza aid flotilla aims to break Israeli blockade
item.description:
MADRID, April 12 (Reuters) - A second flotilla carrying humanitarian aid to Palestinians ÔÇîin Gaza was due to set sail on Sunday from the Spanish port of Barcelona, aiming to try to break the Israeli blockade.
Thirty-nine boats were due to leave the Mediterranean port city, a spokesperson for the flotilla ÔÇősaid, and more vessels also laden with medical aid and other supplies are expected ÔÇőto join along the route towards Palestine .
Rough seas mean the flotilla will sail to Ôüáanother port then head out to international waters later in the week, Thiago Avila, a ÔÇőmember of the flotilla's organising committee, told a press conference on Sunday.
The Israeli military halted a previous flotilla ÔÇőassembled by the same organisation last October as the boats attempted to reach blockaded Gaza, arresting Swedish activist Greta Thunberg and more than 450 other participants.
Israel, which controls all access to the Gaza Strip, ÔÇődenies withholding supplies for its more than 2 million residents. Yet Palestinians and international aid ÔÇőbodies say supplies reaching the territory are still insufficient, despite a ceasefire reached in October which included guarantees of ÔÇîincreased Ôüáaid.
Liam Cunningham, an actor who starred in the Game of Thrones television series who is supporting the flotilla but not taking part, told Reuters: "Every kilogram of aid that is on these ships is a failure because all these people on these ships giving up their time to help ÔÇőtheir fellow human beings are ÔÇődoing what their Ôüágovernments are legally obliged to do."
The World Health Organization has said that even during armed conflicts, states are obligated under international humanitarian law to ÔÇőensure that people are able to reach medical care in safety.
ÔÇťThis is ÔÇőa mission Ôüáthat aims to open a humanitarian corridor so the aid delivery organisations can arrive,ÔÇŁ Saif Abukeshak, a Palestinian activist and member of the flotillaÔÇÖs organising committee, told Reuters.
Swiss and Spanish activists on last year's Ôüáflotilla said ÔÇőthey were subjected to inhumane conditions during their detention ÔÇőby Israeli forces - an allegation that was rejected by an Israeli foreign ministry spokesperson.

item.title: Hungary's Orban concedes landmark defeat to centre-right opposition
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 12 (Reuters) - Hungary's veteran nationalist leader Viktor Orban conceded defeat on Sunday after a ÔÇőlandslide election victory by the upstart opposition Tisza party, in a setback for his allies in Russia and U.S. ÔÇîPresident Donald Trump's White House.
Results based on 46% of votes counted showed the centre-right, pro-EU Tisza party of Peter Magyar , opens new tab winning 135 seats - or a crucial two-thirds majority - in the 199-member parliament, ahead of Orban's Fidesz party.
"The election results are not final yet, but the situation is understandable and clear," Orban said at the Fidesz campaign offices. "The election result is painful ÔÇőfor us, but clear."
Pollsters predicted a record voter turnout, with Hungarian television showing long queues outside some voting stations in ÔÇőBudapest. Data at 1630 GMT, half an hour before polls were due to close, showed 77.8% of voters Ôüácasting their ballots, up from 67.8% four years earlier.
If the final results confirm the early readings, an end to Orban's period in ÔÇőgovernment after 16 years in power would have significant implications not only for Hungary, but for the European Union, Ukraine and beyond.
It would ÔÇőlikely spell an end to Hungary's adversarial role inside the EU, possibly opening the way for a 90 billion euro ($105 billion) loan to war-battered Ukraine blocked by Orban.
Defeat for Orban could also mean the eventual release of EU funds to Hungary that the bloc had suspended due to what Brussels said was ÔÇőOrban's erosion of democratic standards.
Orban's exit would also deprive Russian President Vladimir Putin of his main ally in the EU and send shockwaves ÔÇőthrough Western right-wing circles, including the White House.
In Hungary, a Tisza victory could open the way for reforms that the party says would aim to ÔÇîcombat corruption Ôüáand restore the independence of the judiciary and other institutions.
However, the extent of such reforms will depend on whether Tisza can secure the two-thirds constitutional majority it would need to reverse much of Orban's legacy.
Orban, a eurosceptic, carved out a model of an "illiberal democracy" seen as a blueprint by Trump's Make America Great Again (MAGA) movement and its admirers in Europe.
But many Hungarians have grown increasingly weary ÔÇőof Orban, 62, after three years ÔÇőof economic stagnation and soaring Ôüáliving costs as well as reports of oligarchs close to the government amassing more wealth.
Tisza's leader Magyar appears to have successfully tapped into this frustration.
Casting his vote for Tisza in the Hungarian capital, Mihaly ÔÇőBacsi, 27, said the country needed change.
"We need an improvement in public mood, there is too ÔÇőmuch tension in many Ôüáareas and the current government only fuels these sentiments," he said.
Another voter, who gave her name as Zsuzsa, said she wanted continuity.
"I would really like if all the results that have been achieved in recent years remain - and I am terribly afraid of the war," she said, referring Ôüáto the ÔÇőconflict raging in Ukraine, Hungary's eastern neighbour.
Orban sought to cast Sunday's election as ÔÇőa choice between "war and peace" . During campaigning, the government blanketed the country with signs warning that Magyar would drag Hungary into Russia's war with Ukraine, something he strongly denies.

item.title: Ben-Gvir visits Al-Aqsa Mosque compound
item.description:
JERUSALEM, April 12 (Reuters) - Israel's far-right police minister Itamar Ben-Gvir visited the flashpoint Al-Aqsa Mosque compound in Jerusalem on Sunday, saying ÔÇőhe was seeking greater access for Jewish worshippers and drawing condemnation ÔÇîfrom Jordan and the Palestinians.
The compound in Jerusalem's walled Old City is one of the most sensitive sites in the Middle East . Known to Jews as Temple Mount, it is ÔÇőthe most sacred site in Judaism and is Islam's third-holiest site.
Under ÔÇőa delicate, decades-old arrangement with Muslim authorities, it is administered by Ôüáa Jordanian religious foundation and Jews can visit but may not pray ÔÇőthere.
Suggestions that Israel would alter the rules have sparked outrage among Muslims and ÔÇőignited violence in the past.
"Today, I feel like the owner here," National Security Minister Ben-Gvir said in a video filmed at the site and distributed by his office. "There is still more to ÔÇődo, more to improve. I keep pushing the Prime Minister (Benjamin Netanyahu) to do ÔÇőmore and more ÔÇö we must keep rising higher and higher."
A statement from the Jordanian foreign ÔÇîministry Ôüásaid it considered Ben-Gvir's visit to be a violation of the status quo agreement at the site and "a desecration of its sanctity, a condemnable escalation and an unacceptable provocation".
The office of Palestinian president, Mahmoud Abbas, said such actions could ÔÇőfurther destabilize the ÔÇőregion.
Ben-Gvir's spokesman said Ôüáthe minister was seeking greater access and prayer permits for Jewish visitors. He also said that Ben-Gvir had prayed at ÔÇőthe site.
There was no immediate comment from Netanyahu's office. ÔÇőPrevious such Ôüávisits and statements by Ben-Gvir have prompted Netanyahu announcements saying that there is no change in Israel's policy of keeping the status quo.
Muslim, Christian and Jewish sites, Ôüáincluding ÔÇőAl-Aqsa had been largely closed , opens new tab to the public during the ÔÇőIran war . There was no immediate sign of unrest on Sunday after Ben-Gvir's visit.

item.title: Nigerian airstrike hits market, 200 feared dead in northeast Yobe state
item.description:
MAIDUGURI, Nigeria, April 12 (Reuters) - At least 200 people are feared dead after Nigerian military jets struck a village market while pursuing Islamist militants in the northeast of the country on Saturday night, a councillor for the area and residents said on Sunday.
The Nigerian ÔÇőAir Force, responding to reports of civilian casualties, said in a statement it had activated ÔÇőits Civilian Harm Accident and Investigation Cell "to immediately proceed to the location on a Ôüáfact-finding mission on the allegation".
Earlier, the Air Force said it had killed Boko Haram militants in ÔÇőthe Jilli axis in Borno state.
The government of neighbouring Yobe state later said in a statement that an ÔÇőair strike on the area had been conducted near a market where shoppers and vendors had gathered.
"Some people from Geidam LGA (local government area) bordering Gubio LGA in Borno state who went to the Jilli weekly market were affected," said ÔÇőBrigadier General Dahiru Abdulsalam, military adviser to the Yobe state government.
He gave no further details.
The strike occurred in a village in Yobe on the border with Borno, the heartland of a long-running insurgency ÔÇîthat has Ôüákilled thousands of people and displaced millions more.
Lawan Zanna Nur Geidam, the councillor and traditional head of Fuchimeram ward in Yobe's Geidam district, told Reuters that those injured were being taken to hospitals in Yobe and Borno.
"It's a very devastating incident at Jilli Market. As I'm speaking to you, over 200 ÔÇőpeople have lost their lives ÔÇőfrom the air strike Ôüáat the market," he said in a telephone interview.
Three other residents and an official from an international humanitarian agency confirmed the strike and the estimated ÔÇődeath toll.
The Yobe State Emergency Management Agency said it had received preliminary reports ÔÇőof an Ôüáincident at Jilli Market "which reportedly resulted in casualties affecting some marketers" and activated emergency response.
Ahmed Ali, a 43-year-old resident who sells medical consumables at the market, said he had been injured in a blast.
"I became so scared Ôüáand attempted ÔÇőto run away, but a friend dragged me and we ÔÇőall lay on the ground," he said from hospital.

item.title: South Africa coalition party elects Cape Town mayor as leader
item.description:
JOHANNESBURG, April 12 (Reuters) - South Africa's Democratic Alliance (DA) ÔÇîelected Cape Town Mayor Geordin Hill-Lewis as its leader on Sunday, as the ruling coalition's second-biggest party sought to capitalise on discontent to expand its power base in local polls this year.
The 39-year-old was widely considered the favourite ÔÇőto succeed Agriculture Minister John Steenhuisen, who has led the pro-business party since 2019 and ÔÇőis stepping down.
"If we work hard, we can win more towns and cities Ôüáthan we've ever won before," Hill-Lewis said in his acceptance speech at a party conference near Johannesburg, while laying ÔÇőout broad ambitions for the next national election in 2029.
"I am not satisfied being a junior partner ÔÇőin a coalition government. Our ambition must be to lead the national government," he said.
Hill-Lewis has given few details about his plans but is not expected to depart significantly from the policies of his predecessor, who took the ÔÇőDA into a coalition with the African National Congress (ANC) in 2024 while continuing to fight it ÔÇőon issues such as national health insurance and affirmative action, which the DA opposes.
Africa's most industrialised country must hold ÔÇîlocal elections Ôüábefore November, and President Cyril Ramaphosa's ANC is expected to see its share of the vote slip again.
Local polls have traditionally gone worse for the ANC than the national vote, as voters furious at failures to deliver basic services like water and road repairs punish the party that has been ÔÇőin power since apartheid ÔÇőended in 1994.
