The DA holds 22% of seats in the lower house of parliament, second to the ANC with 41%, and ÔÇőhas kept roughly the same vote share for the last decade.
The DA ÔÇőhas a reputation Ôüáfor protecting the interests of South Africa's white minority - which it denies - and has not had a non-white leader since Mmusi Maimane resigned in 2019. Some analysts see that as a limiting factor for growth Ôüáin a country ÔÇőthat is more than 90% non-white.
"It would've been so much ÔÇőeasier for them to appeal to a broader constituency if there was a dynamic person ... of another colour," author and political ÔÇőscientist Susan Booysen said.

item.title: Three killed as armed men attack Pakistan Coast Guards in Arabian Sea
item.description:
QUETTA, Pakistan, April 12 (Reuters) - Separatist insurgents killed three Pakistan Coast Guard personnel on Sunday in the first-ever attack on one of the maritime authority's patrol boats operating ÔÇőin the Arabian Sea, security officials said.
Intelligence and police officials ÔÇősaid the boat was on a routine patrol in Ôüáa coastal area close to the Pakistan-Iran border when the militants ÔÇőopened fire and killed three on board.
The incident adds to security ÔÇőchallenges in Balochistan, a flashpoint of insurgency where armed groups have frequently targeted security forces and infrastructure.
The banned separatist group Balochistan Liberation Army (BLA) claimed responsibility for ÔÇőthe attack. "Following the operations on land, the action in maritime ÔÇőboundaries marks a new development in the BLAÔÇÖs military strategy," the BLA said ÔÇîin Ôüáa statement.
Authorities have launched an investigation and security has been heightened in the area, said the intelligence and police sources.
Pakistan carried out airstrikes in February that sparked a week-long conflict ÔÇőwith Afghanistan. Islamabad blames ÔÇőKabul for Ôüáproviding safe haven to militant groups including the BLA to plan and coordinate their attacks. The Taliban ÔÇőgovernment has denied the accusation.
Gwadar, an important district ÔÇőwith a Ôüástrategic deep-sea port that is a key node in China's flagship "Belt and Road" infrastructure initiative, has seen repeated attacks on security forces Ôüáand installations ÔÇőby Baloch separatist militants.
Authorities did not ÔÇőrespond to requests for comment about the incident.

item.title: Saudi Arabia summons Iraq envoy over drone threats from Iraqi territory
item.description:
April 12 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia said on ÔÇőSunday it ÔÇîhad summoned Iraq's ambassador over ÔÇőwhat ÔÇőit described as threats Ôüátargeting the ÔÇőkingdom and ÔÇőother Gulf states from drones launched ÔÇőfrom Iraqi ÔÇőterritory.
The Saudi foreign ministry ÔÇîsaid Ôüáin a statement that it had "emphasised ÔÇőthe ÔÇőimportance Ôüáof Iraq dealing ÔÇőresponsibly with ÔÇőthese Ôüáthreats and attacks".

item.title: Australia and US boost support for critical minerals with $3.5 billion
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 12 (Reuters) - Australia and the U.S. have committed more than A$5 billion ($3.5 billion) to back a range of critical mineral projects, nearly double the amount pledged when the two countries struck ÔÇőa co-operation agreement six months ago, Canberra said on Sunday.
The funding seeks to ÔÇîsupport Australian ventures to develop and refine metals vital to industries including defence, advanced manufacturing and the energy transition, a market that has long been dominated by China.
By doing so, they would support the re-industrialisation of America's ÔÇőhigh-tech manufacturing base, while helping to "counter China's export dominance and ensure Western supply-chain resilience," ÔÇőthe two nations said when they reached a framework deal last October .
In that Ôüádeal, Australia and the U.S. agreed to each provide at least $1 billion in investments towards ÔÇőan $8.5 billion pipeline of priority critical mineral projects across the two countries over the subsequent six months.
Australia ÔÇőhas a vast supply of critical minerals such as rare earths, but China has mastered the technically difficult and environmentally fraught refining process.
"Australia and the U.S. are delivering on the commitments made in the White House, with priority ÔÇőprojects in Australia that support production of rare earths and critical minerals," Resources Minister Madeleine ÔÇőKing said in a statement.
"Australia is taking a global lead to diversify crucial supply chains for critical minerals and ÔÇîrare Ôüáearths, which are vital to support economic and national security for Australia and our trading partners."
The A$5 billion in funding to Australian critical mineral projects will be carried out through the Export Finance Australia (EFA) and U.S. Export-Import Bank (EXIM) agencies.
Among the largest investments is for a rare ÔÇőearths refinery project owned by Tronox ÔÇőHoldings (TROX.N) , opens new tab where EFA Ôüáand EXIM have issued letters of support and interest worth a combined A$849 million. Tronox, with operations in Western Australia and the U.S., would tap its ÔÇőexisting mining and processing capabilities to produce mixed rare earth carbonate containing ÔÇőboth light Ôüáand heavy rare earth elements, King said.
The agencies also committed up to A$1 billion in combined backing for Ardea Resources' (ARL.AX) , opens new tab Kalgoorlie Nickel Project in Western Australia.
Other projects supported under the framework include Alcoa's (AA.N) , opens new tab Gallium Recovery ÔüáProject and ÔÇőArafura's (ARU.AX) , opens new tab Nolans Rare Earths Project along with graphite, magnesium ÔÇőand tungsten ventures.
Additional projects for minerals including vanadium and scandium have also received indications of support, King said.
($1 = 1.4164 Australian dollars)
(1 ÔÇőAustralian dollar = $0.7060)

item.title: Israel approves appointment of new intelligence agency chief
item.description:
JERUSALEM, April 12 (Reuters) - Israel approved the appointment of Roman ÔÇőGofman as the new Mossad ÔÇîintelligence agency head, a statement from the Israeli prime ÔÇőminister's office said on ÔÇőSunday.
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin ÔüáNetanyahu announced his intention ÔÇőto nominate Gofman in December ÔÇő2025 and a committee for senior appointments approved the decision on ÔÇőSunday. Gofman will ÔÇőstart his role in June 2026, ÔÇîreplacing Ôüáthe agency's current director David Barnea.
Gofman, 49, is a seasoned military general who ÔÇőrushed ÔÇőto Israel's Ôüásouthern border to rescue Israeli communities ÔÇőunder fire from a ÔÇőHamas-led Ôüáinfiltration on October 7, 2023. The incoming director Ôüácurrently ÔÇőserves as the ÔÇőprime minister's military secretary.

item.title: Iran's new supreme leader has severe and disfiguring wounds, sources say
item.description:
DUBAI, April 11 (Reuters) - Iran's new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei is still recovering from severe facial and leg injuries suffered in the airstrike that killed his father at the beginning of the war, three people close to his inner circle told Reuters.
Khamenei's face was disfigured in the attack on the supreme leader's compound in central Tehran and he suffered a significant injury to one or both legs, all three sources said.
The 56-year-old is nonetheless recovering from his wounds and remains mentally ÔÇősharp, according to the people, who requested anonymity to discuss sensitive matters. He is taking part in meetings with senior officials via audio conferencing and is engaged in decision-making on major issues including the war and negotiations ÔÇîwith Washington, two of them said.
The question of whether Khamenei's health allows him to run state affairs comes during Iran's moment of gravest peril for decades, with high-stakes peace talks with the United States opening in the Pakistani capital Islamabad on Saturday.
The accounts of the people close to Khamenei's inner circle provide the most detailed description of the leader's condition for weeks. Reuters couldn't independently verify their descriptions.
Khamenei's whereabouts, condition and ability to rule still largely remain a mystery to the public, with no photo, video or audio recording of him published since the air attack and his subsequent appointment as his ÔÇőfather's replacement on March 8.
Iran's United Nations mission did not respond to Reuters questions about the extent of Khamenei's injuries or the reason he has not yet appeared in any images or recordings.
Khamenei was wounded on February 28, the first ÔÇőday of the war launched by the U.S. and Israel, in the attack that killed his father and predecessor Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who had ruled since 1989. Mojtaba Khamenei's wife, brother-in-law Ôüáand sister-in-law were among other members of his family killed in the strike.
There has been no official Iranian statement on the extent of Khamenei's injuries. However, a newsreader on state television described him as a "janbaz", a term used for those badly wounded in war, ÔÇőafter he was named supreme leader.
The accounts of Khamenei's injuries tally with a statement made by U.S. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth on March 13 when he said that Khamenei was "wounded and likely disfigured".
A source familiar with U.S. intelligence assessments told Reuters that Khamenei was ÔÇőbelieved to have lost a leg.
The CIA declined to comment on Khamenei's condition. The Israeli prime minister's office didn't respond to questions.
Alex Vatanka, senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, said that, regardless of the severity of his injuries, it was unlikely the new and inexperienced leader would be able to command the overarching power wielded by his father. While he is seen to represent continuity, it could take years for him to build up the same level of automatic authority, Vatanka added.
"Mojtaba will be one voice but it will not be the decisive one," he said. "He needs to prove himself ÔÇőas the credible, powerful, overriding voice. The regime as a whole has to make a decision in terms of where they are going to go."
One of the people close to Khamenei's circle said images of the supreme leader could be expected to be ÔÇőreleased within one or two months and that he might even appear in public then, although all three sources stressed he would only emerge when his health and the security situation allowed.
In Iran's theocratic system of rule, ultimate power is meant ÔÇîto be wielded Ôüáby the supreme leader, a venerable Shi'ite Muslim cleric appointed by an assembly of 88 ayatollahs. The leader oversees the elected president while directly commanding parallel institutions including the Revolutionary Guards, a powerful political and military force.
Iran's first supreme leader, Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, enjoyed unquestioned authority as the charismatic leader of the revolution and the most learned cleric of his day.
His successor, Ali Khamenei, was a less revered cleric but had served as Iran's president. He spent decades cementing his authority after his appointment in 1989, partly through promoting the power of the Revolutionary Guards.
His son Mojtaba does not command absolute power in the same way, senior Iranian sources have previously told Reuters. The Revolutionary Guards, who helped steer him into the top job after his father's assassination, have emerged as the dominant voice on strategic ÔÇődecisions during the war. Iran's U.N. mission didn't respond to questions ÔÇőabout the power wielded by the Guards and the Ôüánew supreme leader.
As an influential figure in his father's office, Khamenei had previously spent years involved in exercising power at the top levels of the Islamic Republic, officials and insiders have said, building ties with senior Guards figures.
While he is widely seen as likely to continue his father's hardline approach due to his links to the Guards, we don't know much about his world view, said Vatanka ÔÇőat the Middle East Institute.
Khamenei's first communication with Iranians as supreme leader came on March 12, saying in a written statement read out by a television news presenter that the Strait ÔÇőof Hormuz should stay closed and Ôüáwarning regional countries to shut U.S. bases.
His office has since issued a few other brief written statements from him, including on March 20 when he welcomed in the Persian new year, which he named the "year of resistance". Public statements of policy on Iran's war stance, its approach towards diplomacy, neighbours, ceasefire negotiations and domestic unrest, have been made by other senior officials.
Khamenei's absence is widely discussed on Iranian social media and in messaging app groups, when the country's patchy internet allows, with conspiracy theories widespread about his condition and Ôüáwho is running the ÔÇőcountry.
One popular meme circulating online is a picture of an empty chair under a spotlight with the slogan "Where is Mojtaba?"
However, some government supporters, including a ÔÇősenior member of the Basij militia, a volunteer paramilitary group run by the Revolutionary Guards, said that it was important for Khamenei to keep a low profile, given the threat posed by waves of U.S. and Israeli airstrikes that have already wiped out much of the country's leadership.
A lower-ranking Basij member agreed.
"Why should he ÔÇőappear in public? To become a target for these criminals?" Mohammad Hosseini, from the city of Qom, said in a text message.

item.title: Some of Swalwell's fellow Democrats urge him to quit Congress amid sexual assault allegations
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 12 (Reuters) - U.S. Representative Eric Swalwell, a front-runner candidate for California governor, faced mounting calls to resign from Congress on Sunday as some of his fellow Democrats urged him to leave following accusations of ÔÇősexual assault.
In separate TV interviews on Sunday morning talk shows, Democratic Representatives Pramila Jayapal, Ro Khanna ÔÇîand Eugene Vindman each said Swalwell should quit Congress.
Their comments came two days after The San Francisco Chronicle and CNN reported that a woman who previously worked in Swalwell's district office accused him of two nonconsensual sexual encounters.
CNN also reported that three other women ÔÇőmade sexual misconduct allegations against Swalwell, who represents a California district.
Swalwell has denied the accusations as "absolutely false" and ÔÇővowed to fight them.
"What he did is sick and disgusting," Khanna told "Fox News Sunday" while Ôüácalling for law enforcement and House ethics investigations of Swalwell.
The Manhattan district attorneyÔÇÖs office on Saturday confirmed that it is ÔÇőinvestigating the sexual assault allegations.
Leading Democrats have previously called on Swalwell to drop out of the California governor's race. ÔÇőAs pressure grows on him to also resign from Congress, some lawmakers have said they would back a motion to expel him from the House of Representatives if he does not leave on his own.
Representative Byron Donalds, a Republican from Florida appearing on NBC's "Meet ÔÇőthe Press", and Jayapal, a representative from Washington also appearing on "Meet the Press", each said on Sunday they would ÔÇővote to expel Swalwell from Congress if such a vote came up.
In a social media post on Saturday, Representative Anna Paulina Luna, ÔÇîa ÔüáFlorida Republican, wrote that she planned to file a motion to expel Swalwell from Congress. She told Fox News that she would file the disciplinary motion next week.
Another Democrat, Representative Sheila Cherfilus-McCormick , a Florida representative, also faces a potential expulsion vote after a House panel recently concluded that she violated ethics rules. A grand jury indicted her in November after ÔÇőshe was accused of stealing ÔÇőpandemic relief funds and Ôüádirecting that money to her 2021 congressional campaign.
Cherfilus-McCormick's office did not immediately return a request for comment. She has previously denied wrongdoing.
While Luna and other Republicans have called for ÔÇőexpelling Swalwell and Cherfilus-McCormick, Democrats have been calling for an expulsion of Representative Tony ÔÇőGonzales, a Texas ÔüáRepublican being investigated by the House over sexual misconduct allegations.
Gonzales' office did not immediately return a request for comment. Last month, he said he would not seek re-election later this year.
Donalds told "Meet the Press" that he wants to see Swalwell Ôüáand Gonzales ÔÇőleave. "As far as I'm concerned both gentlemen need to go home," ÔÇőDonalds said.
While calling on Swalwell to resign, Khanna told Fox that he also believes "there are other members (of Congress) and Republican members who should resign ÔÇőas well," such as Gonzales.

item.title: Hungary's opposition Tisza party leads last Median poll done before Sunday election
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 12 (Reuters) - Hungary's centre-right Tisza party ÔÇőwas projected to ÔÇîwin 135 mandates in Hungary's 199-seat ÔÇőparliament, ahead ÔÇőof Prime Minister Viktor ÔüáOrban's Fidesz ÔÇőparty, in a ÔÇőpoll carried out in the days before Sunday's ÔÇőelection by ÔÇőpollster Median.
According to the ÔÇîsurvey Ôüáannounced on RTL Klub television and conducted between ÔÇőApril 7 ÔÇőand Ôüá11, the far-right Mi ÔÇőHazank (Our Homeland) ÔÇőparty Ôüáwould not pass the 5% threshold Ôüáto ÔÇőget ÔÇőinto parliament.

item.title: Iran's Guards will view military vessels approaching strait as ceasefire breach
item.description:
CAIRO, April 12 (Reuters) - Iran's Revolutionary Guards said ÔÇőon Sunday that ÔÇîany military vessels attempting to approach the ÔÇőStrait of Hormuz ÔÇőwill be considered a Ôüáviolation of ÔÇőthe two-week U.S. ceasefire ÔÇőand be dealt with harshly and decisively.
The strait ÔÇőis under ÔÇőthe control and 'smart management' of ÔÇîIran's ÔüáNavy, the Guards said in a statement reported by ÔÇőIranian ÔÇőstate Ôüámedia, adding it is "open for ÔÇőthe safe passage ÔÇőof Ôüánon-military vessels in accordance with specific Ôüáregulations".

item.title: Trump says it will take a little while for US to blockade the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: MIAMI, Florida, April 12 (Reuters) - Implementing a ÔÇînewly announced U.S. ÔÇőblockade ÔÇőof the Strait Ôüáof ÔÇőHormuz will ÔÇőtake a little while, ÔÇőPresident ÔÇőDonald Trump said ÔÇîin Ôüáan interview with Fox ÔÇőNews' "Sunday ÔÇőMorning ÔüáFutures with ÔÇőMaria ÔÇőBartiromo".

item.title: Peruvians vote in crowded presidential race as runoff looms
item.description:
LIMA, April 12 (Reuters) - Peruvians headed ÔÇőto the polls on Sunday to vote for a new president and members of Congress, casting ballots in a first-round field of ÔÇîmore than 30 presidential candidates after years of political turmoil that have eroded confidence in institutions and left voters deeply disillusioned.
With no clear frontrunner and all major candidates polling well below the 50% needed to win outright, a June 7 runoff appears likely. That could prolong uncertainty in the world's third-largest copper producer at a time of rising crime ÔÇőand intensifying competition for influence between the United States and China.
Voting stations opened at 7 a.m. local time (1200 GMT), with about 27 million people ÔÇőeligible to vote.
Early in the day, voters in parts of Lima complained that several polling stations had yet to open.
Margarita ÔüáSandoval, 35, said she had waited in line for two hours without being able to enter her ballot station in Chorrillos, a southern district of ÔÇőthe capital.
ÔÇťI have to work and I can't vote," Sandoval said. "These elections are a disaster."
Others said the crowded race had made the decision difficult. ÔÇťI only decided ÔÇőwho to vote for about a week ago,ÔÇŁ said 33-year-old shop assistant Benjamin Alcantara.
Since 2018, Peru has cycled through eight presidents, fueling skepticism that any new administration will last a full five-year term following a dizzying turnover driven by impeachments, corruption scandals and weak governing coalitions that have paralyzed decision-making.
ÔÇťPeople really despise the current Congress," said Martin ÔÇőCassinelli at the Atlantic Council. "They recognize them as responsible for the political chaos we've had over the last ten years,ÔÇŁ he added.
Political distrust has fueled ÔÇőa crowded field spanning the ideological spectrum, including seasoned politicians, a far-right businessman and a television comedian.
Among the best known is conservative Keiko Fujimori , making her fourth presidential bid after reaching ÔÇîthe runoff Ôüáin all three previous races.
Educated in the U.S. and leader of the powerful Popular Force party in Congress, Fujimori has framed herself as a guarantor of order and economic stability, appealing to voters alarmed by surging violent crime. Her candidacy remains polarizing, however, due to her family legacy and past legal troubles .
Ricardo Belmont, a former Lima mayor running for the center-left Civic Works Party, has shot up into second place after a late rise in support. He is followed by ÔÇőpopular comedian Carlos Alvarez, who is ÔÇőcampaigning on a tough-on-crime platform. Both Ôüáare considered outsiders who have gained traction by tapping into a broader anti-establishment mood, according to analysts.
On the right, former Lima Mayor Rafael Lopez Aliaga, a wealthy businessman with socially conservative views, has campaigned on an ultra-conservative platform ÔÇőbut has seen support fluctuate.
Public insecurity has emerged as the dominant theme of the campaign. Homicides and extortion have ÔÇőclimbed in recent years, Ôüádriven in part by drug trafficking and illegal mining. Most leading candidates have proposed expanding the role of the armed forces in internal security.
The election also carries geopolitical implications. Peru's deepening economic relationship with China -- now its largest trading partner and a major investor in mining and infrastructure -- has raised concerns in Washington , which has Ôüástepped up ÔÇődiplomatic and security engagement ahead of the vote.
Whoever advances to the runoff will face a fractured ÔÇőCongress and newly reinstated Senate, which may complicate efforts to pass legislation and raise the risk of renewed impeachment battles.
Polls close at 5 p.m. (2200 GMT), with preliminary results from the country's election ÔÇőmonitor expected soon after.

item.title: China offers incentives to Taiwan following opposition leader's visit
item.description:
BEIJING, April 12 (Reuters) - China on Sunday unveiled 10 new incentive measures for Taiwan, including easing tourist curbs, allowing in "healthy" television ÔÇődramas and facilitating food sales, following a visit by the island's opposition ÔÇîleader.
The move comes at the end of a trip to China by Cheng Li-wun, chairwoman of the Kuomintang (KMT), Taiwan's largest opposition party. She met Chinese President Xi Jinping and talked about ÔÇőthe need for peace and reconciliation.
The 10 measures, unveiled by the official ÔÇőXinhua news agency, "explore" the establishment of a regular communication mechanism between Ôüáthe KMT and China's Communist Party, the full resumption of flights between the ÔÇőtwo sides and permission for individuals from Shanghai and Fujian province to visit Taiwan.
A ÔÇőmechanism will be established to ease inspection standards for food and fishery products, but that has to be on the political foundation of "opposing Taiwan independence," Xinhua said.
Taiwanese TV dramas, documentaries and ÔÇőanimation will be allowed to be shown as long as they have "correct orientation, ÔÇőhealthy content, and high production quality," it added.
Taiwan's Mainland Affairs Council, which is in charge of ÔÇîthe Ôüáisland's China policy, said in a statement that Beijing's so-called "unilateral concessions" are merely poisoned pills packaged as "generous gift packages."
The Taiwanese government supports healthy and orderly cross-strait exchanges, but they should not be subject to political preconditions or objectives, it said.
In a ÔÇőstatement, the KMT welcomed ÔÇőChina's announcement, saying Ôüáit was a "gift" to the people of Taiwan.
China refuses to speak to Taiwan President Lai Ching-te, saying he is a "separatist." He ÔÇőrejects Beijing's sovereignty claims over the democratically governed island.
China and ÔÇőTaiwan have Ôüáblamed each other for the lack of a resumption of large-scale Chinese tourism to the island since the end of the COVID-19 pandemic.
Taiwan has also previously complained about ÔüáChinese ÔÇőrestrictions on imports of certain agricultural and aquatic products, ÔÇősaying China has in some cases used unjustified excuses to stop the spread of pests and diseases.

item.title: Irish police clear blockades as Dublin acts to soften fuel price blow
item.description:
DUBLIN, April 12 (Reuters) - Irish police on Sunday cleared tractors and trucks that had ÔÇîbeen blocking traffic and blockading oil infrastructure across the country during a wave of protests against surging fuel prices.
In a bid to ease the discontent, the government announced ÔÇő500 million euros ($586 million) worth of spending increases and tax ÔÇőcuts to soften the impact on consumers and businesses.
Protesters, angered Ôüáby a more than 20% rise in diesel prices since the outbreak ÔÇőof the Iran war , had used vehicles to block an oil refinery, two ports, ÔÇőa fuel terminal and Irish roads.
The protests caused major transport disruption in Dublin and left about a third of Ireland's petrol stations without fuel, creating what Finance Minister ÔÇőSimon Harris described as a "very dangerous moment" for the country.
Police removed vehicles ÔÇőblocking access to Ireland's only oil refinery on Saturday and cleared a blockade of ÔÇîGalway Port, Ôüáa major fuel depot, and removed roadblocks in Dublin on Sunday.
The government has refused to negotiate with the protesters, who included farmers, drivers and contractors, who complained that an earlier 250 million euro package to temporarily cut ÔÇőtaxes on petrol ÔÇőand diesel did Ôüánot go far enough.
But after talks with agricultural and transport industry groups, Dublin announced measures including a 10 ÔÇőcent per litre reduction for diesel and petrol and ÔÇőa delay Ôüáin an increase in a carbon tax. A fuel subsidy support scheme will also be introduced for farming and fisheries, the government said.
A poll in Ôüáthe ÔÇőSunday Independent newspaper showed that 56% of ÔÇővoters surveyed supported the protesters, but that most supporters of the two governing parties opposed them.
($1 = ÔÇő0.8533 euros)

item.title: Gas prices might stay high through midterms, Trump says
item.description:
MIAMI, April 12 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump said on Sunday that the price of oil and gasoline ÔÇőmay remain high through November's midterm elections, a rare acknowledgement of the potential political fallout from his decision to attack Iran ÔÇîsix weeks ago.
"It could be, or the same, or maybe a little bit higher, but it should be around the same," Trump, who is in Miami for the weekend, told Fox News' "Sunday Morning Futures With Maria Bartiromo" when asked whether the cost of oil and gas would be lower by the fall.
The average price for regular gas at ÔÇőU.S. service stations has exceeded $4 per gallon for most of April, according to data from GasBuddy. Trump's comments on Sunday came after ÔÇőweeks of asserting that the spike in prices is a short-term phenomenon, though his top advisers are cognizant of Ôüáthe war's economic impacts, officials have said.
Earlier on Sunday, Trump announced on social media that the U.S. Navy would blockade the Strait of Hormuz and intercept ÔÇőany ship that paid a crossing fee to Iran, after marathon talks between the U.S. and Iran in Pakistan over the weekend did not yield ÔÇőa peace deal.
"No one who pays an illegal toll will have safe passage on the high seas," he wrote on Truth Social.
Any U.S. blockade is likely to add more uncertainty to the eventual resolution of the conflict, which is currently subject to a tenuous two-week ceasefire. The new tactic is in response to Iran's own closure of the ÔÇőstrait's critical shipping lanes, which has caused global oil prices to skyrocket about 50%.
The war began on February 28, when ÔÇőthe U.S. launched a joint bombing campaign with Israel against Iran. The scope quickly expanded as Iran and its allies attacked nearby countries, while Israel targeted Hezbollah with ÔÇîmassive strikes Ôüáin Lebanon.
The war has buffeted global financial markets and caused thousands of civilian deaths, mostly in Iran and Lebanon.
Trump's political standing at home has suffered, with polls showing the war is unpopular among most Americans, who are frustrated by rising gasoline prices.
The president's approval rating has hit the lowest levels of his second term in office, raising concern among Republicans that his party is poised to lose control of Congress in the midterm elections. A Democratic majority ÔÇőin either chamber could launch investigations ÔÇőinto the Trump administration while Ôüáblocking much of his legislative agenda.
U.S. Senator Mark Warner of Virginia, the ranking Democrat on the Senate Intelligence Committee, questioned the strategy behind Trump's planned blockade.
"I don't understand how blockading the strait is going to somehow push the ÔÇőIranians into opening it," he told CNN's "State of the Union" on Sunday.
In a separate appearance on CBS's "Face the ÔÇőNation," Warner said the Ôüáblockade would not undermine Iranian control of the waterway.
"The Iranians have hundreds of speedboats where they can still mine the strait or put bombs against tankers in closing the strait," he said. "How is that going to ever bring down gas prices?"
Although Trump has repeatedly said that the war would be over soon, ÔüáRepublican U.S. Senator ÔÇőRon Johnson of Wisconsin told ABC News' "This Week" on Sunday that achieving U.S. aims ÔÇőin Iran "could take a long time."
"It's going to be a long-term project," said Johnson, who was not asked about Trump's proposed blockade. "I never thought this would be easy."

item.title: Australia PM Albanese to visit Brunei, Malaysia to shore up fuel supply
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 12 (Reuters) - Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese will visit Brunei and Malaysia this week, continuing diplomatic efforts to secure fuel supplies ÔÇőfrom Asian allies after disruptions caused by the Middle East ÔÇîconflict.
Albanese will travel to Bandar Seri Begawan and Kuala Lumpur from April 14 to 17, his office said in a statement on Sunday.
He is scheduled to ÔÇőmeet Brunei's ruler Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah and Malaysian Prime ÔÇőMinister Anwar Ibrahim.
Foreign Minister Penny Wong would also meet her Ôüácounterparts in Brunei and Malaysia, the statement said.
"Engaging with critical ÔÇőregional partners such as Brunei Darussalam and Malaysia will help ensure Australia's ÔÇőenergy supply remains secure during times of uncertainty," Albanese said.
"We are taking every step to reinforce relationships and engage with key partners to keep our fuel ÔÇősupply flowing."
The trip comes as Australia leans on its Asian allies ÔÇőto maintain fuel supplies amid disruptions caused by Iran's blockade of the Strait ÔÇîof ÔüáHormuz, through which about one-fifth of the world's oil and gas transited before the conflict.
Australia, which imports about 80% of its fuel, has experienced localised shortages and soaring prices since the strait's closure.
Malaysia is ÔÇőAustralia's third-largest source ÔÇőof refined fuel Ôüáand supplies 10% of its fertiliser-grade urea imports, while Australia supplies 95% of Malaysia's imported natural ÔÇőgas.
Brunei supplies 9% of Australia's diesel imports and 11% ÔÇőof its Ôüáfertiliser-grade urea imports.
The government has held talks on fuel with Brunei, China, Indonesia, Japan, Malaysia and South Korea, among others.
Albanese also travelled to ÔüáSingapore ÔÇőlast week and met Prime Minister Lawrence Wong ÔÇőon Friday, with the two leaders agreeing to closer cooperation to ensure energy security.

item.title: Morning Bid: Back from the brink?
item.description:
April 10 - Everything Mike Dolan and the ROI team are excited to read, watch and listen to over the weekend.
From the Editor
Hello Morning Bid readers!
Markets returned from the long holiday weekend anything but rested, as U.S. President Donald Trump threatened civilizational destruction if Iran didnÔÇÖt reopen the Strait of Hormuz before his deadline on Tuesday. But instead of hellfire came news of a two-week ceasefire, which was initially cheered by markets. The question now is whether that optimism will last as investors consider the wide gap ÔÇőbetween Washington and Tehran and the damage to energy supply chains caused by this conflict.
No one knows what will happen next, of course, but it seems likely that conditions will be less benign than many traders currently seem to believe .
The eleventh-hour ÔÇîceasefire announcement late on Tuesday sent both Brent and West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude prices tumbling from upwards of $110/bbl to under $100 a barrel .
This, in turn, allowed global stocks and bonds to stage a relief rally on Wednesday , as U.S. equity indexes finished sharply higher , Asian markets surged , and EuropeÔÇÖs STOXX 600 logged its best day in over 4 years . Government bonds rallied as well, while the dollar weakened against developed-market peers on hopes of a lasting end to the conflict.
However, cracks quickly emerged in the Pakistan-brokered ceasefire, pushing crude prices back towards $100/bbl on Thursday and stalling the global equity relief rally .
The deal appeared increasingly fragile after Israel on Wednesday staged its largest attack on Hezbollah during this conflict, with Washington and Tehran ÔÇődisagreeing about whether strikes on the Lebanese militant group were meant to be included in the ceasefire agreement. Additionally, Iran attacked Saudi Arabia's East-West Pipeline ÔÇô its only crude oil export route since the war began ÔÇô shortly after the ceasefire was agreed .
For markets, the biggest ÔÇőquestion is what is ÔÇô or is not ÔÇô happening in the Strait of Hormuz, the critical artery for the global energy system. Trump had said the ceasefire is contingent on the narrow waterwayÔÇÖs reopening, yet the Ôüástrait remains effectively closed .
For Tehran, reopening appears to involve charging tolls for transiting ships, a demand that would likely result in higher global energy prices for years to come . This has all led Trump to write on Truth Social: ÔÇťThat is not the agreement we have!ÔÇŁ
Further peace talks are still ÔÇőset to go ahead in Islamabad this weekend, despite Iran suggesting they would be ÔÇťunreasonableÔÇŁ in light of the Israeli strikes in Lebanon and Trump issuing further military threats . Israel has said it would open separate talks with the Lebanese government aimed at ending the war there and disarming Hezbollah.
Markets appear somewhat optimistic ÔÇőabout the likelihood that fighting in Iran will not resume soon, with U.S. stock indexes advancing on Thursday and Asian equities rising on Friday , putting them on track for their best week in over three years. Wall Street was flat before the bell on Friday.
However, even if the parties agree on a lasting ceasefire, this will likely only bring partial relief to global energy markets. As a reminder, oil prices are still well above their levels before the conflict broke out, with Brent and WTI up around 34% and 48%, respectively, since the war began on February 28.
What's more, production capacity in the region is still depressed, with Saudi Arabia's oil output ÔÇődown by 600,000 barrels per day and throughput on its East-West Pipeline by about 700,000 bpd, according to government sources .
While exporters in the Middle East could soon begin shipping oil trapped in the Gulf if the Strait of Hormuz is reopened, hopes of a rapid return to normal ÔÇőflows are almost certainly misplaced . This is particularly worrisome for Asia, where physical energy markets are likely to remain under stress for months even in the most optimistic scenarios.
All of this leaves the Federal Reserve in an increasingly unenviable position as higher global energy prices threaten to exacerbate already elevated inflation ÔÇô and potentially unanchor ÔÇîinflation expectations ÔÇô as ÔüáAmericans eye higher prices at the pump .
On that front, this weekÔÇÖs macro releases included U.S. services PMIs that showed rising input costs ÔÇô an early signal of renewed inflationary pressure ÔÇô alongside PCE inflation figures that showed an expected uptick in U.S. price increases in February, before the war started.
At a global level, IMF chief Kristalina Georgieva warned again on Monday that ÔÇťall roadsÔÇŁ now point toward higher prices and slower growth , a message unlikely to comfort policymakers wary of the spectre of stagflation.
Federal Reserve minutes released on Wednesday suggested more officials are leaning toward a rate hike in response to inflation risks, though most continue to see rate cuts as the base case later this year, with scope for deeper easing should the conflict begin to weigh on U.S. employment.
Both policymakers and investors will thus be eagerly awaiting todayÔÇÖs release of consumer price index (CPI) data for March, as investors shift their attention away from the ÔÇőbattlefield ÔÇô at least for now.
For more data-driven insights on markets and ÔÇőcommodities, check out Reuters Open Interest . You can learn:
This weekend, we're reading...
RON ÔÇőBOUSSO, ROI Energy Columnist: This Foreign Affairs article , opens new tab offers a sharp analysis of ChinaÔÇÖs view on the Iran war and the growing risk that a volatile U.S. poses to BeijingÔÇÖs economic and diplomatic interests.
ANDY ÔÇőHOME, ROI Metals Columnist: A new International Energy ÔüáAgency report , opens new tab is essential reading on rare earths that goes beyond the basics to spotlight an often-overlooked driver: explosive demand for rare earth permanent magnets.
JAMIE MCGEEVER, ROI Markets Columnist: "Shattered Assumptions And The Energy Quandary" , opens new tab is a deep dive by the always interesting Louis-Vincent Gave of Gavekal Research into the place global energy holds in the minds of investors - the risks and rewards, the upsides and downsides.
We're listening to...
CLYDE RUSSELL, ROI Asia Commodities and Energy Columnist: I appeared on Gulf Intelligence's Daily Energy Markets Podcast , opens new tab for a great discussion on the Iran war ceasefire, with heaps of scepticism over whether it will hold and result Ôüáin a lasting ÔÇődeal. We also explored why the real crisis lies in refined fuels, not crude prices.
And we're watching...
ANNA SZYMANSKI, ROI EditorÔÇĹinÔÇĹCharge: Why hasnÔÇÖt the Iran conflict triggered a classic flight ÔÇőto safety? I explored this question on the Reuters Econ World Podcast , opens new tab with host Carmel Crimmins and my ROI colleague Mike Dolan, discussing the recent moves in gold, Treasuries and haven currencies. We ask whether it may be time to rethink the entire concept of a safe haven.
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? ÔÇőSign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the authors. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: What the Chicago Bears relocation fight can teach us about economics
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 10 (Reuters) - The battle to house the Chicago BearsÔÇÖ new stadium is pitting Midwestern U.S. states against each other. Taxpayers are apt to be the losers - and not just because sports stadiums rarely live up to their economic billing.
After more than half a century, the National Football LeagueÔÇÖs Chicago Bears are looking for a site to build ÔÇőa new stadium. The question of where has become a political football, with two states falling over themselves to pay for it.
The odds-on favorite destination was initially Arlington Heights, Illinois, a ÔÇîsuburb located northwest of Chicago. That cause was advanced last month by a committee of the Illinois General AssemblyÔÇÖs House of Representatives which approved a bill that would bestow tax breaks on qualified ÔÇťmegaprojects.ÔÇŁ
Meanwhile, the other leading contender, Hammond, Indiana, won support from its own state legislature in the form of a $1 billion public funding package. Indiana Governor Mike Braun also signed a bill to create a Northwest Indiana stadium authority.
Players within the federal government have also gotten into the game. Democratic U.S. Representative Greg Casar of Texas and ÔÇőIndependent Senator Bernie Sanders of Vermont have introduced a Home Team Act that would bar any sports team from relocating without first offering itself for sale to local buyers.
Casar contended that such a ÔÇőrestriction was imperative, saying that under current conditions, ÔÇťBillionaire owners pit taxpayers against one another and then extort the government for billions of dollars.ÔÇŁ
Leaving aside his demonization of the Ôüásuperrich, the congressman has a point about taxpayers often getting soaked to pay for athletic stadiums.
It is hardly news that tax-financed stadiums have a proven record of failing to deliver the economic benefits promised to voters.
My ÔÇőbook ÔÇťUnwarranted Intrusions: The Case Against Government Intervention in the Marketplace,ÔÇŁ published 20 years ago, summarized research showing that the presence of a stadium merely diverts spending from other entertainment and creates mostly low-paying jobs.
Proponents of tax subsidies for ÔÇősports franchises often counter that having a major-league team enables a city to attract industrial facilities and corporate headquarters.
In reality, when considering a city as a potential site, corporations are primarily concerned with other factors important to their workforce, such as the quality of an areaÔÇÖs schools.
Another point to remember is that sports teams havenÔÇÖt always needed enticements to move to ÔÇô or stay in ÔÇô a city. Professional baseball's first enclosed ballpark opened in Brooklyn in 1862, yet it took another 91 years before a local government first ÔÇőused taxpayer dollars to lure a team, when Milwaukee did so to attract the Boston Braves in 1953.
GovernmentsÔÇÖ rush to splash tax breaks on sports teams has only amplified the problem over time, a reminder of the ÔÇőpower ÔÇô and danger ÔÇô of economic incentives.
If tax breaks for stadiums do not represent a worthwhile public investment in economic development, what kinds of government expenditures actually can move the needle?
The Information Technology and Innovation Foundation (ITIF), a well-respected nonpartisan ÔÇîU.S. nonprofit concerned Ôüáwith public policy, publishes estimates of economic multipliers, measures of the proportional increase in gross domestic product (GDP) that results from private investment or government spending.
According to ITIF estimates, in an advanced economy like the U.S., an investment of 1% of GDP in infrastructure increases economic output by approximately 1.5%. A comparable investment in education or human capital produces an increase of about 3%.
These investments take time to mature, of course. Infrastructure spendingÔÇÖs peak impact is typically reached after five to ten years, with the full benefits of education and human capital investment taking 20 years or more to show up, the ITIF notes.
A third category of productive public investment is scientific research and development. ITIF ÔÇőestimates that putting 1% of GDP into R&D raises ÔÇőannual growth by more than 1%. Peak impact Ôüáis reached in seven or more years.
The International Monetary Fund largely backs up these conclusions. Its October 2025 Fiscal Monitor found that in advanced economies, a reallocation of 1% of GDP from low-impact government consumption to infrastructure can raise output by approximately 1.5% over 25 years, increasing to 3%ÔÇô6% when redirected to human capital investments, namely, education ÔÇőor health.
Unfortunately, economic growth payoffs that occur over years or decades are less visible to voters than the splashy arrival of an NFL ÔÇőteam.
Legislators with reelection concerns are thus Ôüálikely to focus more on that type of short-term ÔÇťwinÔÇŁ rather than investments with more potential to boost growth over the long haul. The future prosperity of constituents not yet old enough to vote is typically not a politicianÔÇÖs chief concern.
Given these dynamics, legislators are likely to continue chasing sports franchises, even if all this does is transfer money from taxpayers to wealthy team owners. Not only could that result in wasted tax dollars but also in wasted potential from the more economically Ôüáviable investments that ÔÇőarenÔÇÖt being made.
(The views expressed here are those of Marty Fridson , the publisher of Income Securities Investor. He is a past governor of the ÔÇőCFA Institute, consultant to the Federal Reserve Board of Governors, and Special Assistant to the Director for Deferred Compensation, Office of Management and the Budget, The City of New York.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab ÔÇőand X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Trading Day: Ceasefire sends stocks higher
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, April 9 (Reuters) - U.S. stocks rallied on Thursday, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq clocking their seventh daily gain, as investors shrugged off a rise in oil prices and pinned their hopes on the fragile U.S.-Iran ceasefire extending to Israel and Lebanon too.
In my column today I look beyond the market euphoria uncorked ÔÇőby Tuesday's announcement of the ceasefire, and outline why the economic, policy and geopolitical backdrop is still pretty sobering for investors.
If you ÔÇîhave more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* Stagflation nation
Figures on Friday are expected to show annual headline U.S. CPI inflation last month was 3.3%, up sharply from 2.4% in February. That would be the highest in nearly two years. Core goods prices are running hot, and with oil 65% more expensive than it was a year ago, ÔÇőinflation hitting 4% is more likely than the Fed's 2% target.
Some of the early March survey data suggest the economy remains pretty resilient. But incoming hard ÔÇődata isn't that robust, and the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow Q1 GDP estimate is now down to 1.3%. Plus, Q4 GDP was revised down on Thursday to 0.5%. Not great.
* AI disruption ÔÇîfears
U.S. software Ôüástocks tumbled on Thursday after Anthropic held back the wide release of a powerful AI model over concerns it could expose hidden cybersecurity vulnerabilities. Software stocks are down 25% this year, against a 4% decline for the wider tech sector.
Meanwhile, IMF Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva said Fund research shows AI could boost productivity by up to 0.8%, but also affect 60% of all jobs in developed economies. That is sobering, to put it mildly.
* Income incoming
The Q1 U.S. reporting season kicks into gear ÔÇőnext week, and the outlook is pretty ÔÇőrosy. The LSEG I/B/E/S consensus is Ôüáfor a 14.4% rise in earnings, again led by tech - income is expected to rise 46%, and if consensus forecasts are met, that would mean tech accounting for 75% of the overall rise in dollar income.
A lot of optimism is ÔÇőbuilt into these forecasts. The Nasdaq is back above its pre-war level, and on Thursday clocked its seventh straight ÔÇőrise, a run not Ôüáseen since August 2024. But remember, tech's valuation premium over the broader market has collapsed to a 7-year low. So maybe the bullishness is justified?
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want ÔÇőto receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , ÔÇőis committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Why investors can still believe in mean reversion
item.description:
LONDON, April 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Over the past century, U.S. small-cap stocks have outperformed the market by more than 2 percentage points a year. Companies that are cheap relative to book value have done slightly better since 1926. The latest Global Investment Returns Yearbook , opens new tab from UBS remains equivocal as to why these so-called investment factors have historically outperformed. Perhaps, suggest authors Elroy Dimson, Paul Marsh and Mike Staunton, it was due to ÔÇťtransient anomalies,ÔÇŁ that ÔÇőcease to work once theyÔÇÖve been spotted. These are not reassuring grounds for investing oneÔÇÖs wealth.
Veteran investor Jeremy Grantham, the co-founder of Boston asset manager GMO, has a more intellectually satisfying explanation for the value and ÔÇîsmall-cap ÔÇťanomaliesÔÇŁ. In his recently published memoir, ÔÇť The Making of a Permabear: The Perils of Long-term Investing in a Short-term World , opens new tab ,ÔÇŁ which I helped compile, Grantham explains how in the 1970s he was perhaps the first investor to construct a data series for small-cap stocks that went back to 1926. In the era before computers were widely used in the investment business, he built his index painfully by hand. It was a labour of love.
Grantham made some interesting discoveries. First, small caps had beaten the market since 1926 ÔÇô a finding that finance academics only later latched onto. Second, ÔÇťtheir relative performance [against the market] moved in cycles, ÔÇőebbing and flowing.ÔÇŁ After periods when small caps had badly lagged the market, they tended to catch up: ÔÇťThe pendulum swung back and forth. Bursts of superior performance werenÔÇÖt sustained indefinitely.ÔÇŁ Third, small caps were riskier. Grantham noticed that they were ÔÇőalmost wiped out during the Great Depression, but over the following decades slowly regained their lost ground. Given that smaller companies are more vulnerable to economic dislocations, it made sense for their Ôüástocks to trade at a discount to the broader market which is dominated by larger more resilient firms.
Grantham also found that most of the extra returns from small caps came from reconstituting the index. After a firm did well and its market capitalisation ÔÇőgrew, it departed the series. And when a formerly large-cap firm fell on hard times, it joined the small-cap index. Rebalancing the portfolio worked thanks to mean reversion, he says. ThatÔÇÖs to say, on balance, the losersÔÇÖ fortunes improved over time while the winnersÔÇÖ advantage ÔÇőeroded.
ÔÇťIn a moderately mean-reverting world, any strategy of selling winners and buying losers should win,ÔÇŁ Grantham writes. ÔÇťThe fact that a companyÔÇÖs depressed profitability reverts to its average level seemed to me just the fortunes of war. Say a firm is operating in an industry that turns down. Its managers scramble around looking for ways to stop the rotÔÇŽ they slash costs and eventually get something rightÔÇŽ and as profits recover the stock price does too. Conversely, when a firm is superprofitable its management becomes overconfident, it invests too much, costs rise faster than sales and margins decline.ÔÇŁ
There was no special ÔÇťsmall-cap effectÔÇŁ, ÔÇősays Grantham, just a process whereby the returns of winners and losers returned to the average, harvested through reshuffling the portfolio. Mean reversion also explains why value stocks have historically outperformed. Grantham realised that buying stocks that were cheap relative to their book value ÔÇőonly worked because on average corporate profitability reverts to the mean. ÔÇťI looked at the data in the Value Line hard copy and saw with my naked eye that in most cases when things get very very profitable, they mean revert downwards. And after profits get wiped out, the ÔÇîreverse happens. The Ôüáreason this happened is straightforward. Capital moves towards profits: excess returns attract competition and bad returns drive capital away.ÔÇŁ
Later in his career, Grantham implemented his belief in mean reversion at the asset-class level. It turned out to be a fraught experience. In the late 1990s, a great stock market bubble formed in the United States: growth stocks were extremely overvalued, while value stocks had never looked cheaper. Grantham bet his firm that bubble would break. Many clients lost patience and left.
At the time, he was told heÔÇÖd lost the plot. One client demanded he explain the firmÔÇÖs relative underperformance, ÔÇťbut for GodÔÇÖs sake donÔÇÖt give us any more of this nonsense about reversion to the mean.ÔÇŁ Grantham held on and was vindicated. The bubble burst, growth companies collapsed and value stocks came roaring back. The experience imparted ÔÇőa painful lesson: ÔÇťMean reversion is a useful and hitherto accurate ÔÇőrule but still a painful oneÔÇŽ,ÔÇŁ he wrote in one Ôüáof his quarterly letters, ÔÇťyou will arrive back on trend eventually, but not necessarily with the same clients you left with.ÔÇŁ
After the financial crisis of 2008, the value factor went into one of its periodic doldrums. Over the past decade, the valuation of the US stock market has entered bubble territory - and stayed there. Corporate profits ÔÇô formerly the most reliably mean-reverting data in finance, according to Grantham ÔÇô ÔÇőhave remained far above average. Mean reversion appears to have lost its gravitational powers.
Grantham frets that American corporations have become more less competitive. U.S. companies exert more political power, he says. ÔÇőThey have used much of their profits Ôüáto buy back shares rather than invest. A handful of large technology firms became super-profitable but did not attract competition because of their insuperable competitive strengths. ÔÇťIf exceptional profit margins do not attract assets and increased competition, then the capitalistic system is broken,ÔÇŁ writes Grantham.
Elsewhere, mean reversion is alive and kicking. Value stocks have outperformed in most markets since late 2020, according to the UBS Yearbook. Last year was a banner period for the value factor almost everywhere, except in the United States. How long can this American exceptionalism last? The current artificial intelligence frenzy Ôüácould signal a change. ÔÇőFormerly asset-light giants like Alphabet (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and Meta Platforms (META.O) , opens new tab are now investing heavily in data centres. AI breakthroughs could lead to more direct competition between large technology ÔÇőfirms. ItÔÇÖs not clear that their competitive moats will remain intact.
ÔÇťHigher capex and more competition could drive mean reversion. We could be entering a new phase,ÔÇŁ says Grantham. Even if that doesnÔÇÖt happen, he believes that AI will accelerate the political pushback against large, powerful and obscenely profitable companies. One way or another, mean ÔÇőreversion is coming for the U.S. stock market. As ever, though, the timing remains uncertain!
Follow @Breakingviews , opens new tab on X.

item.title: AI struggles to model for angry Main Street mobs
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Artificial intelligence is getting stumped by angry and anxious humans. Facing the fastest-rising electricity bills in ÔÇőthe country, MaineÔÇÖs House of Representatives advanced , opens new tab a proposal to ban new data centers larger than ÔÇî20 megawatts until late 2027. Local governments and utility commissions have mustered even stronger resistance. The opposition leaves Silicon Valley and Wall Street to rethink their ambitious plans.
The backlash is broadening and accelerating. States introduced more than 300 bills spanning , opens new tab ÔÇőmoratoriums, tax-incentive rollbacks and ratepayer protections in the first six weeks of 2026, according to lobbying ÔÇőfirm MultiState. Community-based pushback blocked or stalled some $160 billion of projects last year, ÔüáThe New York Times reported , opens new tab last month, citing Data Center Watch, a research outfit backed by AI safety ÔÇőstartup 10a Labs.
When regulatory pressure squeezes developers, construction shifts to more receptive places like Texas, Indiana and Ohio. ÔÇőThe flow creates new bottlenecks. The Lone Star StateÔÇÖs grid operator received 233 gigawatts of power requests , opens new tab as of December, nearly quadruple the previous year's amount.
The risk to ratepayers is quietly metastasizing. Utilities are booking guaranteed regulated returns on buildouts that ÔÇőmay prove speculative. Georgia PowerÔÇÖs plan , opens new tab to add 9.9 GW carries an estimated $50 billion to $60 billion cost to ÔÇőcustomers over decades. If demand disappoints , opens new tab , households face rate hikes well into the next decade.
Responses so far have failed to ÔÇîneutralize concerns. ÔüáAmazon.com (AMZN.O) , opens new tab , Google, Meta Platforms (META.O) , opens new tab and Microsoft (MSFT.O) , opens new tab signed the White House's ratepayer protection pledge , opens new tab in March, but the commitments are voluntary and carry no penalties. Utility AEP Ohio forced data centers to cover 85% of claimed capacity, weeding out speculative projects and shrinking , opens new tab its waitlist by 57% to 13 GW. Expanding this rule nationwide risks ÔÇőconstraining investment and driving up ÔÇőpower prices.
Individual policy spats Ôüámask the bigger issue: AI resistance is entrenching itself across every level of government. Only one in four Americans say they think data centers deliver meaningful , opens new tab local ÔÇőjobs, while half are more concerned than excited , opens new tab about AI's increased use in ÔÇődaily life, according Ôüáto Pew Research Center. Lawmakers at every level now have a convenient boogeyman. AI is meant to help solve diffuse and multi-faceted problems, but the technology isn't equipped to handle this emotionally charged one.
Follow Sebastian Pellejero Ôüáon LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: 7-Eleven IPO delay plays into Couche-TardÔÇÖs hands
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Seven & i's (3382.T) , opens new tab decision to push back the listing of 7-Eleven was almost certainly the right call. But time is running out for the ÔÇőJapanese corner store empire to improve operations at its North American gas-and-goods subsidiaryÔÇöa key plank in CEO Stephen ÔÇîDacus' bid to revive growth and improve shareholder returns. The case is building for spurned Canadian suitor Alimentation Couche-Tard (ATD.TO) , opens new tab to take another run.
The planned spin-off of the $34 billion group's largest foreign business, spanning 13,000 shops across the U.S. and Canada, was whipped up last year to help fund 2 trillion yen ($12.6 ÔÇőbillion) in share buybacks through 2030. That was part of a broader drive led by Dacus to reassure shareholders ÔÇődeprived of a juicy payout following the collapse of Couche-Tard's $46 billion buyout offer. After announcing the IPO Ôüádelay on Thursday, Seven & i insisted this programme will continue apace. That, however, didn't stop investors from dumping the stock, which ÔÇőhas now fallen 6% since the Nikkei published a report on the delay just before Thursday's market close.
This reflects an uncomfortable reality ÔÇőfor the Seven & i boss who, per the Financial Times , opens new tab , said that any listing would be ÔÇťdriven strictly by valueÔÇŁ, adding the delay was necessitated by slow progress in turning the American business and market conditions. Dacus took the helm last year with a mandate to convince investors that their best bet ÔÇőto secure sustained returns lay with management in Tokyo.
Progress has been dismal so far. 7-Eleven's same-store merchandise sales fell in three out ÔÇőof the last four quarters, with those in the December quarter down 0.6% year-on-year. To further complicate matters, the American chain also relies on ÔÇîpetrol Ôüásales, leaving it exposed to oil price shocks like those now roiling global markets. Since Couche-Tard walked away in July, Seven & i has delivered measly shareholder returns of 4.5%, versus 34.6% for Japan's benchmark Topix (.TOPX) , opens new tab index.
Such struggles are music to the ears of Couche-Tard chair Alain Bouchard, who dropped his 2,600 yen per share takeover bid due to what the Circle K owner described as a ÔÇőlack of engagement from Seven & i. ÔÇőShares in the latter Ôüánow trade at a 20% discount to that offer price, leaving a wide gap for the Canadian rival to step through if it wants another crack at JapanÔÇÖs largest-ever foreign acquisition.
Follow Hudson Lockett ÔÇőon Bluesky , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: Discount Tesla could be a painful reality check
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 9 (Reuters Breakingviews) - ItÔÇÖs the irony of Elon MuskÔÇÖs life that his ambitions make battery-powered cars seem pedestrian. Whether itÔÇÖs rockets, robots, chips or chatbots, ÔÇőthe Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab tycoon has had his hands full of late. The distraction is evident in the $1.3 trillion ÔÇîelectric-vehicle pioneer's results. Both sales and analystsÔÇÖ estimates of future earnings are sliding alarmingly. That wasnÔÇÖt supposed to matter, as MuskÔÇÖs driverless revolution would render anything with a steering wheel obsolete. A new effort to build a cheap sports-utility vehicle, reported by Reuters on Thursday, sits awkwardly with that narrative. ÔÇőThe discount vehicle could be an expensive reality check for Tesla investors.
Musk has promised to build a super-cheap ÔÇőcar in the past. After much talk of a brand-new manufacturing process that would slash costs in Ôüáhalf, though, the plan seemed to disappear, replaced in part by cut-down versions of the best-selling Model 3 and Model ÔÇőY. Instead, Tesla trumpeted the bizarre, no-steering-wheel Cybercab , with Musk decreeing that a ÔÇťregular $25,000 modelÔÇŁ would be not only ÔÇťpointlessÔÇŁ but ÔÇťcompletely at odds with ÔÇőwhat we believe.ÔÇŁ The company's future, he implied, lay in building vehicles tuned to specifically serve as robotic chauffeurs.
This is a crucial thesis for TeslaÔÇÖs extraordinary valuation. Even as analysts' estimates of future profit have declined precipitously, according to Visible Alpha, the company's shares have traded at ÔÇőan ever-higher multiple of that shrinking figure. First-quarter car sales of under 337,000 fell short of Wall Street expectations, and led ÔÇőto the widest gap between vehicles manufactured and delivered since the pandemic-ravaged first quarter of 2020. ItÔÇÖs also a far cry from the ÔÇînear-500,000 vehicles Ôüáthat Tesla sold in the final three months of 2024.
Producing a cheaper conventional ride may shore up TeslaÔÇÖs slipping market share as battery-powered rivals from China and Europe gain ground. The company has held expenses admirably steady even as it ramped up production of the dud Cybertruck: the average car cost it about $34,000 after adjusting for leases last quarter. If old efficiency ÔÇőtargets are still realistic, then ÔÇőa cheap vehicle is within Ôüáreach. Pricey gasoline due to conflict in the Middle East gives drivers an extra incentive to make the switch . The new car will surely incorporate existing self-driving software, allowing Tesla to cling ÔÇőto the promise of future autonomy.
The problem is that the Middle East conflict is also ÔÇőscrambling supply chains, Ôüájust as the pandemic aftermath pushed Tesla's costs above $41,000 per vehicle in 2022. Even worse, though, is the tacit admission that Musk cannot single-handedly redefine what a car is. Regulations and customer acceptance take time. The expansion of robotaxis like Waymo is halting. ÔüáThe driverless ÔÇőrevolution might be delayed. TeslaÔÇÖs valuation isnÔÇÖt yet pricing that in.
Follow Jonathan ÔÇőGuilford on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Orban ousted after 16 years as Hungarians flock to pro-EU rival
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 12 (Reuters) - Prime Minister Viktor Orban, a key opponent of European Union efforts to help Ukraine fend off Russia's invasion, lost power after 16 years on Sunday as Hungarians voted in record numbers for ÔÇőa pro-EU course spearheaded by centre-right rival Peter Magyar.
Orban, 62, had won endorsements from U.S. President Donald Trump and some top European conservatives, but early results showed ÔÇîhis nationalist Fidesz party losing to Peter Magyar 's pro-EU Tisza party due to Hungary's economic stagnation .
A fiery anti-Communist youth leader during the Cold War, Orban, the European Union's longest-serving leader, is a patriotic hero to supporters, but critics at home and abroad have accused him of taking Hungary on an authoritarian path.
Born in 1963 in a village west of Budapest, Orban trained as a lawyer, briefly studied political philosophy at Oxford, and even played semi-professional soccer before becoming ÔÇőprime minister for the first time in 1998 aged just 35.
Hungary joined NATO on Orban's watch but he lost power in 2002. After eight years in opposition, he won ÔÇőa landslide victory in 2010, enabling him to rewrite Hungary's constitution and pass major laws aimed at creating an "illiberal democracy".
His consolidation of executive power, new Ôüácurbs on NGO activities and media freedoms, and a weakening of judicial independence led to clashes with the European Union over democratic standards, culminating in a decision to suspend billions of euros in ÔÇőfunding for Hungary.
But all that came crashing down for Orban on Sunday night as preliminary results put Magyar on course for a parliamentary supermajority, enabling his centre-right party to unwind all of Orban's ÔÇőcontroversial reforms.
"What tonight's election result means for the fate of our country and nation and what the deeper or higher meaning of all this is, remains unclear. We do not know it yet. Time will tell," Orban told supporters, conceding defeat.
"But however it has turned out, we will keep serving our country and the Hungarian nation from opposition."
During Europe's 2015 migrant crisis, Orban cast himself as the guardian of Hungary's national identity and ÔÇőChristian heritage, refusing to accept EU quotas for taking in asylum seekers, mostly Muslims from the Middle East and beyond. His government has gradually taken steps to erode LGBTQ+ rights .
His hard line on ÔÇőimmigration and his efforts to revive Hungary's declining birth rate earned him praise from other conservative leaders, including Trump.
Orban - who also racked up sweeping election wins in 2014, 2018 and 2022 - had secured endorsements this time ÔÇîfrom Italy's ÔüáPrime Minister Giorgia Meloni, Marine Le Pen of France's National Rally, and Alice Weidel of the Alternative for Germany.
Trump has also endorsed Orban, saying U.S.-Hungary relations have reached "new heights" owing to their leadership after years of conflict under Democratic administrations in Washington.
Orban has maintained close ties with Russia, a key energy supplier , and China, whose firms are building major EV and battery plants in the landlocked central European country.
He has sought to frame the election as a choice between "war or peace" , suggesting Tisza wants to drag Hungary into the war raging in neighbouring Ukraine , which it strongly denies.
"For peace, Fidesz is the ÔÇősafe choice," Orban said on the campaign trail in ÔÇőFebruary. He has clashed frequently with Ukraine's ÔüáPresident Volodymyr Zelenskiy and infuriated Hungary's EU partners by blocking a 90 billion euro aid package for Kyiv.
But surveys showed Hungarian voters were more concerned with domestic issues such as healthcare and the economy, which has stagnated for the past three years.
Hungary experienced the EU's worst inflationary surge following Russia's ÔÇőFebruary 2022 invasion of Ukraine, which lifted food prices close to EU average levels, while Hungarian wages are still third-lowest in the 27-member ÔÇőbloc.
Despite generous pro-family policies, including Ôüácheap loans and tax benefits, Orban appears to have lost the support of younger voters as he has lurched to the right.
Polls before the election showed young voters particularly eager for change, with Orban shifting back and forth between trying to woo the key demographic and deriding their opposition to his leadership as a 'phony rebellion'.
"I know young people like to turn against their parents and this can cause political problems," Ôüásaid Orban, a ÔÇőfather of five and a grandfather.
Despite attending countless election rallies and keeping up a barrage of interviews and social ÔÇőmedia posts, he gave a rare glimpse late last year into the toll that the campaign may have taken after so many years at the helm.
"When I was a soldier (doing military service), they told us a soldier cannot be cold, he can only ÔÇőperceive the cold," he said. "I am the same. I am not tired. It is just that my strength is running out."

item.title: Once inspired by Orban, Hungary's Peter Magyar unseats him in landmark election
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 12 (Reuters) - When Peter Magyar was a child, he taped a photo of Viktor Orban , then an anti-Communist firebrand, on his bedroom wall, thrilled by Hungary's first democratic elections in 1990.
Decades later, he ended Orban's 16-year rule as prime minister in an election that brought a record-high turnout and was expected to rattle Russia and send shockwaves through right-wing circles across the ÔÇőWest, including U.S. President Donald Trump's White House.
Magyar's centre-right, pro-European Union Tisza party beat Orban's nationalist Fidesz party in Sunday's parliamentary election. Partial results showed Tisza ÔÇîwould win 137 seats, or a two-thirds majority, in the 199-seat parliament.
Only nine years old when communism collapsed, Magyar said he had decorated his walls with photos of leading political figures in his Budapest family home.
Orban, then a young lawyer, had become a hero of Hungary's pro-democracy movement when he publicly demanded in 1989 that Soviet troops leave the country.
"There was a surge of energy around the regime change that swept me up as ÔÇőa child," Magyar told the Fokuszcsoport podcast last year.
Magyar, whose family name literally means "Hungarian", burst into the limelight two years ago after his ex-wife, Orban's former justice minister ÔÇőJudit Varga, resigned from all political roles after a sex-abuse case pardon that caused public uproar.
Magyar quickly distanced himself from the governing party and Ôüáaccused it of corruption and spreading propaganda, saying he had become disillusioned with Fidesz.
Just four months after emerging from near-total obscurity with an interview at YouTube channel Partizan, MagyarÔÇÖs new party ÔÇőwon 30% in the June 2024 European elections, finishing second to Fidesz and crushing the rest of the opposition.
Orban's defeat has significant implications not only for Hungary but for Europe and ÔÇőits populist far right.
Orban has sought to create what he calls an "illiberal democracy" since 2010, curbing media freedoms and NGO activities, and weakening the independence of the judiciary.
He has forged good relations with Russian President Vladimir Putin and also with U.S. President Donald Trump but has clashed repeatedly with the EU, which suspended billions of euros in funding due to concerns over Hungary's democratic standards.
By contrast, Magyar has pledged to rebuild Hungary's Western ÔÇőorientation and end its dependence on Russian energy by 2035 while striving for "pragmatic relations" with Moscow. He has also promised to unlock the frozen EU funds, which would help revive Hungary's ÔÇőstagnant economy.
"On the first day we need to pass anti-corruption measures and we need to submit our application to join the European Prosecutor's Office," Magyar said on Sunday morning after casting his vote.
But he has ÔÇîtrodden carefully Ôüáduring the election campaign, keen not to scare away more conservative voters.
Unlike Orban, he does not reject in principle Ukraine's right to join the EU one day but Tisza's programme does not support fast-track entry for Kyiv. Like Fidesz, Tisza opposes EU quotas for taking in migrants and it would also keep in place a border fence built under Orban to keep out illegal migrants.
But analysts say tensions between Budapest and the EU - further aggravated by Orban's veto of a 90 billion euro aid package for Kyiv - could ease under Tisza.
"Orban has lost faith in the current form ÔÇőand direction of European integration, and is pursuing ÔÇőa policy of vetoes and obstruction," said ÔüáBotond Feledy, a geopolitical analyst at Red Snow Consulting.
"Tisza has no objection in principle to integration and would pitch its battles at a practical level."
Magyar drew from Orban's playbook in this election, waging a grassroots campaign that took him into Fidesz's rural heartlands.
His rallies ÔÇőalways featured lots of national flags, in an Orban-style appeal to Hungarian voters' patriotism.
His consistent and clear messages, and skilful use of social ÔÇőmedia have all contributed Ôüáto his rapid rise, said Gabor Toka, senior research fellow at the Vera and Donald Blinken Open Society Archives.
"Many people are also reassured by the story of someone who has irrevocably come into conflict with the system, and has no way back," he said, referring to Magyar's break with Orban.
Born in 1981 into a family of lawyers, Magyar also studied law. He married Varga in 2006 and Ôüáwhen her career ÔÇőtook her to Brussels, Magyar joined Hungary's diplomatic corps and worked on EU legislation. After returning to Hungary, he ÔÇőjoined a state bank and then headed a student-loan agency.
Magyar and Varga, who divorced in 2023, have three sons.
Magyar describes himself as religious and says he enjoys cooking and playing soccer with his friends and sons.
Asked in December how ÔÇőhe had changed since going into politics, Magyar alluded to media reports that describe him as short-tempered, saying: "Now I count to 10."

item.title: Reactions to PM Orban's defeat in Hungary's election
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 12 (Reuters) - Hungary's opposition centre-right Tisza party appeared on track to win Sunday's national election , partial results showed, ending 16 years of rule by Prime Minister Viktor Orban, a nationalist backed by U.S. President Donald Trump and Russia.
Here are some quotes ÔÇőfrom Hungarian politicians and foreign leaders on the election results.
"As ÔÇîwe promised, as we hoped, today, on April 12, 2026, Hungary and several million people made history again, exactly 23 years to the day after there was a referendum on joining the European Union in Hungary."
"The election results are not final yet but the ÔÇősituation is understandable and clear. The election result is painful for us, but clear. The responsibility and possibility ÔÇőof governing was not given to us. I have congratulated the winner."
"Far-right authoritarian Viktor Orb├ín has lost the election. Trump ÔÇősycophants and MAGA extremists in Congress are up next in November. Winter is coming."
"Hungary ÔÇőhas chosen Europe. Europe has always chosen Hungary. A country reclaims its European path. The Union grows stronger. Hungary has chosen Europe. Europe has always chosen Hungary. Together we are stronger. A country returns to its European path. The Union becomes stronger... EuropeÔÇÖs heart is beating stronger ÔÇőin Hungary tonight."
"IÔÇÖve just spoken with Peter Magyar to congratulate him on his victory in Hungary! France welcomes ÔÇőwhat has been a victory in terms of people taking part in the democratic process, and a victory which shows the attachment of ÔÇîthe Hungarian Ôüápeople to the values of the European Union and for Hungary's role in Europe."
"The Hungarian people have decided. My heartfelt congratulations on your electoral success, dear @magyarpeterMP
"I am looking forward to working with you. LetÔÇÖs join forces for a strong, secure and, above all, united Europe. Gratul├ílok, kedves Magyar P├ęter!"
"I congratulate Peter Magyar and Tisza on ÔÇőtheir victory in the Hungarian ÔÇőparliamentary elections - a result Ôüáwith great importance to all of Europe. I look forward to close and constructive cooperation in pursuing peace and stability, democracy and the rule of law on our continent."
"In democratic elections, the Hungarian people have demonstrated their strong will to be an active ÔÇőmember of European ÔüáUnion and NATO. The election result gives Hungary the opportunity to return to our community of values and security as a constructive actor."
"Hungarians have made a historic choice for a free and strong Hungary in a united Europe, rejecting forces that Ôüáignore ÔÇőtheir interests. Congratulations to Peter Magyar on winning the election! I look forward ÔÇőto working together on what unites Estonia, Hungary and Europe."
"Big win for Hungary! Big win for Europe! Congratulations to @magyarpeterMP. There are a lot ÔÇőof things we could and should do for the sake of democracy, justice and peace."

item.title: US military says it will start blockade of all ships going to and from Iran on Monday
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 12 (Reuters) - U.S. Central Command said it will begin implementing ÔÇőa blockade of all maritime ÔÇîtraffic entering and exiting Iranian ports on April 13 at 10 ÔÇőa.m. ET (1400 GMT), after ÔÇőPresident Donald Trump said the U.S. ÔüáNavy would start blockading ÔÇőthe Strait of Hormuz.
"The blockade ÔÇőwill be enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ÔÇőports and coastal areas, ÔÇőincluding all Iranian ports on the Arabian ÔÇîGulf Ôüáand Gulf of Oman. CENTCOM forces will not impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting ÔÇőthe Strait ÔÇőof ÔüáHormuz to and from non-Iranian ports," CENTCOM wrote ÔÇőin a statement on ÔÇősocial Ôüámedia.
Additional information will be provided to commercial mariners through a Ôüáformal ÔÇőnotice before the ÔÇőblockade starts, CENTCOM added.

item.title: Oil bounces back above $100 after US, Iran talks end in stalemate
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 13 (Reuters) - Oil prices jumped above $100 ÔÇőa barrel on ÔÇîMonday as the U.S. Navy prepared ÔÇőto blockade the ÔÇőStrait of Hormuz after Ôüátalks between ÔÇőthe U.S. and Iran ÔÇőfailed to reach a deal to end ÔÇőthe war.
Brent crude ÔÇőfutures rose $7.11, or 7.47%, ÔÇîto $102.31 Ôüáa barrel by 2204 GMT after settling 0.75% lower ÔÇőon ÔÇőFriday.
U.S. ÔüáWest Texas Intermediate was at $104.43 ÔÇőa barrel, ÔÇőup $7.86, Ôüáor 8.14%, following a 1.33% loss Ôüáin ÔÇőthe previous ÔÇősession.

item.title: Spanish premier Sanchez heads for fourth China visit, risks annoying Trump
item.description:
MADRID, April 13 (Reuters) - Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez begins his fourth visit to China in as many years on Monday, underlining his ÔÇődetermination to steer an independent course that has riled U.S. President Donald Trump .
Spain has been one of Europe's loudest proponents of ÔÇîexpanding trade and treating China as a strategic ally rather than the economic and geopolitical rival that Trump sees it as.
