The repeat visit highlights Sanchez's determination not to align himself with Trump ÔÇö who has heavily criticised Spain for denying landing rights to U.S. forces in the war with Iran and for its relatively low defence spending - ÔÇőand threatened to punish uncooperative NATO military allies.
Sanchez's approach is popular with voters, but has unnerved firms and opposition politicians who fear that ÔÇőfriction with the U.S. administration could be costly for Spain.
A government source Ôüáwho was not authorised to speak to the media said Tuesday's meeting with President Xi Jinping would focus on geopolitics, and that Spain ÔÇősees China as a stabilising force.
Yet Ramon Gascon Alonso, Asia-Pacific coordinator of Spain's Exporters' and Investors' Club, said this focus could further harm trade burdened ÔÇőby Trump's tariffs on imports from the European Union, and "make our already precarious situation much worse".
"The United States is the leading foreign investor in Spain," he said. "We have a significant volume of trade with the United States in sectors that are central to our economy ÔÇô absolutely vital."
Sanchez stands out among European allies as an outspoken ÔÇőcritic of the U.S. and Israeli military campaigns.
A senior opposition source advised against any further criticism while Sanchez is in China, accusing Sanchez of ÔÇőtangling with Trump for domestic reasons and putting at risk NATO unity and the U.S. military presence in Spain.
Chinese ambassador to Spain Yao Jing said warm ÔÇîrelations had Ôüáencouraged Chinese companies to invest.
"Spain is more reasonable in dealing with China," he said. "It has its own judgment, its own interests, it wants access to Chinese markets. So we do business."
Chinese firms invested 643 million euros in Spain in 2025, up from 149 million a year earlier, data from the Economy Ministry shows, bringing the total to 9.7 billion euros between 2010 and 2025, primarily in extractive industry and ÔÇőthe energy sector.
Xi will this time ÔÇőfete Sanchez and his wife Begona Ôüáwith a formal banquet, while he will also meet with China's premier, Li Qiang, and top legislator Zhao Leji during the three-day visit.
On day one, Sanchez will visit the headquarters of tech giant Xiaomi and host ÔÇőa forum for Spanish and Chinese energy, infrastructure, bioscience and technology firms. He will also meet with ÔÇőinternational relations specialists Ôüáand make a speech at Beijing's Tsinghua University.
Madrid hopes the visit will help narrow a trade deficit that has more than doubled in four years to nearly $50 billion in 2025, looking to boost agricultural and manufacturing exports to offset high volumes of Chinese imports.
Still, Sanchez is not taking a business delegation Ôüáwith him ÔÇőthis time, and no major deals are planned.
Officials do, however, hope to sign a regionalisation ÔÇőagreement for Spanish poultry exports affected by bird flu outbreaks. A similar deal for the pork industry is credited with staving off a major blow to one of Spain's most ÔÇőlucrative exports during a recent African swine fever outbreak.

item.title: Keiko Fujimori leads Peru's presidential vote with 16.6%, shows early exit poll from Ipsos Peru
item.description:
LIMA, April 12 (Reuters) - Right-leaning candidate Keiko Fujimori ÔÇőis leading Peru's ÔÇîpresidential vote with 16.6%, an early ÔÇőexit poll by ÔÇőIpsos Peru showed on ÔüáSunday.
Leftist candidate ÔÇőRoberto Sanchez trailed with ÔÇő12.1%, and a third candidate, center-leftist Ricardo ÔÇőBelmont, had 11.8% ÔÇőof the vote.
If no ÔÇîcandidate Ôüáclears the 50% threshold needed to win outright, the ÔÇőtwo ÔÇőmost-voted Ôüácandidates would advance to a ÔÇősecond round, ÔÇőscheduled Ôüáfor June 7.

item.title: Cyclone Vaianu leaves North Island cleanup underway in New Zealand
item.description:
WELLINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - New Zealanders who evacuated ahead of a cyclone that battered the North Island on Sunday ÔÇőwere gradually being allowed to return home as ÔÇîthe storm moved away from the country.
Local media reported cleanup crews were clearing roads, removing fallen trees and checking ÔÇőfor possible landslides. Power companies continued to report ÔÇőunplanned outages, while some roads remained closed.
Julie Jukes, Ôüáacting mayor of Whakatane, one of the areas ÔÇőhit by the cyclone, told Radio New Zealand it ÔÇőhad brought the worst weather she had ever seen, but "the main thing is that everybody was safe and it's only damage ÔÇőto property and trees, power and things like ÔÇőthat."
Prime Minister Christopher Luxon told Radio New Zealand the emergency system ÔÇîhad Ôüáworked well, people had prepared for the storm and the response had been much better than in previous emergencies.
New Zealand forecaster MetService said in a post ÔÇőon X ÔÇőlate on ÔüáSunday that Cyclone Vaianu had moved offshore and was east of the North Island.
By ÔÇőMonday morning, the only warnings still in ÔÇőplace Ôüáwere for large ocean swells off the coast of lower North Island.
New Zealand has been hit by a Ôüáseries ÔÇőof severe weather events this ÔÇőyear, including heavy rain in January that triggered a landslide and killed ÔÇősix people.

item.title: US to blockade Iran after talks fail to yield a┬ádeal
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 12 (Reuters) - The U.S. military said it will begin a blockade of all maritime ÔÇőtraffic entering and exiting Iranian ports and coastal areas on Monday after weekend talks failed to reach a deal to end the war with Iran , jeopardizing a ÔÇîfragile two-week ceasefire.
The talks in Islamabad, which ran from Saturday into early Sunday, were the first direct U.S.-Iranian meeting in more than a decade and the highest-level discussions since Iran's 1979 Islamic Revolution. The negotiations came days after a ceasefire began on Tuesday, aimed at ending six weeks of fighting that has killed thousands of people across the Gulf, throttled vital supplies of energy and sparked fears of a wider regional conflict.
The U.S. Central ÔÇőCommand said on Sunday that the U.S. blockade, starting at 10 a.m. ET (1400 GMT), would be "enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal ÔÇőareas, including all Iranian ports on the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman."
Vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports will Ôüánot be impeded, the U.S. military said. Additional information would be provided to commercial mariners through a formal notice prior to the start of the blockade, it said.
President Donald Trump on ÔÇőSunday also said U.S. forces would intercept every vessel in international waters that had paid a toll to Iran.
"No one who pays an illegal toll will have safe passage on the high ÔÇőseas," Trump wrote on social media, adding: "Any Iranian who fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be BLOWN TO HELL!"
He added that the U.S. Navy will begin destroying mines that the Iranians had dropped in the Strait of Hormuz , a choke point for about 20% of global energy supplies.
While shipping data showed three supertankers fully laden with oil passed through the Strait on Saturday, tankers were steering clear of the waterway on Monday, ahead ÔÇőof the U.S. blockade.
After Trump's initial remarks on Sunday, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guards warned that military vessels approaching the strait will be considered a ceasefire breach and dealt with ÔÇőharshly and decisively, underlining the risk of a dangerous escalation.
A U.S. official said Iran rejected Washington's call for an end to all uranium enrichment, the dismantling of all major enrichment facilities and the transfer of ÔÇîhighly enriched Ôüáuranium.
Iran also refused U.S. demands that Iran cease funding for Hamas, Hezbollah and the Houthis, as well as fully open the Strait of Hormuz, the official added.
Iranian media said there was agreement on a number of issues, but the strait and Iran's nuclear program were the main sticking points.
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi said Iran had "encountered maximalism, shifting goalposts, and blockade" when just inches away from an "Islamabad MoU."
"Zero lessons learned," he added. "Good will begets good will. Enmity begets enmity."
Six weeks of fighting has killed thousands , roiled the global economy and sent oil prices soaring as Iran prevented traffic through ÔÇőthe Strait.
The U.S. dollar and oil rose in ÔÇőearly trading on Monday after the failure Ôüáof the talks, while stocks in Asia eased.
Trump told Fox News' "Sunday Briefing" program that oil and gasoline prices may remain high through November's midterm elections , a rare acknowledgement of the potential political fallout from the war.
Iran's Qalibaf posted a map of Washington-area gasoline prices on social media with the ÔÇőcomment: "Enjoy the current pump figures. With the so-called 'blockade'. Soon you'll be nostalgic for $4ÔÇô$5 gas."
Trump told Fox News on Sunday that he believed ÔÇőIran would continue to negotiate Ôüáand called the Islamabad discussions "very friendly."
"I do believe they're going to come to the table on this, because nobody can be so stupid as to say, ÔÇśWe want nuclear weapons,ÔÇÖ and they have no cards," he said.
But several hours later, the U.S. president said he did not care whether a "desperate" Iran returned to the negotiating table.
"If they don't come back, I'm fine," Trump told journalists on Sunday night after Ôüáhe returned ÔÇőto the Washington area from an overnight stay in Florida.
Qalibaf blamed the U.S. for not winning Tehran's trust, despite ÔÇőhis team offering "forward-looking initiatives," Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian, who discussed the talks in a call with Russian President Vladimir Putin, said Tehran wanted "a balanced and fair agreement."
"If the United States returns to the framework of international law, reaching an ÔÇőagreement is not far off," he told Putin, Iranian state media reported.

item.title: Hungary opposition's landslide win heralds reforms, thaw in EU ties
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Peter Magyar's landslide victory in Sunday's Hungarian election has handed his centre-right Tisza party a sweeping mandate that will give it a free ÔÇőhand to enact reforms, bolster the rule of law and potentially unlock billions in European Union funding.
Economists and political analysts say the incoming government's expected two-thirds supermajority ÔÇîwas the most EU- and market-friendly scenario - and before Sunday, one of the most improbable - and would likely trigger a strong rally in Hungarian assets on Monday.
A number of uncertainties remain, and wary diplomats and analysts say the new government must deliver on its promises before reaping the full benefits, but the markets for now look willing to give Budapest's new masters the benefit of the doubt.
"The result is a game-changer and will allow Magyar ÔÇőto govern with a free hand," said Mujtaba Rahman, a managing director at Eurasia Group. "Most importantly, he will be able to unwind Orban's autocracy and deliver on all of ÔÇőthe reforms the EU is demanding.
"That means at least 6.4 billion euros ($7.46 billion) from the resilience and recovery facility should flow quickly, shoring up Ôüáthe real economy and further consolidating Tisza's win."
The election had long been anticipated as the most market-sensitive in Europe this year given the eurosceptic Orban's frequent clashes with ÔÇőBrussels during his 16-year rule, over issues ranging from immigration to his closeness to Russia.
Orban had expressed confidence throughout the election campaign despite lagging in opinion polls, saying his goal was to protect ÔÇőHungary's national identity and traditional Christian values within the EU, while denying any wrongdoing.
But the markets had been signalling for weeks that investors expected change. The share prices of companies linked to Orban fell sharply, while market volatility gauges indicated that big currency moves were likely after the election.
Magyar, addressing jubilant supporters chanting "Europe, Europe" after Orban conceded defeat, pledged to make Hungary a strong EU and NATO ally and rebuild ties marred by years ÔÇőof conflict.
"With the two-thirds majority allowing us to amend the constitution, we will restore the system of checks and balances," Magyar said.
"We will join the European Public Prosecutor's Office and guarantee the ÔÇődemocratic functioning of our country. We will never again allow anyone to hold free Hungary captive or to abandon it."
One fundamental plank of Magyar's plan to kick-start Hungary's economy, which has been mired in near-stagnation ÔÇîfor the past Ôüáthree years, was to unlock EU funds frozen as democratic standards eroded under Orban.
"A constitutional majority is a different story entirely," said Ian Bremmer at GZERO Media.
"That would give Magyar the power to rewrite the constitution, clear out Fidesz loyalists from captured institutions, fully access EU funding, and even adopt the euro ÔÇô a core campaign pledge."
On Sunday, Magyar called on Hungary's chief prosecutor, the head of the top court, the head of media authority and other officials to resign, saying the country's public institutions had been captured by Orban loyalists over the past 16 years.
Magyar has pledged a sweeping anti-corruption ÔÇődrive as his party seeks to meet ÔüáEU conditions, including stronger judicial independence and public procurement, to unlock the funds.
However, credit rating agencies such as S&P Global and Fitch Ratings, as well as some EU diplomats, are sceptical about whether any money still available under Hungary's pandemic recovery funding would be released.
Diplomats and analysts say comparisons ÔÇőwith Poland's 2023 election, when Prime Minister Donald Tusk's pro-EU cabinet secured a quick release of EU funding on promises to roll back ÔÇőhis nationalist predecessor's policies, Ôüámay be misguided.
"There is no willingness to give out the money only on a promise like the EU did to Tusk in Poland, who was not able to deliver on most promises," said an EU diplomat.
"Tisza would need to demonstrate that it can deliver. But if something is legally impossible, and that can be demonstrated, then the EU could figure out a way."
Analysts at Capital Economics say the release Ôüáof EU funding ÔÇőcould help cut Hungary's budget deficit towards 3.5% to 4% of national output by the end of the decade ÔÇőand stabilise public debt - the EU's highest outside the euro zone.
"Overall, the election result marks a major turning point for Hungary's economy," Liam Peach said in a note.
"The durability of any positive market reaction will now depend on how quickly ÔÇőTisza moves to rebuild relations with the EU, secure EU fund disbursements and signal a credible medium-term fiscal anchor."
($1 = 0.8573 euros)

item.title: US, Australia, Philippines hold second joint drills in South China Sea this year
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - The U.S. and Australia joined the Philippines for their second joint maritime exercises in ÔÇőthe South China Sea this year, amid tensions ÔÇîwith China in the disputed waterway.
The four-day drills from April 9 to 12 brought together warships, fighter jets ÔÇőand surveillance aircraft in a series of coordinated ÔÇőoperations to strengthen maritime defence capabilities, the ÔüáPhilippine military said on Monday.
The exercises underscored the "deepening ÔÇődefence cooperation among the three nations and their shared ÔÇőcommitment to regional security", it said in a statement.
The Philippines deployed its FAÔÇĹ50 fighter jets, while Australia contributed PÔÇĹ8A ÔÇőPoseidon aircraft and the United States the USS ÔÇőAshland, a dock landing ship.
The drills came ahead of the April ÔÇî20 Ôüáopening of the annual large-scale war games called Balikatan - or "shoulder-to-shoulder" - between Manila and Washington, which for the first time will include Japan as a full ÔÇőparticipant rather ÔÇőthan an Ôüáobserver, alongside Australia.
The Philippines and China have traded accusations over a series of ÔÇőincidents in the South China Sea, ÔÇőincluding a Ôüárecent encounter in which Beijing fired flares at a Philippine Coast Guard aircraft.
Beijing has routinely opposed Manila's joint Ôüámilitary ÔÇőactivities with its allied partners ÔÇőin the South China Sea, saying such drills heighten tensions in ÔÇőthe region.

item.title: Russia, Ukraine trade accusations of Orthodox Easter ceasefire violations
item.description:
KYIV/MOSCOW, April 12 (Reuters) - Russia and Ukraine accused each other on Sunday of breaching the 32-hour ceasefire in their four-year war, reporting ÔÇîmore than a thousand drone and shelling attacks just hours after the truce began on Saturday to mark Orthodox Easter.
The Russian defence ministry said it recorded 1,971 ceasefire violations overnight into Sunday.
The General Staff of Ukraine's military, in a late evening report issued as the end of the truce ÔÇőwas approaching, said it had compiled a total of 7,696 Russian violations over the course of the ÔÇőtruce.
According to the calendar of the Orthodox faith dominant in both countries, Easter is celebrated Ôüáthis year on Sunday.
A soldier from Ukraine's 65th brigade fighting in Zaporizhzhia said Russian reconnaissance drones were still circling ÔÇőthe area despite the ceasefire, blocking efforts to recover the bodies of fallen troops.
"We wanted to evacuate our fallen comrades ÔÇőtoday, but (the Russians) are not letting us do so just yet," the soldier, known by the call sign Spider, said at a candle-lit military Easter service.
Both sides continued to report what they described as violations of the ceasefire throughout the day on Sunday. The ÔÇőtruce was due to end at midnight (2100 GMT).
The latest report by the Ukrainian General Staff said the 7,696 Russian ÔÇőviolations noted over the course of the truce included 1,355 artillery shelling incidents, 115 assault operations and 6,226 strikes by attack drones. ÔÇîIt said Ôüáno air strikes had been recorded.
Ukraine's State Emergencies Service said two civilians were wounded on Sunday by a Russian drone attack in northeastern Kharkiv region. Police in the region later said a Russian drone had struck a car, injuring one person.
Russia said a child was among civilians injured in a Ukrainian drone attack on the Kursk region. The ÔÇőBelgorod region's governor, Vyacheslav Gladkov, ÔÇőreported two people killed by ÔüáUkrainian shelling and several injured in other strikes through the border region.
The Russia-appointed head of the parts of southern Kherson region held by Russian forces, Vladimir Saldo, said Ukrainian troops ÔÇőhad shelled an administrative building and a private home in two villages, without causing ÔÇőinjuries.
Reuters could not Ôüáindependently verify reports of military activity.
Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said on Sunday the truce was a "humanitarian gesture" from Russian President Vladimir Putin, but that Russian troops would resume fighting as soon as it ends.
"Putin has instructed the Russian military to Ôüáremain ÔÇőon high alert in case of provocations," Peskov said.
Putin announced the temporary ÔÇőOrthodox Easter ceasefire on April 9. Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskiy, whose previous ceasefire proposals were turned down by Moscow, agreed to the truce.

item.title: Businessman Rafael Lopez Aliaga leads Peru's presidential vote with 23.4%, official count shows
item.description:
LIMA, April 12 (Reuters) - Right-leaning candidate Rafael Lopez ÔÇőAliaga is leading Peru's ÔÇîpresidential race with 23.4% of the votes, ÔÇőearly results ÔÇőof the official vote count Ôüáshowed Sunday.
Center-left ÔÇőcandidate Jorge Nieto trailed ÔÇőwith 16.4% of votes, with 5% of ballots ÔÇőcounted.
A third candidate, ÔÇőright-wing Keiko Fujimori, had 16.1% ÔÇîof Ôüáthe votes.
If no candidate clears the 50% threshold needed ÔÇőto ÔÇőwin Ôüáoutright, the two most-voted candidates ÔÇőwould advance to ÔÇőa Ôüásecond round, scheduled for June 7.

item.title: Trump attacks Pope Leo, calling him 'weak' on crime and 'terrible'
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 12 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald ÔÇîTrump issued a scathing attack on Pope Leo late on Sunday, calling him "weak" on ÔÇőcrime and "terrible" for foreign policy after ÔÇőthe religious leader criticized Trump's foreign Ôüáand immigration policies.
"Pope Leo is WEAK ÔÇőon Crime and terrible for Foreign Policy," ÔÇőTrump wrote in a lengthy post on Truth Social.
Leo, who is known for choosing his ÔÇőwords carefully, has emerged as an ÔÇőoutspoken critic of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran that ÔÇîbegan Ôüáon February 28.
The pope said this month's threat from Trump to destroy Iranian civilization was " unacceptable ." He has also called ÔÇőfor " deep reflection " ÔÇőabout the Ôüáway migrants are being treated in the U.S. under ÔÇőthe Trump administration.
"Leo should get his ÔÇőact Ôüátogether as Pope," Trump wrote in his post, later telling reporters he was Ôüánot ÔÇőa "big fan" of the ÔÇőpontiff.

item.title: Budapest turns into party zone after historic election that ends Orban era
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Budapest turned into a party zone well into the early hours on Monday with people dancing outside pubs and cars honking as supporters of the opposition Tisza party celebrated ÔÇőtheir sweeping victory in a pivotal election on Sunday.
The election, which brought a record ÔÇîturnout of 80% based on preliminary data, ended the 16-year rule of right-wing Prime Minister Viktor Orban and for Hungarians toasting and waving flags in the capital city this meant voting for the country's European orientation and ÔÇőa restoration of democracy, eroded by Orban and his government since 2010.
Budapest's famous Chain ÔÇőBridge was illuminated in HungaryÔÇśs national colors and along the embankment by the ÔüáDanube river tens of thousands partied, waving the national flag and European Union flags, and some ÔÇőtoasting with champagne in paper cups.
"I feel amazing! Really amazing! Like, I never thought this would ÔÇőactually happen. I've been praying for this for 16 years so I'm super happy," said Szilvia, a Tisza supporter, as people watched the victory speech from opposition leader Peter Magyar.
Chanting and singing broke out on the city's metro, ÔÇőwith people chanting "it's over." Other Tisza supporters celebrated at a rave outside the country's iconic parliament ÔÇőbuilding.
"It's a big celebration. I'm quite happy. I have been waiting for this for some time. And I ÔÇîhope that Ôüáthe new leadership, new prime minister will be really a prime minister for all Hungarians, as he said," said Tisza supporter Sandor Zoletnik.
"Amazing!" "Can't describe it!" "We are so happy that Orban is finally gone." "We did not believe that it would change," a group of young people hugged and jumped, shouting.
"So, ÔÇőfinally, after at least ÔÇő16 years, we feel Ôüálike there is a hope. We feel like there is a hope that Hungary can start walking into the right direction," one of them ÔÇősaid. Orban's Fidesz party had the lowest support in the young generation .
Fidesz, originally ÔÇőlaunched as Ôüáan opposition youth movement in the late 1980s, is backed by just 8% of voters aged between 18 and 29, based on a survey by pollster Median, or 22% in the wider 18-39 age group, Ôüáaccording ÔÇőto Zavecz Research.
Traffic stopped along some of the main boulevards ÔÇőas crowds in front of pubs overflowed and people waved national flags, stopping cars. Hundreds strolled along the bridges spanning ÔÇőthe Danube well after midnight.

item.title: Pope Leo to begin 10-day Africa tour on mission to spotlight continent's needs
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, April 13 (Reuters) - Pope Leo begins an ÔÇőambitious 10-day tour of four countries in Africa on Monday urging global leaders to address needs on the ÔÇîcontinent where more than a fifth of the world's Catholics live.
The first U.S. pope heads to Algeria for two days before continuing to Cameroon, Angola and Equatorial Guinea in a whirlwind tour to 11 cities and towns, traversing nearly 18,000 km (11,185 miles) over 18 flights.
The ÔÇőpope is making the visit with a mission "to help turn the world's attention to Africa", Cardinal Michael ÔÇőCzerny, a senior Vatican official and close adviser to Leo, told Reuters.
The pope, who has emerged as Ôüáan outspoken critic of the Iran war , has made only one big overseas trip since being elected last May, ÔÇővisiting Turkey and Lebanon in November and December. He visited Monaco in March.
Leo, aged 70, relatively young and in good health ÔÇőfor a pope, is undertaking one of the most complicated tours arranged for a pontiff in decades.
More than 20% of the world's Catholics live in Africa, according to Vatican statistics. The three sub-Saharan nations the pope is visiting have populations where more than half identify as ÔÇőCatholic.
Algeria, though, is an overwhelmingly Muslim country with under 10,000 Catholics among its population of some 48 million people. ÔÇőThis is the first time it will host a Catholic pope.
Leo's tour is the ÔÇî24th Ôüáby a pope to Africa since the late 1960s .
He is expected to touch on many topics in 25 planned speeches over 10 days, Vatican spokesperson Matteo Bruni told journalists on Friday, given that the four nations face diverse issues.
Likely topics include exploitation of natural resources, Catholic-Muslim dialogue, and dangers of political corruption, said Bruni.
Cameroon and Equatorial Guinea have presidents who have ÔÇőbeen in power for decades ÔÇőand have been accused Ôüáof human rights abuses, which they deny .
The biggest event of the itinerary will likely come in Cameroon on Friday, when the Vatican said some 600,000 are expected for a Mass in ÔÇőthe coastal city of Douala.
Comfortable in several languages, Leo is expected to speak Italian, ÔÇőEnglish, French, Portuguese Ôüáand Spanish during the trip.
Landing in Algeria in mid-morning on Monday, Leo will address the country's political leaders before visiting the Great Mosque of Algiers in only his second visit to a mosque as pope.
He will travel on Tuesday to Annaba Ôüáon Algeria's ÔÇőnortheast coast, for a visit to the ruins of the ancient ÔÇőtown of Hippo.
The site has special meaning to Leo, who is a member of the Augustinian religious order. The order is inspired by the teachings ÔÇőof fourth-century St. Augustine of Hippo, a major figure in the early Church.

item.title: Russia, Ukraine accuse each other of violating Orthodox Easter ceasefire
item.description:
MOSCOW/KYIV, April 11 (Reuters) - Russia and Ukraine on Saturday accused each other of violating ÔÇőa brief ceasefire in their four-year-old war hours into the truce put in place to mark Orthodox Easter.
Governors of two Russian ÔÇîborder regions said Ukrainian drones had attacked targets in the Kursk and Belgorod regions, injuring five people.
The General Staff of Ukraine's military said Russian forces had violated the terms of the 32-hour truce 469 times, including assault actions, shelling and drone strikes.
The ceasefire, announced by Russian President Vladimir Putin on Thursday, went into effect at 4 ÔÇőp.m. Moscow time (1300 GMT). His Ukrainian counterpart, Volodymyr Zelenskiy, said he would abide by it.
Reuters could not independently verify reports of military ÔÇőactivity.
The ceasefire was put in place as U.S.-led negotiations to reach a settlement have been halted amid the Ôüáwar in the Middle East. U.S. and Iranian negotiators were meeting in Pakistan on Saturday to try to end their six-week-old war.
According to ÔÇőthe calendar of the Orthodox faith, which is dominant in both Russia and Ukraine, Easter is celebrated this year on Sunday.
Russian news agencies said ÔÇőPutin attended services after midnight at Moscow's vast Christ the Saviour Cathedral.
In his Easter greeting, quoted by agencies, Putin described Easter as the "triumph of love, good and justice". He also expressed thanks to Orthodox Patriarch Kirill for his support of those fighting in Russia's military campaign, described as a "special military operation".
"I would like especially ÔÇőto note the effective assistance you provide to our heroes - the participants and veterans of the special military operation," he wrote.
Alexander Khinshtein, governor of ÔÇőRussia's Kursk border region, wrote on the state-backed messenger service MAX that a Ukrainian drone had struck a petrol station in the town of Lgov, injuring ÔÇîthree people, Ôüáincluding a child.
Khinshtein said the attack took place after the start of the truce.
In the adjacent Belgorod region, Governor Vyacheslav Gladkov said two people were injured in Ukrainian drone attacks.
Gladkov, writing on Telegram, said a man and woman were injured in attacks in Shebekino and Grayvoron, two small towns just inside the border. He also said Ukrainian forces had shelled Shebekino, damaging homes and other buildings.
A local Russia-installed official in a ÔÇőpart of southern Kherson region held ÔÇőby Moscow also reported on social Ôüámedia that a Ukrainian drone attack had injured one person.
The Ukrainian General Staff, in a late evening report on front-line activity, said it had counted 469 ceasefire infringements since 1300 GMT.
"Specifically, 22 enemy assault ÔÇőactions, 153 shelling incidents, 19 strikes by attack drones ... and 275 strikes by (First-Person View) drones."
During a similar ÔÇő30-hour Easter ceasefire Ôüáagreed last year, each side accused the other of infringements.
Zelenskiy, in his nightly video address, made no reference to alleged ceasefire violations. He repeated that Ukraine would stick to the truce and wished it could be in effect longer.
"It would be right for the ceasefire to continue beyond this," Zelenskiy Ôüásaid.
"We have ÔÇőmade this proposal to Russia and if Russia once again chooses war instead of peace, ÔÇőit will show the world, and particularly the United States, who truly wants what."
Zelenskiy has proposed a halt to fighting on a number of occasions, but has been turned ÔÇődown by Moscow, which says it is seeking an overall settlement.

item.title: Oil tankers steer clear of Hormuz ahead of US blockade
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 13 (Reuters) - Oil tankers are steering clear of the ÔÇőStrait of Hormuz ahead of a U.S. blockade later on Monday following failed peace talks ÔÇîbetween the U.S. and Iran over the weekend, shipping data showed.
President Donald Trump said on Sunday the U.S. Navy would start blockading the Strait of Hormuz, raising the stakes after marathon talks with Iran failed to reach a deal to end the war, ÔÇőjeopardising a fragile two-week ceasefire.
U.S. Central Command said U.S. forces would begin implementing the blockade of ÔÇőall maritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports at 10 a.m. ET (1400 GMT) on ÔüáMonday.
It would be "enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal areas, ÔÇőincluding all Iranian ports on the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman," it said in a statement on X.
U.S. ÔÇőforces would not impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports, and additional information would be provided to commercial mariners through a formal notice prior to the start of the blockade, it said.
Iran's ÔÇőRevolutionary Guards said on Sunday that any military vessels attempting to approach the Strait of Hormuz would ÔÇőbe considered a violation of the ceasefire and be dealt with harshly and decisively.
Pakistan-flagged tankers Shalamar and Khairpur entered the ÔÇîGulf on ÔüáSunday, data from LSEG and Kpler showed.
The Aframax tanker Shalamar is heading to the United Arab Emirates on Monday to load Das crude while the Panamax-sized Khairpur is heading to Kuwait to load refined products, the data showed.
Pakistan National Shipping, which manages Shalamar, did not immediately respond to a request for comment outside of ÔÇőoffice hours.
Liberia-flagged very large crude ÔÇőcarrier (VLCC) Mombasa B, which Ôüáalso transited the strait earlier on Sunday, is ballasting in the Gulf, the data showed.
Malta-flagged VLCC Agios Fanourios I, which tried to pass through the strait on ÔÇőSunday to enter the Gulf to load Iraqi Basra crude for Vietnam, has ÔÇősince turned Ôüáback and is now anchored near the Gulf of Oman, the data showed. The tanker plans to head to Iraq.
Eastern Mediterranean Maritime, which manages the Agios Fanourios I, and CMB.TECH NV, the manager for the Mombasa B, did Ôüánot respond ÔÇőto requests for comment outside office hours.
Despite the stalemate, three supertankers ÔÇőfully laden with oil passed through the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday, shipping data showed. They appeared to be the first vessels to ÔÇőexit the Gulf since the ceasefire deal was struck last week.

item.title: US blockade of Iran will be major military endeavor, experts say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 12 (Reuters) - A U.S. naval blockade of Iran is a major, open-ended military endeavor that could trigger fresh retaliation from Tehran and put tremendous strain on an already ÔÇőfragile ceasefire, experts say.
President Donald Trump, in a social media post after no deal emerged from peace talks this weekend in Islamabad, said the U.S. Navy "will begin ÔÇîthe process of BLOCKADING any and all Ships trying to enter, or leave, the Strait of Hormuz."
The U.S. military's Central Command later said the blockade will only apply to ships going to or from Iran, including all Iranian ports on the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman. It will take effect on Monday at 10 a.m. in Washington (1400 GMT), CENTCOM said.
Trump also said U.S. forces would interdict vessels that have paid tolls to Iran, ÔÇőeven if those ships are now in international waters. "No one who pays an illegal toll will have safe passage on the high seas," Trump wrote on Truth Social.
The ÔÇőultimate goal, Trump said, would be to pressure Iran to end its effective closure of the strait, a choke point for about 20% Ôüáof the world's oil, to all but the countries that secure safe passage from Tehran.
If Trump's strategy succeeds, he would eliminate Iran's greatest point of leverage in negotiations with the ÔÇőUnited States and clear the strait again for global trade, potentially lowering oil prices. But a blockade, experts say, is an act of war that requires an open-ended commitment of a significant number of ÔÇőwarships.
"Trump wants a quick fix. The reality is, this mission is difficult to execute alone and likely unsustainable over the medium to long-term," said Dana Stroul, a former senior Pentagon official during the Biden administration now at The Washington Institute for Near East Policy.
The U.S. military has not offered basic details yet about the blockade, including how many U.S. warships will enforce it, whether warplanes will be used ÔÇőand whether any Gulf allies will assist in the effort. Central Command declined to respond to requests for comment.
With enough warships, the U.S. Navy could set up a blockade that intimidates ÔÇőmany commercial tankers from trying to power through with Iranian oil, experts say.
But would the United States be prepared to board and seize ÔÇö or even damage or sink ÔÇö ships that try to break the blockade? What ÔÇîif they carry Ôüáoil for China, a major power, or U.S. partners such as India or South Korea?
And what would Iran do? Retired Admiral Gary Roughead, a former chief of U.S. naval operations, cautioned that Iran could fire on ships in the Gulf or attack infrastructure of the Gulf states that host U.S. forces.
"I honestly believe that if we begin to do it, that Iran will have some kind of a reaction," Roughead said.
Iran's threats to shipping have caused global oil prices to skyrocket about 50% since the U.S. and Israel launched the war on February 28.
Trump said on ÔÇőSunday that the price of oil and gasoline may ÔÇőremain high in the United States Ôüáthrough November's U.S. midterm elections, which could see Trump's Republicans lose control of the U.S. Congress if there is a public backlash. The war has already been unpopular.
Frustrated by Iran's refusal to end the war on his terms, Trump on Sunday also floated the possibility of a ÔÇőresumption of U.S. strikes inside Iran, citing missile factories as one possibility.
U.S. Senator Mark Warner of Virginia, the ranking Democrat on the ÔÇőSenate Intelligence Committee, questioned Ôüáthe strategy, noting Iran could send speedboats to mine the strait or put bombs against tankers.
"How is that going to ever bring down gas prices?" Warner asked on CBS's "Face the Nation."
Thousands of U.S. military strikes have severely weakened Iran's military. But analysts say Tehran has emerged from the conflict as a vexing problem for Washington, with a more hardline leadership and a buried stockpile of highly enriched Ôüáuranium.
Trump threatened ÔÇőon Sunday that "any Iranian who fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be BLOWN TO HELL!"
Iran's Revolutionary ÔÇőGuards responded with a statement warning that military vessels approaching the strait will be considered a ceasefire breach and dealt with harshly and decisively, underlining the risk of a dangerous escalation.
Stroul said the crisis will require a long-term, ÔÇőinternational effort to resolve.
"Over the long run, this will need to be resolved through diplomacy and international political will," she said.

item.title: Trump returns to weary and failing playbook with Hormuz blockade threat
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, April 13 (Reuters) - Escalate to de-escalate is a well-worn playbook employed by U.S. President Donald Trump, and no doubt global crude oil markets are hoping his promise to blockade the Strait of Hormuz is just the latest example of this ploy.
The announcement that the U.S. ÔÇőNavy will block vessels from Iranian ports using the narrow waterway raises the stakes massively in the six-week-old conflict.
Trump's threat was delivered with his usual bombast ÔÇîin a social media post on Sunday, and it effectively ended the surge in optimism that a ceasefire between the two parties would result in a re-opening of the vital waterway.
Trump's action follows the failure of weekend talks between the two parties in Pakistan, which ended with both sides seemingly far apart on key issues.
The latest escalation saw global benchmark Brent crude futures jump about 8% in early Asian trade on ÔÇőMonday, climbing to $102.80 a barrel from a close of $95.20 on April 10.
Since the conflict started on February 28, the flow of crude oil, refined products and liquefied ÔÇőnatural gas through the Strait of Hormuz has slowed to a trickle from pre-war levels of roughly 20% of global supply.
What little traffic Ôüáthat has gone through the strait since the start of April has mainly been from Iran, with data compiled by commodity analysts Kpler showing 15 vessels that loaded at Iran's ÔÇőKharg Island terminal leaving between April 1 and 12.
Over the same period 12 tankers from other countries have made it through, with four from the United Arab Emirates, six from Saudi ÔÇőArabia and two from Iraq, according to Kpler.
There are several ways to look at the threat to blockade vessels from using the Strait of Hormuz.
The first is that it's surprising this hasn't happened sooner.
By halting Iran's crude shipments the United States would put serious pressure on Tehran's revenue and also likely force China, the major buyer of Iranian oil, to become more involved, with the hope that Beijing would encourage ÔÇőits ally to make concessions in order to ensure vessels moved freely.
However, there are major challenges to stopping tankers from Iran.
It's unlikely the U.S. military would fire missiles or other ÔÇőweapons at them, given the risk of an environmental disaster.
This leaves the most likely option as trying to force them to change course through threats, and if that doesn't work, launching armed boarding parties ÔÇîto take Ôüáphysical control of the ships.
Another way to look at Trump's threat is that it marks a return to escalation in the conflict, which makes the Iranian reaction key.
So far the hard-line clerical leaders in Iran have shown little sign of bending to U.S. demands, which range from ending its nuclear programme to fully re-opening the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran has largely done the opposite, responding with escalation of its own, such as attacking energy and military installations in its Gulf neighbours that host U.S. bases.
If the U.S. military does successfully interdict vessels from ÔÇőIranian ports, it would be logical to expect ÔÇőTehran would launch more attacks on Ôüáits neighbours, and the most effective targets would be export facilities that bypass the Strait of Hormuz.
Already Iran has damaged Saudi Arabia's east-to-west pipeline to Yanbu on the Red Sea, as well as hitting facilities at the United Arab Emirates' Fujairah terminal on the Gulf of ÔÇőOman.
Further attacks on these export pathways would inflict maximum damage on the already shaky crude oil markets, and likely turn what is ÔÇőalready a crisis into Ôüáa full-blown emergency.
The hope in the crude market is that Trump is once again escalating to de-escalate, but there are limits as to how many times this can happen before the damage to the world economy becomes severe and long-lasting.
It's a common refrain among U.S. right-wing media and commentators that Trump is playing some sort of four-dimensional chess and will eventually outwit Iran.
But the reality Ôüáis that Trump ÔÇőappears to be playing noughts and crosses, known as tic-tac-toe to Americans, and he hasn't yet worked out ÔÇőthat you cannot win.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI) , your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, data-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ever. ROI can ÔÇőhelp you keep up. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
The views expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.

item.title: War-fuelled debt spiral will shape global power
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters Breakingviews) - The United Kingdom cut debt after the Napoleonic wars so it had the fiscal firepower to fight subsequent battles. But todayÔÇÖs superpower struggle between the United States and China, and the conflicts in Iran and Ukraine, are occurring when government borrowing across the world is already high.
Governments are spending more on defence and cutting their reliance on rivals in response to global threats. That is pushing ÔÇőup debt relative to GDP, limiting their ability to project power. Borrowing costs have also shot up. The yield on 10-year U.S. government bonds has almost trebled to 4.3% over the past five years.
Few countries ÔÇîare immune to this spiral. The United States, China, France, the United Kingdom and Japan already have gross government debt equivalent to more than a yearÔÇÖs worth of economic output. Russia has run down its National Wealth Fund to finance its war in Ukraine, while foreign governments have frozen around $300 billion of MoscowÔÇÖs sovereign assets. Gulf Arab countries will also now have to dig deeper to defend themselves against Iran.
There is an old joke that the IMF stands for ÔÇťitÔÇÖs mostly fiscalÔÇŁ. When the International Monetary Fund and World Bank hold their annual meetings this week in Washington, the interaction between ÔÇőgovernment borrowing and geopolitical tension will be high on the agenda.
Part of the problem is rising defence costs. President Donald Trump wants to increase U.S. spending by $500 billion to $1.5 trillion next year. Meanwhile, European members of NATO last ÔÇőyear committed to increasing their outlay to 5% of output in 2035 from the previous goal of 2%.
Governments are also increasingly alive to the risk of blackmail. IranÔÇÖs control of the Strait Ôüáof Hormuz, through which a fifth of the worldÔÇÖs daily oil output previously passed, is the latest example. ChinaÔÇÖs domination of rare earths and EuropeÔÇÖs reliance on Russian gas before its invasion of Ukraine are others. Meanwhile, many countries are heavily dependent on ÔÇőU.S. defence, technology and finance ÔÇö which gives Washington the ability to bully them.
The sensible response is for countries to cut their exposure to potential enemies. But that is costly as it interferes with the free movement of goods, services and capital. Governments either ÔÇőhave to subsidise the process or put in controls that limit economic growth. Borrowing will be higher or output lower than it would have been ÔÇö boosting debt ratios.
All borrowing is not created equal. While debt-to-output ratios and annual borrowing levels are a good starting point to assess a stateÔÇÖs fiscal strength, other factors are also important.
How a government spends money is vital. For example, defence spending can boost productivity , opens new tab if it is used for research and development. The United States is a well-known example. Ukraine is now building a thriving military technology sector as a result of innovation forced ÔÇőby the need to defend itself from Russia. By contrast, subsidising energy consumption ÔÇö as some governments are doing to offset the higher oil prices that the Iran war has unleashed ÔÇö does nothing to spur long-run growth.
Off-balance sheet liabilities also matter. For example, pension ÔÇőcommitments will show up in future debt figures. The damage will be a function of how rapidly a population is ageing and the generosity of retirement payments. China and Japan are in a bad place in terms of ageing, while France is burdened by an expensive pension ÔÇîsystem it cannot Ôüáreform. By contrast, the United States gives relatively little money to retired citizens and its population is ageing more slowly.
Private-sector bad loans can also flip back onto governmentsÔÇÖ debt ratios ÔÇö either because states have to bail out banks as they did during the 2008 global financial crisis, or because sweeping problems under the carpet depresses growth. Moscow may be at risk because it has encouraged , opens new tab lenders to support its war effort. So may Beijing from its past promotion of housing investments. Washington could eventually find that leverage in areas such as private credit causes broader trouble.
How governments fund themselves also matters. If they rely mainly on domestic investors, like China, Japan and the euro zone do, they are in a stronger position. In extremis, they can use financial repression ÔÇö policies that lower government ÔÇőfinancing costs ÔÇö to force their people to fund them.
The United States ÔÇőis not so lucky. After years of running current Ôüáaccount deficits, the value of its investments in the rest of the world is $27 trillion , opens new tab less than the value of what other countries have invested in America. This means Washington relies on the ÔÇť kindness of strangers ÔÇŁ, as Mark Carney, now CanadaÔÇÖs prime minister, put it when he was governor of the Bank of England.
Of course, the dollarÔÇÖs status as the worldÔÇÖs main reserve currency gives the ÔÇőUnited States a funding advantage. But TrumpÔÇÖs wars and U.S. economic bullying are undermining that privilege, creating an opportunity for the euro, yuan and crypto currencies.
If an ÔÇőeconomy is vigorous, a government can Ôüágrow out of high debt. On this yardstick, all the main economic blocs have their problems.
While the United States has a thriving technology sector, TrumpÔÇÖs erratic policies and immigration curbs are undermining its vitality. Although China has grown spectacularly, its export model is unsustainable, and political repression may eventually dim creativity and animal spirits. Meanwhile, the European Union has not invested enough in technology or integrated the markets of its 27 member states adequately.
Yet another factor which determines a governmentÔÇÖs ability to carry heavy fiscal burdens is whether it can impose sacrifices Ôüáon its people. ÔÇőWestern states have little capacity to cut public spending or push up taxes. By contrast, China and Russia have high pain thresholds, in part because ÔÇőtheir autocratic governments find it easier to push through unpopular policies.
But even they have limits. For example, Russia is seemingly so keen to avoid conscription that it has used troops from North Korea and elsewhere to help fight its war in Ukraine.
Debt dynamics will be one of the factors that determine the superpower struggle ÔÇőbetween Washington and Beijing. On this score, both have challenges ÔÇö but China seems to have the edge.
Follow @Hugodixon , opens new tab on X

item.title: The Week in Breakingviews: Too big to fail again
item.description:
LONDON, April 12 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Welcome back! A week that started with Donald Trump threatening to destroy a ÔÇť whole civilization ÔÇŁ ended with peace talks in Pakistan and Iran charging tolls in the Strait of Hormuz. IÔÇÖm taking a break from the conflict to write about another looming fight: over shadow banks. Let me know , opens new tab what else we should be paying attention to. If this newsletter was forwarded to you, sign up here to get it in ÔÇőyour inbox every weekend.
ÔÇťSimply stating the facts is enough to make clear how absurd things have become at Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena: shareholders must pick a new CEO who has never run a ÔÇîmainstream bank before, or the lenderÔÇÖs fired ex-boss.ÔÇŁ
Read more: Rebel CEO is a rational vote in ItalyÔÇÖs bank drama .
HereÔÇÖs a potted history of financial regulation over the past 15 years: After 2008, watchdogs tightened capital and liquidity rules to prevent big banks from ever threatening the global economy again. To the surprise and consternation of some ÔÇőbank CEOs, this effort broadly succeeded. Entire chunks of financial activity, including proprietary trading in bonds and equities and lending to riskier borrowers, migrated from regulated institutions into shadow banks, or what Ôüápolicymakers now prefer to call non-bank financial intermediaries.
The shift was expected, and deliberate. Regulators reasoned that non-banks which got into trouble would pose less of a threat to the economy and therefore be less prone to seeking politically toxic bailouts. Over the last ÔÇődecade and a half, however, several things have changed. The first is that non-banks have grown much bigger: they now account for more than half of the roughly $500 trillion of financial assets in existence around the globe, according to the Financial Stability Board. The second ÔÇőis that they have moved closer to the heart of the financial system. Companies like Citadel Securities, Apollo Global Management (APO.N) , opens new tab and Stripe are not regulated banks, but still play key roles in making markets, allocating capital, and processing transactions.
Up to this point, bankers and regulators agree. Where they diverge is over what to do next. Many bank executives argue that some of the regulations introduced since 2008 should be modified, enabling them to grab back lost business or take advantage of new opportunities. JPMorgan (JPM.N) , opens new tab boss Jamie Dimon this week singled out the ÔÇťGSIB surchargeÔÇŁ, which imposes an extra capital requirement on ÔÇőthe worldÔÇÖs biggest banks. The proposed charge, he wrote in his annual letter , opens new tab to shareholders, seems to punish ÔÇťour success, our strength, our consistency and our balanced business model. Frankly itÔÇÖs not right, and itÔÇÖs un-American.ÔÇŁ ItÔÇÖs a rare post-2008 example of a top banker publicly ÔÇőmaking the case that big is better .
Regulators take a different view. They point to the relative stability of big banks in the last six turbulent years as a sign of success. As Klaas Knot, the former head of the Dutch central bank, told me this week, 2008 showed ÔÇîthat bank exposures, Ôüáwhich may seem diversified in calm times, tend to become highly correlated in a crisis.
Watchdogs would therefore prefer to tighten up oversight of non-banks, to avoid remaking the drama of 2008 with a different set of villains. A working group organised by the Group of Thirty former regulators and central bankers, chaired by Knot and Agustin Carstens, the former head of the Bank for International Settlements, this week warned of a brewing ÔÇť perfect storm , opens new tab ÔÇŁ in the non-bank sector. Their report , opens new tab cites two specific concerns: the central role played by hedge funds and others in government debt markets, and the rapid growth of private credit providers in lending to companies. Both groups also tend to have extensive links to regulated banks.
The coming battle in financial regulation is therefore whether to raise the playing field for non-banks, or ÔÇőto lower it for traditional lenders. The shape of the next ÔÇőmeltdown will depend on who gets the upper hand.
The sums earmarked to build data centres around the world are so vast they can be hard to put into context. Nvidia (NVDA.O) , opens new tab boss Jensen Huang reckons it costs $60 billion to $80 billion to build a 1 gigawatt facility. With more than 100 GW of projects already in the planning stages, the implied outlay is at least $6.6 trillion. Is it possible to finance construction on such a ÔÇőscale? Jeffrey Goldfarb added up the numbers and reckons thereÔÇÖs $7.5 trillion available. Spending it at high speed is a different challenge, though. The U.S. interstate highway system, which cost a mere $500 ÔÇőbillion in todayÔÇÖs money, took three decades Ôüáto complete.
Ransomware has become a large and lucrative global industry: half of all businesses with $10 million or more of turnover have suffered an attack on their computer systems in the past year. On The Big View , opens new tab this week I talked to Anja Shortland, professor of political economy at KingÔÇÖs College London and author of ÔÇťWe Know You Can Pay a MillionÔÇŁ, about why the business has boomed, and the fresh threats posed by new artificial intelligence and geopolitical conflicts.
Over in the Viewsroom , opens new tab , Aimee Donnellan and Jonathan Guilford talked to Neil Unmack about the Ôüádramatic corporate moves by ÔÇőglobal food groups, exemplified by Unilever (ULVR.L) , opens new tab and U.S. rival McCormick (MKC.N) , opens new tab creating a $65 billion mayonnaise-to-mustard giant.
The upsurge in Japanese takeovers has been one of the most ÔÇőstriking recent developments in corporate finance. After decades in the doldrums, Japanese companies have started prioritising returns to shareholders, under pressure from the government, activist investors, and hostile suitors. Now a seemingly benign directive from JapanÔÇÖs Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry threatens to derail the M&A bonanza. Hudson Lockett explains why an instruction ÔÇőfor companies to prioritise ÔÇťcorporate valueÔÇŁ may be something of a poison pill.
Want to receive The Week in Breakingviews in your inbox every Saturday? Sign up for the newsletter here .
Follow Peter Thal Larsen on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: US Democrats cheer defeat of Trump ally Orban in Hungary
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 12 (Reuters) - U.S. Democrats celebrated the defeat of ÔÇőHungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban on Sunday, while President Donald Trump's allies and Republican peers ÔÇîoffered a more mixed response to the loss of the leader that Trump had endorsed.
Trump had backed Orban leading up to the vote, even speaking briefly last week at a campaign rally in Hungary, when U.S. Vice President JD Vance telephoned ÔÇőhis boss upon taking the stage.
But Orban lost power after 16 years as Hungarians voted ÔÇőin record numbers for a pro-EU course spearheaded by center-right rival Peter Magyar.
U.S. Ôüálawmakers from both major parties congratulated Magyar on his victory.
Some Democrats framed Orban's loss as a ÔÇőharbinger of things to come for the November midterm elections in the United States.
"Pay attention, Donald Trump. ÔÇőWannabe dictators wear out their welcome," Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer said.
"Far-right authoritarian Viktor Orban has lost the election. Trump sycophants and MAGA extremists in Congress are up next in November," said U.S. House of Representatives Minority Leader Hakeem ÔÇőJeffries.
Republicans such as U.S. Senator Roger Wicker viewed the Hungarian election result as a repudiation of Russian ÔÇőPresident Vladimir Putin, with whom Orban had cultivated ties over the years.
Wicker, a Mississippi Republican who chairs the Senate ÔÇîArmed ÔüáServices Committee, said the results showed that Hungary's population rejected "the malign influence of Vladimir Putin" and "decided their own future."
Trump himself did not mention the Hungarian elections on Sunday, even as he opined on topics through social media posts, a television interview and a brief gathering with reporters.
But a few of his ÔÇőpro-Orban allies, such as technology ÔÇőtycoon Elon Musk, Ôüálamented the result.
"Soros Organization has taken over Hungary," Musk wrote on his X social media platform.
Billionaire financier and major Democratic donor George Soros, a Hungarian immigrant ÔÇőto the United States, has long been vilified by many conservatives.
Orban has been ÔÇőat loggerheads Ôüáwith the European Union over a range of issues, including Russia's war in Ukraine .
His self-described "illiberal democracy" has echoed policies touted by Trump, including a hard line against immigration, hostility toward global institutions, and attacks on the media Ôüáand ÔÇőuniversities.
Orban was the first European leader to endorse Trump during his ÔÇő2016 presidential bid.
Trump said last week his administration stood ready "to use the full economic might of the United States to strengthen ÔÇőHungary's economy" if Orban won.

item.title: Spanish premier urges China to take bigger role in multipolar order
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 13 (Reuters) - Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez on Monday said China should take on a more substantial role with issues including climate change, security, defense ÔÇőand the fight against inequality, adding that Europe will also have to ÔÇîredouble its efforts as the U.S. withdraws from leadership roles on many fronts.
Sanchez, who is making his fourth visit to China in four years, spoke at Beijing's Tsinghua University.
His visit comes as ÔÇőmany Western governments seek to maintain engagement with Beijing despite lingering security ÔÇőand trade tensions. It follows visits to China earlier this year Ôüáby the prime ministers of Britain, Ireland, Canada and Finland.
Spain has been one ÔÇőof Europe's loudest proponents of expanding trade and treating China as a strategic ally ÔÇőrather than an economic and geopolitical rival.
Sanchez said China could do more in terms of fighting climate change, promoting global health, controlling the development of responsible artificial intelligence as well as nuclear ÔÇőweapons.
"For example, by demanding as it is doing, that international law be respected ÔÇőand that the conflicts in Lebanon, Iran, Gaza and the West Bank and Ukraine cease," he ÔÇîsaid.
"Europe Ôüáwill also have to redouble its efforts, especially now that the United States has decided to withdraw from many of these fronts."
Sanchez is expected to meet Chinese President Xi Jinping on Tuesday where they are expected to focus on geopolitics.
China accounted ÔÇőfor 74% of Spain's ÔÇőtotal trade deficit, ÔüáSanchez said, adding that cooperation was important to build a "balanced, globalised economy that generates shared prosperity."
Madrid hopes Sanchez's visit will ÔÇőnarrow Spain's trade deficit, which more than doubled in four ÔÇőyears to Ôüánearly $50 billion in 2025. It is looking to boost agricultural and manufacturing exports to offset high volumes of China's imports.
China's official news agency Xinhua on Monday said Sanchez's visit Ôüáwas ÔÇőset to further consolidate bilateral ties and pointed to ÔÇőa broader pathway for steady engagement between China and Europe at a time of growing global uncertainty.

item.title: US, Indonesia discussing agreement allowing US military overflight in Indonesian airspace, Indonesian ministry says
item.description:
JAKARTA, April 13 (Reuters) - Indonesia and ÔÇîthe United States are discussing ÔÇőan ÔÇőagreement to give U.S. Ôüámilitary aircraft ÔÇőaccess to ÔÇőIndonesian airspace, Indonesia's defence ministry said ÔÇőon Monday, ÔÇőadding that no deal ÔÇîhas Ôüáyet been reached.
U.S. Defence Secretary Pete ÔÇőHegseth ÔÇőis Ôüáset to meet ÔÇőwith his ÔÇőIndonesian Ôüácounterpart Sjafrie Sjamsoeddin later Ôüáon ÔÇőMonday.

item.title: South Korea and Poland to upgrade ties as Tusk calls Seoul key ally after US
item.description:
SEOUL, April 13 (Reuters) - South Korean President Lee Jae Myung and Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk agreed on Monday to upgrade ties to a comprehensive strategic partnership, with the leaders ÔÇőplacing defence cooperation at the centre of the relationship.
In remarks made before ÔÇőtalks between the leaders at the presidential Blue House, Lee said Ôüáthe countries would further expand defence industry cooperation under a $44.2 billion framework pact signed ÔÇőin 2022.
"K2 tanks, K9 selfÔÇĹpropelled howitzers, FAÔÇĹ50 light-attack aircraft, and Chunmoo multiple rocket ÔÇőlaunchers ÔÇö bearing South Korea's technology and pride ÔÇö are now safeguarding PolandÔÇÖs territory and its people across its vast lands," Lee said.
The South Korean leader said that the partnership extended beyond arms ÔÇősales to include joint production, technology transfers and training.
Tusk described South Korea ÔÇőas Poland's "most important ally after the United States, especially in the defence industry" and said he ÔÇîwould Ôüápersonally oversee expanded defence cooperation between the countries.
He said the upgraded partnership meant the two countries would take on a shared responsibility and should play a role in contributing to global peace and international stability.
The two leaders also affirmed ÔÇőexpanded cooperation across a ÔÇőwide range of Ôüáfields, including energy supply chains, infrastructure, science and technology, advanced industries, space and peopleÔÇĹtoÔÇĹpeople exchanges.
South Korea has become one ÔÇőof Poland's leading arms suppliers in recent years as Warsaw ÔÇőmoves to Ôüárapidly modernise its military following Russia's invasion of Ukraine.
In 2022, South Korea and Poland signed a defence framework agreement for South Korean companies to supply Poland with Ôüáarms as ÔÇőwell as jointly produce military equipment on Polish ÔÇősoil.
Since then, defence companies including Hanwha Aerospace (012450.KS) , opens new tab and Hyundai Rotem (064350.KS) , opens new tab have signed follow-up, multi-billion-dollar contracts to ÔÇősupply equipment such as tanks and missile launchers.

item.title: Australia appoints woman to lead its army for the first time
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 13 (Reuters) - Australia on Monday said a woman would lead its army for the first time in history, as part of a reshuffle of ÔÇőthe country's defence force leadership.
Lieutenant General Susan Coyle, the current chief ÔÇîof joint capabilities, will become chief of army in July, the government said in a statement. She will replace Lieutenant General Simon Stuart.
Coyle's appointment comes as Australia's military seeks to ÔÇőboost the number of female officers in its ranks. It ÔÇőfaces a wave of allegations of systematic sexual harassment and discrimination.
"From ÔüáJuly, we will have the first ever female chief of army ÔÇőin the Australian Army's 125-year history," Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said in a ÔÇőstatement.
Defence Minister Richard Marles called Coyle's appointment a "deeply historic moment."
"As Susan said to me, you cannot be what you cannot see," he said.
"Susan's achievement will be deeply significant ÔÇőto women who are serving in the Australian Defence Force today and ÔÇőwomen who are thinking about serving in the Australian Defence Force in the future."
Coyle, ÔÇî55, Ôüáenlisted in the military in 1987 and has held a number of senior command roles. She will be the first woman to lead any service branch of the military, Marles said.
Women currently make up around 21% of ÔÇőthe Australian Defence ÔÇőForce (ADF) and 18.5% Ôüáof senior leadership roles. The ADF has set a target of 25% of overall participation for women by 2030.
Last October, ÔÇőa class action lawsuit was filed against the ADF alleging ÔÇőit failed Ôüáto protect thousands of female officers from systematic sexual assault, harassment and discrimination.
The government on Monday also appointed Vice Admiral Mark Hammond, the current chief Ôüáof ÔÇőthe navy, as the head of the ADF, succeeding ÔÇőAdmiral David Johnston.
The current deputy chief of navy, Rear Admiral Matthew Buckley, will replace Hammond as ÔÇőhead of the branch.

item.title: Haiti declares three days of national mourning for 25 killed in stampede
item.description:
PORT-AU-PRINCE, April 12 (Reuters) - Haiti declared three ÔÇődays of national mourning ÔÇîon Sunday, a day after a deadly stampede killed 25 people ÔÇőduring an annual celebration thronged ÔÇőby students and visitors at Ôüáits Laferriere Citadel tourist ÔÇőattraction.
In a national address, Prime Minister ÔÇőAlix Didier Fils-Aim├ę said mourning would begin from Tuesday, and the government ÔÇőwould cover the funeral ÔÇőexpenses for disaster victims.
Earlier in the day, ÔÇîEmmanuel ÔüáPierre, the national head of the civil protection authority, told Reuters authorities had revised down ÔÇőthe ÔÇődeath toll Ôüáto 25 from an initial tally of ÔÇő30.
The early-19th-century fortress built ÔÇőshortly Ôüáafter Haiti's independence from France is a UNESCO World Heritage Ôüásite.

item.title: Slovakia's Fico ready to cooperate with Hungary's new prime minister
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Slovak Prime Minister Robert Fico congratulated Hungary's incoming leader, Peter Magyar, on Monday, offering "intensive cooperation", while expressing gratitude ÔÇőto outgoing Prime Minister Viktor Orban after a major election upset .
Fico, ÔÇîa key ally who had backed Orban before voting started, said Slovakia was ready for the close ties and joint action in protecting energy interests remained a goal.
Veteran nationalist ÔÇőOrban lost power in Sunday's election after 16 years in ÔÇőoffice, with Magyar and his upstart centre-right Tisza party winning Ôüáa comfortable majority of seats in parliament.
"With full respect, I take note ÔÇőof the decision of the citizens of Hungary ... and am ready for ÔÇőintensive cooperation with the new Hungarian prime minister, whom I congratulate on the election result," Fico said in a statement.
Under Fico, who returned to office in 2023, Slovakia ÔÇőhas been a close ally of Hungary, with both neighbours maintaining warm ÔÇőties with Moscow, opposing European Union sanctions and keeping up purchases of Russian oil ÔÇîand Ôüágas.
Both have sought to restore Russian oil flows through the Druzhba pipeline, which has been out of action since late January after damage in Ukraine that Kyiv said was caused by a Russian strike.
Fico said he ÔÇőbelieved Slovakia, Hungary and ÔÇőcentral Europe Ôüáwere still interested in resuming its operation, adding that joint action with Hungary in protecting energy interests remained unchanged ÔÇőas a government goal.
Last week, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy ÔÇősaid pipeline Ôüárepairs would be completed in spring. Fico and Orban have had tense relations with Zelenskiy despite Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
In a separate statement Ôüáon Facebook, ÔÇőFico said Slovak-Hungarian relations have never been ÔÇőat such a high level, and he praised Orban for exemplary cooperation and protection of ÔÇősovereignty and national interests.

item.title: Millions of people in Sudan surviving on one meal a day as food crisis deepens, NGOs say
item.description:
GENEVA, April 13 (Reuters) - Millions of people in Sudan are surviving on just one meal a day, as the country's food crisis deepens and threatens to spread, according to ÔÇőa report published on Monday by a group of non-governmental organisations.
Sudan's war between ÔÇîthe Sudanese army and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces, which enters its third year on Wednesday, has caused widespread hunger and displaced millions of people amid one of the world's largest humanitarian crises.
"In the two areas ÔÇőworst hit by the conflict - North Darfur and South Kordofan - millions of families can ÔÇőonly access one meal a day," the report by Action Against Hunger, ÔüáCARE International, International Rescue Committee, Mercy Corps, and the Norwegian Refugee Council found.
"Often, they miss ÔÇőmeals for entire days," the report stated, adding that many people have resorted to eating leaves ÔÇőand animal feed to survive.
The army-aligned Sudanese government denies the existence of famine, while the RSF denies responsibility for such conditions in areas under its control.
Some 61.7% of SudanÔÇÖs population ÔÇô 28.9 million people ÔÇô is acutely ÔÇőfood-insecure, according to the 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan.
The United Nations has reported widespread ÔÇőatrocities and waves of ethnically charged violence. In November, the global hunger monitor confirmed, for the first time, famine ÔÇîconditions Ôüáin al-Fashir, as well as Kadugli.
In February, the U.N.-backed Integrated Food Security Phase Classification found that famine thresholds for acute malnutrition have been surpassed in Um Baru, where the rate of acutely malnourished children under 5 was nearly double the famine threshold, and Kernoi.
The report, based on interviews ÔÇőwith farmers, traders, and ÔÇőhumanitarian actors in Sudan, Ôüádetails how the war in Sudan is driving communities towards famine conditions - due to disruptions to farming as well as the use of starvation ÔÇőas a weapon of war - including deliberate destruction of farms and ÔÇőmarkets.
Communal kitchens Ôüáare increasingly unable to meet rising needs, while major donor funding cuts are impeding aid agencies' abilities to respond, the report said.
Women and girls have been disproportionately affected, as they face a high risk Ôüáof rape ÔÇőand harassment when going to fields, visiting markets or ÔÇőcollecting water, the report said. Female-headed households are three times more likely to experience food insecurity than male-headed households, it ÔÇőadded.

item.title: Taiwan government should lead engagement with China on new measures, senior official says
item.description:
TAIPEI, April 13 (Reuters) - Taiwan's engagement with China on looser controls proposed by Beijing for trade ÔÇîand tourism should be led by the government rather than private party-to-party contacts, a senior Taiwanese security official said on Monday.
China on Sunday unveiled 10 new incentive measures for Taiwan, including easing tourist curbs, allowing in "healthy" television ÔÇődramas and facilitating food sales, following a visit by the island's opposition leader.
But China refuses ÔÇőto speak with Taiwan President Lai Ching-te's administration, saying he is a "separatist", Ôüáand has rebuffed multiple offers of talks.
Tsai Ming-yen, head of Taiwan's National Security Bureau, said only ÔÇőthe government should be assessing, planning and driving relevant exchanges.
"Having the government take charge of the ÔÇőrelevant planning can therefore also better help avoid unnecessary risks and long-term consequences," he told reporters at parliament.
China's Taiwan Affairs Office did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The Kuomintang (KMT), Taiwan's largest opposition party whose leader ÔÇőCheng Li-wun met Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing last week, has welcomed the measures, saying ÔÇőthey can help with peace and bring economic opportunities.
Hou Yu-ih, the KMT's 2024 presidential candidate and mayor of Taipei's ÔÇîsister Ôüácity New Taipei, told reporters on Monday that exchanges must be initiated on the basis of equality, dignity, and mutual respect, starting with less sensitive areas like culture.
"I believe that the leaders on both sides of the strait must take the responsibility of avoiding war. Peace is the ÔÇőultimate goal," he said. "Engagement ÔÇőis about communication and Ôüáminimising conflict as much as possible."
Tsai said such "goodwill measures" from China were historically brought up ahead of elections, concentrated on certain specific ÔÇőcounties and cities, or specific companies, industries, or individuals.
"However, this has already ÔÇőbecome a Ôüátool the Chinese communists use to interfere in Taiwan's elections," he added.
Taiwan will hold key local elections in November.
Ahead of the last presidential elections in early 2024, Taiwan complained that China had used Ôüáa series ÔÇőof trade measures to try to sway electors, including ÔÇőreviewing tariff concessions on agricultural and fishery products, machinery, auto parts and textiles.
Taiwan's government rejects Beijing's territorial claims, saying only the ÔÇőisland's people can decide their future.

item.title: Taiwan president to visit Eswatini, last diplomatic ally in Africa
item.description:
TAIPEI, April 13 (Reuters) - Taiwan President Lai Ching-te will visit Eswatini next week, his office said on Monday, the ÔÇőisland's last remaining diplomatic ally in Africa.
Taiwan, which ÔÇîChina claims as its own territory with no right to state-to-state relations, now has formal ties with only 12 ÔÇőcountries, almost all small, less-developed nations in Latin ÔÇőAmerica, the Caribbean and the Pacific, like Belize Ôüáand Tuvalu.
Lai will be in Eswatini from April ÔÇő22-26, his spokesperson Karen Kuo told reporters, for the ÔÇő40th anniversary of King Mswati III's accession and his 58th birthday.
Lai is flying directly to Eswatini, which is almost entirely surrounded ÔÇőby South Africa, and does not require a ÔÇőlayover, unlike visits to Latin America, which require transits via the ÔÇîUnited ÔüáStates that routinely anger China.
This will be Lai's first trip outside of Taiwan since November 2024, when he visited the Marshall Islands, Tuvalu, and Palau, and ÔÇőtransited through Hawaii ÔÇőand the ÔüáU.S. territory of Guam.
The last time a Taiwanese president visited Eswatini, formerly known ÔÇőas Swaziland and home to around 1.3 ÔÇőmillion people, Ôüáwas in 2023, when Tsai Ing-wen made the journey.
Taiwan has provided large amounts of aid to the small Ôüásouthern ÔÇőAfrican nation, an absolute monarchy. ÔÇőIn 2021, it sent antiviral medication to help King Mswati III recover ÔÇőfrom COVID.

item.title: TotalEnergies discovers hydrocarbons in Congo near existing production facilities
item.description:
PARIS, April 13 - French oil major ÔÇőTotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab has found ÔÇîhydrocarbons as part of a drilling campaign to ÔÇőexpand its currently ÔÇőoperational Moho field offshore ÔüáRepublic of Congo, ÔÇőit said on Monday.
The ÔÇőwell encountered a column of 160 metres of oil ÔÇőand gas. Total ÔÇősaid the Moho G discovery ÔÇîand Ôüáits previous Moho H find represent recoverable resources of close ÔÇőto ÔÇő100 Ôüámillion barrels.
Oil majors have expressed renewed ÔÇőinterest in West ÔÇőAfrica Ôüáin a bid to replenish resources from Ôüádeclining ÔÇőfields elsewhere.

item.title: Baker Hughes sells Waygate unit to Hexagon for about $1.45 billion
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Oilfield services provider Baker ÔÇőHughes (BKR.O) , opens new tab sold its Waygate Technologies ÔÇîunit to Swedish industrial technology group Hexagon (HEXAb.ST) , opens new tab for about $1.45 billion in ÔÇőcash, the two companies ÔÇősaid on Monday.
The deal is expected Ôüáto close in the ÔÇősecond half of 2026, the ÔÇőcompanies added.
Hexagon said it plans to finance the acquisition using cash and existing ÔÇődebt capacity.
Last year, Baker ÔÇőHughes agreed to buy Chart Industries (GTLS.N) , opens new tab in ÔÇîa $13.6 Ôüábillion all-cash deal to leverage its industrial and energy technology portfolio to drive growth and ÔÇőexpand its ÔÇőpresence Ôüáin the natural gas and LNG sectors.
Baker Hughes ÔÇőCEO Lorenzo Simonelli has ÔÇőbeen Ôüáreshaping the company to adapt to the global energy transition , shedding Ôüánon-core ÔÇőassets while investing ÔÇőin cleaner energy solutions.

item.title: Malaysia detains two tankers suspected of illegally transferring┬ádiesel
item.description:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 13 (Reuters) - Malaysia's Maritime Enforcement Agency (MMEA) said it has detained two ÔÇőtankers for allegedly conducting an illegal ship-to-ship transfer of ÔÇîabout 700,000 litres of diesel off the island of Penang over the weekend.
Malaysia has been cracking down on fuel smuggling amid mounting shortages and supply disruptions ÔÇőthroughout the region caused by the war in the ÔÇőMiddle East.
($1 = 3.9700 ringgit)
(This story has been corrected to change the number to 700,000, from 700,00, in paragraph 1)

item.title: China model gains appeal in Vietnam as police expand power
item.description:
HANOI, April 13 (Reuters) - Vietnam is increasingly edging closer to China's model of governance, tightening state control while embracing Chinese technology and regulation as its most powerful leader in decades heads to Beijing this week, ÔÇőaccording to internal documents, public policy plans and sources.
The two Communist neighbours have swung between conflict and cooperation over centuries. Now, Vietnam is leaning more openly toward Beijing, ÔÇîas China-friendly security figures rise in Hanoi under party chief To Lam, a former public security boss.
Lam will meet China's leader Xi Jinping on Wednesday on his first overseas trip since becoming state president on April 7, a move that formally unites two of Vietnam's most powerful roles, echoing Xi's own concentration of authority and breaking from Vietnam's traditional emphasis on collective leadership.
"Vietnam-China relations have entered a new stage, marked by higher political trust, more substantive defence and security cooperation, deeper and ÔÇőmore practical cooperation across sectors," Lam said in a joint statement with Xi after they last met in April 2025.
This week's visit is expected to yield dozens of cooperation agreements, people ÔÇőbriefed on the plans said. While such documents are often non-binding, the relationship is becoming more tangible: China's exports to Vietnam are at record highs , and Chinese Ôüáinvestment in manufacturing south of the border is booming.
The people spoke on condition of anonymity due to the sensitive nature of the topic.
Vietnam is still hedging geopolitically, analysts say, keeping doors open to Washington ÔÇőand others. But at home, it is moving closer to China's governance model - particularly control-driven regulation despite Western misgivings, underscoring how China's influence is deepening as Lam reshapes the state.
Vietnam has "a dual approach of actively ÔÇőlearning from the Chinese model while selectively resisting its influence," said Nguyen Khac Giang, a visiting fellow at Singapore's ISEAS Yusof Ishak Institute.
Alexander Vuving of the Asia-Pacific Center for Security Studies in the U.S., said that closer ties with China without adequate guardrails "will have a negative impact not only on Vietnam's security, prosperity, and autonomy, but also on its relations with the U.S. and the West."
Vietnam's foreign ministry did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Technology has emerged ÔÇőas one of the clearest markers of warming ties.
